  1135Ôćĺ
  1136Ôćĺ\noindent
  1137ÔćĺThree cross-checks tie the census to the Diophantine system:
  1138Ôćĺ\begin{itemize}
  1139Ôćĺ\item \textbf{Neutral composites:} The 12 states at $Q_{\text{em}} = 0$
  1140Ôćĺmatch $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 9 + 4 - 1 = 12 = 4N$.
  1141Ôćĺ\item \textbf{Charge $+1/3$ composites} (one up $\times$ one
  1142Ôćĺdown): $rs = 3 \times 2 = 6$ composites, matching equation
  1143Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:rs} with $rs = 6 = 2N$.  These states distribute as
  1144Ôćĺ3 from $(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})_{5/6} \subset \mathbf{24}$
  1145Ôćĺand 3 from $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  1146Ôćĺ\item \textbf{Charge $-2/3$ composites} (symmetric down--down):
  1147Ôćĺ$r(r+1)/2 = 6$ symmetric pairs fill the color sextet
  1148Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})_{-2/3} \subset \mathbf{15}$, matching
  1149Ôćĺequation~\eqref{eq:rr} with $r(r+1)/2 = 6 = 2N$.
  1150Ôćĺ\end{itemize}
  1151Ôćĺ
  1152Ôćĺ\subsection{Anomaly cancellation and asymptotic freedom}
  1153ÔćĺThe representation $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus
  1154Ôćĺ\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ must be anomaly-free if it is to appear
  1155Ôćĺin a consistent gauge theory.
  1156Ôćĺ
  1157Ôćĺ\medskip
  1158Ôćĺ\noindent\textbf{Proposition 2} (Anomaly cancellation).
  1159Ôćĺ\textit{The cubic $SU(5)$ anomaly of $\mathbf{24} \oplus
  1160Ôćĺ\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ vanishes.}
  1161Ôćĺ
  1162Ôćĺ\medskip
  1163Ôćĺ\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1164ÔćĺThe anomaly coefficient $A(\mathbf{R})$ for $SU(n)$ satisfies
  1165Ôćĺ$A(\text{adjoint}) = 0$ (the adjoint is real),
  1166Ôćĺ$A(\mathbf{15}) = n + 4 = 9$, and
  1167Ôćĺ$A(\overline{\mathbf{15}}) = -(n+4) = -9$.  Therefore
  1168Ôćĺ\[
  1169ÔćĺA(\mathbf{24}) + A(\mathbf{15}) + A(\overline{\mathbf{15}})
  1170Ôćĺ= 0 + 9 + (-9) = 0\,.
  1171Ôćĺ\]
  1172Ôćĺ\hfill$\square$
  1173Ôćĺ
  1174Ôćĺ\medskip
  1175Ôćĺ\noindent\textbf{Proposition 3} (Asymptotic freedom).
  1176Ôćĺ\textit{An $SU(5)$ gauge theory with matter in the
  1177Ôćĺ$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1178Ôćĺis asymptotically free.}
  1179Ôćĺ
  1180Ôćĺ\medskip
  1181Ôćĺ\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1182ÔćĺThe one-loop beta-function coefficient is
  1183Ôćĺ\[
  1184Ôćĺb_0 = \frac{11}{3}\,C_2(G) - \frac{2}{3}\,\sum_{\text{Weyl}} T(\mathbf{R})\,,
  1185Ôćĺ\]
  1186Ôćĺwhere $C_2(G) = n = 5$ for $SU(5)$, $T(\mathbf{adj}) = n = 5$,
  1187Ôćĺand $T(\mathbf{15}) = (n+2)/2 = 7/2$.  The Weyl fermion content
  1188Ôćĺconsists of one adjoint and one $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$
  1189Ôćĺ(treating each as a Weyl fermion), giving
  1190Ôćĺ$\sum T = 5 + 7/2 + 7/2 = 12$.  Therefore
  1191Ôćĺ\[
  1192Ôćĺb_0 = \frac{11}{3} \times 5 - \frac{2}{3} \times 12
  1193Ôćĺ= \frac{55 - 24}{3} = \frac{31}{3} > 0\,.
  1194Ôćĺ\]
  1195Ôćĺ\hfill$\square$
  1196Ôćĺ
  1197Ôćĺ\medskip
  1198ÔćĺBoth properties are nontrivial: anomaly cancellation ensures
  1199Ôćĺthe gauge symmetry is quantum-mechanically consistent, and
  1200Ôćĺ$b_0 > 0$ ensures the theory is well-defined in the ultraviolet.
  1201ÔćĺTogether they confirm that the representation forced by the
  1202ÔćĺsBootstrap counting is physically viable.
  1203Ôćĺ
  1204Ôćĺ\subsection{Summary of the counting argument}
  1205ÔćĺThe logical chain is:
  1206Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  1207Ôćĺ\item The sBootstrap postulate (composites $=$ scalars of the SSM)
  1208Ôćĺyields three Diophantine equations in $(N, r, s)$.
  1209Ôćĺ\item The unique positive-integer solution is $(3, 3, 2)$:
  1210Ôćĺthree generations, with the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ forced over
  1211Ôćĺthe antisymmetric $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.
  1212Ôćĺ\item The solution organizes into $SU(5)$ representations
  1213Ôćĺ$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$,
  1214Ôćĺwhich are anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
  1215Ôćĺ\item Right-handed neutrinos are required: without them, no
  1216Ôćĺinteger solution exists.
  1217Ôćĺ\item The top quark is excluded from the composite sector by
  1218Ôćĺthe counting itself ($s = 2 < N = 3$), matching its
  1219Ôćĺkinematic decoupling ($m_t \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$).
  1220Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  1221Ôćĺ
  1222Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1223Ôćĺ\section{The Diquark Sector and SU(5) Flavor}
  1224Ôćĺ\label{sec:diquarks}
  1225Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1226Ôćĺ
  1227Ôćĺ\subsection{Counting partners}
  1228Ôćĺ
  1229ÔćĺIn the full sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005}, the scalar partners of

1223Ôćĺ\section{The Diquark Sector and SU(5) Flavor}
  1224Ôćĺ\label{sec:diquarks}
  1225Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1226Ôćĺ
  1227Ôćĺ\subsection{Counting partners}
  1228Ôćĺ
  1229ÔćĺIn the full sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005}, the scalar partners of 
  1230Ôćĺthe Standard Model fermions include both mesons ($q\bar{q}$) and 
  1231Ôćĺdiquarks ($qq$). For five active flavors, the meson matrix gives 
  1232Ôćĺ$5 \times 5 = 25$ scalars. The diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ form a 
  1233Ôćĺrepresentation of $SU(5)_f$:
  1234Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1235Ôćĺ\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{5} = \overline{\mathbf{10}}_A 
  1236Ôćĺ\oplus \mathbf{15}_S\,.
  1237Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1238Ôćĺ
  1239ÔćĺThe symmetric $\mathbf{15}_S$ gives 15 diquarks including 
  1240Ôćĺsame-flavor states $[uu]$, $[dd]$, \ldots, while the antisymmetric 
  1241Ôćĺ$\overline{\mathbf{10}}_A$ gives 10 diquarks with $i \neq j$ only.
  1242Ôćĺ
  1243Ôćĺ\subsection{The symmetric representation}
  1244Ôćĺ\label{subsec:sym15}
  1245Ôćĺ
  1246ÔćĺThe sBootstrap SUSY generator acts asymmetrically on the two
  1247Ôćĺfermion sectors. When it acts on a \emph{lepton}, it produces a
  1248Ôćĺmeson: $\mathcal{Q} \cdot \ell \sim q\bar{q}$. Since the quark and antiquark 
  1249Ôćĺare distinguishable, the meson lies in the full 
  1250Ôćĺ$\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}} = \mathbf{24} + \mathbf{1}$. 
  1251ÔćĺThere is no Pauli constraint, and the meson spectrum is perfectly 
  1252Ôćĺreproduced.
  1253Ôćĺ
  1254ÔćĺWhen it acts on a \emph{quark}, it produces a diquark:
  1255Ôćĺ$\mathcal{Q} \cdot q_i \sim q_i q_j$. The bosonic SUSY generator applied to 
  1256Ôćĺa single flavor index $i$ naturally produces the \textbf{symmetric} 
  1257Ôćĺproduct---the $\mathbf{15}_S$ representation, not the antisymmetric 
  1258Ôćĺ$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. This is the representation identified 
  1259Ôćĺin the $SO(32)$ decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}: the adjoint of 
  1260Ôćĺ$SO(32)$ under $SU(5)_f$ decomposes to include the 
  1261Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ (diquarks) alongside the 
  1262Ôćĺ$\mathbf{1} + \mathbf{24}$ (mesons).
  1263Ôćĺ
  1264ÔćĺThe 15 includes 5 same-flavor states ($[uu]$, $[dd]$, $[ss]$, 
  1265Ôćĺ$[cc]$, $[bb]$) absent from the $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. Among 
  1266Ôćĺthese are exotic $+4/3$-charged diquarks such as $[uu]$. The cost: 
  1267Ôćĺan additional chiral $+4/3$ quark per generation, as identified 
  1268Ôćĺin \cite{Rivero2005}. Whether these exotic states appear in nature 
  1269Ôćĺas resonances, or whether they decouple at some high scale, is an 
  1270Ôćĺopen question.
  1271Ôćĺ
  1272Ôćĺ\subsection{The Pauli theorem for scalar diquarks}
  1273Ôćĺ\label{subsec:pauli}
  1274Ôćĺ
  1275ÔćĺThe requirement of the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ conflicts with 
  1276ÔćĺFermi statistics---not merely for $L=0$ diquarks, but for 
  1277Ôćĺ\emph{all} scalar diquarks in the color $\bar{3}$ channel, 
  1278Ôćĺregardless of internal orbital structure.
  1279Ôćĺ
  1280Ôćĺ\medskip
  1281Ôćĺ\noindent\textbf{Theorem.}
  1282Ôćĺ\textit{For a spin-0 ($J=0$) diquark in the color $\bar{3}$ 
  1283Ôćĺ(antisymmetric) representation, the flavor wave function is 
  1284Ôćĺantisymmetric for \textbf{all} values of the orbital angular 
  1285Ôćĺmomentum $L$ and spin $S$.}
  1286Ôćĺ
  1287Ôćĺ\medskip
  1288Ôćĺ\noindent\textit{Proof.}
  1289Ôćĺ$J = 0$ requires $L = S$ (since $|L - S| \leq J \leq L + S$). 
  1290ÔćĺThe exchange symmetry of the spin-orbital wave function is 
  1291Ôćĺ$(-1)^{L+S+1} = (-1)^{2L+1} = -1$ for all $L$. Combined with 
  1292Ôćĺthe color $\bar{3}$ factor $(-1)$, the non-flavor product is 
  1293Ôćĺ$(-1)(-1) = +1$ (symmetric). Total antisymmetry then requires 
  1294Ôćĺflavor to be antisymmetric: $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. \hfill$\square$
  1295Ôćĺ
  1296Ôćĺ\medskip
  1297ÔćĺThis result is purely kinematic---independent of dynamics, 
  1298Ôćĺbinding mechanisms, or spatial extent. No wave function, however 
  1299Ôćĺextended, can produce a symmetric-flavor scalar diquark in color 
  1300Ôćĺ$\bar{3}$ while respecting Fermi statistics. The key cases:
  1301Ôćĺ
  1302Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  1303Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
  1304Ôćĺ\toprule
  1305Ôćĺ$L$ & $S$ & Spin-orbital $(-1)^{2L+1}$ & $\times$ Color & Flavor \\
  1306Ôćĺ\midrule
  1307Ôćĺ0 & 0 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1308Ôćĺ1 & 1 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1309Ôćĺ2 & 2 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
  1310Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  1311Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  1312Ôćĺ\end{center}
  1313Ôćĺ
  1314ÔćĺThe $L=1$, $S=1$ row closes the ``P-wave diquark'' escape route 
  1315Ôćĺthat might otherwise seem available.
  1316Ôćĺ
  1317ÔćĺThe theorem has a sharp consequence: \textbf{the sBootstrap 
  1318Ôćĺdiquark in the $\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite.} It 
  1319Ôćĺmust be either:
  1320Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  1321Ôćĺ\item An algebraically generated object---the image of the 
  1322ÔćĺMiyazawa supercharge \cite{Miyazawa, CattoGursey} acting on 
  1323Ôćĺan antiquark, whose symmetry is determined by the superalgebra 
  1324Ôćĺrather than by the statistics of constituent quarks; or
  1325Ôćĺ\item A fundamental string-theoretic object, as in the $SO(32)$ 
  1326Ôćĺdecomposition \cite{Rivero2005}, where the $\mathbf{15}$ 
  1327Ôćĺarises from the adjoint representation of the gauge group and 
  1328Ôćĺthe open-string diquark is not built from point-like quarks.
  1329Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  1330Ôćĺ
  1331ÔćĺThe contrast with the meson sector is instructive. When the 
  1332ÔćĺSUSY generator acts on a lepton, it produces a meson 
  1333Ôćĺ($q\bar{q}$): the quark and antiquark are distinguishable, 
  1334Ôćĺno Pauli constraint applies, and the meson spectrum is 
  1335Ôćĺreproduced without tension. When it acts on a quark, it 
  1336Ôćĺproduces a diquark in the ``wrong'' representation---and the 
  1337Ôćĺtheorem shows this cannot be fixed by any internal structure 
  1338Ôćĺof a $qq$ pair. The asymmetry between the two SUSY directions 
  1339Ôćĺis absolute: mesons work as composites; diquarks do not. 
  1340ÔćĺThis is an unresolved obstruction: the sBootstrap requires
  1341Ôćĺthe symmetric $\mathbf{15}$, but QCD composite diquarks cannot
  1342Ôćĺfill it. The proposed resolutions---Miyazawa supercharges and
  1343ÔćĺSO(32) string embeddings---are directions for future work, not
  1344Ôćĺestablished mechanisms. The diquark sector remains the weakest
  1345Ôćĺpoint of the program.
  1346Ôćĺ
  1347Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1348Ôćĺ\section{Two Algebraic Structures}
  1349Ôćĺ\label{sec:two_structures}
  1350Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1351Ôćĺ
  1352ÔćĺThe sBootstrap particle content and mass spectrum are constrained by

1690Ôćĺ\section{SUSY Breaking: From Seeds to Spectrum}
  1691Ôćĺ\label{sec:breaking}
  1692Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1693Ôćĺ
  1694ÔćĺThe SUSY-breaking story begins with the foundational observation 
  1695Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:pimu}: $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2$. This is the 
  1696ÔćĺVolkov--Akulov mass formula for a pseudo-Goldstino---the muon---whose 
  1697Ôćĺsuperpartner---the pion---is a pseudo-Goldstone boson. The SUSY-breaking 
  1698Ôćĺscale is $f_\pi \approx 92\MeV$, and the breaking mechanism is chiral: 
  1699Ôćĺ$\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1700Ôćĺsimultaneously. The Volkov--Akulov realization is the \emph{physical 
  1701Ôćĺstarting point}; what follows in this section is the SQCD machinery 
  1702Ôćĺthat makes it precise.
  1703Ôćĺ
  1704ÔćĺThe dynamics must accomplish three things: generate the seed-to-bloom 
  1705Ôćĺtransition for each tuple, preserve the energy-balance condition 
  1706Ôćĺ$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ through the breaking, and produce 
  1707Ôćĺthe correct splitting between meson and lepton masses at scale $f_\pi$.
  1708Ôćĺ
  1709Ôćĺ\subsection{The Seiberg framework}
  1710Ôćĺ
  1711ÔćĺThe natural arena is $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with gauge group $SU(3)_c$ 
  1712Ôćĺand $N_f$ flavors of chiral superfields $Q^i$, $\bar{Q}_j$ in the 
  1713Ôćĺfundamental and antifundamental representations. For $N_f = N_c = 3$ 
  1714Ôćĺ(the case relevant to light QCD), Seiberg's exact results 
  1715Ôćĺ\cite{Seiberg1995} establish that the theory confines, with the 
  1716Ôćĺlow-energy degrees of freedom being the composite meson superfield
  1717Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1718ÔćĺM^i{}_j = Q^i \cdot \bar{Q}_j\,,
  1719Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1720Ôćĺa $3\times 3$ matrix of chiral superfields, together with baryons 
  1721Ôćĺ$B = \epsilon_{ijk}Q^iQ^jQ^k$ and $\bar{B}$. These satisfy the 
  1722Ôćĺquantum-modified constraint
  1723Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1724Ôćĺ\label{eq:seiberg}
  1725Ôćĺ\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^{6}\,,
  1726Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1727Ôćĺwhere $\Lambda$ is the dynamical scale ($2N_c = 6$ for $N_c = 3$).
  1728Ôćĺ
  1729ÔćĺEach entry $M^i{}_j$ is a chiral superfield containing a complex 
  1730Ôćĺscalar (the meson) and a Weyl fermion (the lepton, in the sBootstrap 
  1731Ôćĺidentification). The scalar and fermion are degenerate in the SUSY 
  1732Ôćĺlimit; SUSY breaking splits them.
  1733Ôćĺ
  1734Ôćĺ\subsection{The three chiral superfields of each seed}
  1735Ôćĺ
  1736ÔćĺThe seed triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ maps naturally onto three 
  1737Ôćĺchiral superfields with the following mass structure before breaking:
  1738Ôćĺ
  1739Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  1740Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{lcl}
  1741Ôćĺ\toprule
  1742ÔćĺSuperfield & Mass & Role \\
  1743Ôćĺ\midrule
  1744Ôćĺ$\Phi_0$ & $m_0 = 0$ & Massless: flat direction \\
  1745Ôćĺ$\Phi_a$ & $m_a = \zch_a^2$ & Light massive member \\
  1746Ôćĺ$\Phi_b$ & $m_b = \zch_b^2$ & Heavy massive member \\
  1747Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  1748Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  1749Ôćĺ\end{center}
  1750Ôćĺ
  1751ÔćĺThe ratio $\zch_a/\zch_b = 2 - \sqrt{3}$ is enforced by the 
  1752ÔćĺKoide energy-balance condition applied to the seed.
  1753Ôćĺ
  1754ÔćĺIn the Seiberg description, these three superfields are entries of 
  1755Ôćĺthe meson matrix $M^i{}_j$. For the $(0, s, c)$ seed, the relevant 
  1756Ôćĺentries involve the strange and charm quarks; $\Phi_0$ is the entry 
  1757Ôćĺwhose quark-mass combination vanishes in the seed limit (i.e., the 
  1758Ôćĺdirection in flavor space where $m_i + m_j \to 0$ through GOR).
  1759Ôćĺ
  1760Ôćĺ\subsection{F-term breaking: the O'Raifeartaigh mechanism}
  1761Ôćĺ\label{subsec:fterm}
  1762Ôćĺ
  1763ÔćĺThe VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} tells us \emph{that} SUSY is broken at 
  1764Ôćĺscale $f_\pi$. To understand \emph{how}, we need the microscopic 
  1765Ôćĺmechanism. The O'Raifeartaigh model \cite{ORaifeartaigh} is the simplest 
  1766Ôćĺrealization of spontaneous $F$-term SUSY breaking. With three chiral 
  1767Ôćĺsuperfields, the superpotential
  1768Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1769ÔćĺW = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_1^2
  1770Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1771Ôćĺgives $F$-terms:
  1772Ôćĺ\begin{align}
  1773ÔćĺF_{\Phi_0}^* &= f + g\,\phi_1^2\,, \\
  1774ÔćĺF_{\Phi_1}^* &= m\,\phi_2 + 2g\,\phi_0\phi_1\,, \\
  1775ÔćĺF_{\Phi_2}^* &= m\,\phi_1\,.
  1776Ôćĺ\end{align}
  1777ÔćĺIf $f \neq 0$ and $m \neq 0$, then $F_{\Phi_2}^* = 0$ requires 
  1778Ôćĺ$\phi_1 = 0$, but then $F_{\Phi_0}^* = f \neq 0$: SUSY is 
  1779Ôćĺ\textbf{necessarily broken}. The key features are:
  1780Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  1781Ôćĺ\item A massless field ($\Phi_0$) exists at tree level---it is the 
  1782Ôćĺpseudo-modulus.
  1783Ôćĺ\item The breaking is triggered because $F_{\Phi_0}$ cannot vanish.
  1784Ôćĺ\item After including loop corrections, $\Phi_0$ acquires a mass 
  1785Ôćĺ(the Coleman--Weinberg potential lifts the flat direction).
  1786Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  1787Ôćĺ
  1788ÔćĺThe structural parallel with the seed triples is:
  1789Ôćĺ
  1790Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  1791Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{llll}
  1792Ôćĺ\toprule
  1793ÔćĺO'Raifeartaigh & Flavor & Seed & After breaking \\
  1794Ôćĺ\midrule
  1795Ôćĺ$\Phi_0$ (massless) & $b$ / $B$ & $\zch = 0$ (flat direction) & $\zch_b = 64.65$ (mass generated) \\
  1796Ôćĺ$\Phi_1$ (light) & $s$ / $\pi$ & $\zch_s = 9.67$ & $\zch \to -9.67$ (sign-flipped) \\
  1797Ôćĺ$\Phi_2$ (heavy) & $c$ / $D_s$ & $\zch_c = 35.64$ & $\zch_c \approx 35.64$ (shifted) \\
  1798Ôćĺ\midrule
  1799Ôćĺ & & $f = 0$ (SUSY) & $f = f_\pi$ (broken) \\
  1800Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  1801Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  1802Ôćĺ\end{center}
  1803Ôćĺ
  1804ÔćĺThis parallel is more than structural: the minimal O'Raifeartaigh
  1805Ôćĺmodel gives $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ a single mass parameter $m$, but the
  1806Ôćĺ\emph{fermionic} mass matrix at the vacuum ($\phi_1 = 0$,
  1807Ôćĺ$\phi_0 = v$) is
  1808Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  1809Ôćĺ  W_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2gv & m \\ 0 & m & 0 \end{pmatrix},
  1810Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  1811Ôćĺwith eigenvalues $0$ (the goldstino) and $m_\pm = \sqrt{g^2v^2 + m^2} \pm gv$.
  1812ÔćĺThe mass ratio $m_-/m_+ = (\sqrt{t^2+1} - t)^2$, where $t = gv/m$,
  1813Ôćĺis \emph{not} unity.  Setting $\sqrt{m_-}/\sqrt{m_+} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$
  1814Ôćĺ(the Koide seed ratio) requires $\sqrt{t^2+1} - t = 2 - \sqrt{3}$,
  1815Ôćĺwhich gives
  1816Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  1817Ôćĺ  t = \frac{gv}{m} = \sqrt{3}\,,
  1818Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  1819Ôćĺthe unique value at which $t^2 + 1 = 4$, so $m_\pm = (2 \pm \sqrt{3})\,m$.
  1820ÔćĺThe Koide ratio for the triple $(0, m_-, m_+) = (0,\, (2-\sqrt{3})\,m,
  1821Ôćĺ\, (2+\sqrt{3})\,m)$ evaluates to $Q = 4m/(\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}} +
  1822Ôćĺ\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}})^2 = 4/6 = 2/3$ exactly, since
  1823Ôćĺ$\sqrt{2-\sqrt{3}}\cdot\sqrt{2+\sqrt{3}} = 1$ and the squared sum
  1824Ôćĺtelescopes to $6$.
  1825Ôćĺ
  1826ÔćĺThe Koide condition on the seed is therefore \emph{one algebraic
  1827Ôćĺconstraint on the superpotential parameters}: $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$.
  1828ÔćĺThe coupling $g$, the pseudo-modulus VEV $v$, and the mass
  1829Ôćĺparameter $m$ are individually undetermined, but their ratio is fixed.
  1830ÔćĺThis is the microscopic content of the energy-balance condition
  1831Ôćĺ$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ applied to the seed.
  1832Ôćĺ
  1833ÔćĺThe $b$ quark and $B$ meson are
  1834Ôćĺalways present as flavor degrees of freedom; the seed configuration
  1835Ôćĺ$(0, s, c)$ means the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction before
  1836Ôćĺbreaking. The bloom is not particle creation but mass generation:
  1837Ôćĺbreaking lifts $\Phi_0$ from zero to $\zch_b \neq 0$, simultaneously
  1838Ôćĺflipping the sign of $\zch_s$, so that the same three flavors
  1839Ôćĺrearrange from $(0, s, c)$ into $(-s, c, b)$.
  1840Ôćĺ
  1841Ôćĺ\paragraph{The bloom as a phase rotation.}
  1842ÔćĺIn the Koide parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, the seed sits at
  1843Ôćĺ$\delta = 3\pi/4$, where $\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2}$ forces one
  1844Ôćĺmass to vanish: $\sqrt{m_0} = \sqrt{M_0}(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos\delta) = 0$.
  1845ÔćĺA Koide-preserving bloom ($Q = 2/3$ maintained) is a pure rotation
  1846Ôćĺ$\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at fixed scale $v_0$
  1847Ôćĺand fixed amplitude $r = \sqrt{2}\,v_0$. The total mass
  1848Ôćĺ$M = 6v_0^2$ is conserved---mass merely redistributes among the three
  1849Ôćĺeigenvalues. The sign flip is instantaneous: the seed sits exactly at
  1850Ôćĺthe boundary $\zch_0 = 0$, and any infinitesimal rotation generates
  1851Ôćĺa nonzero charge with definite sign.
  1852Ôćĺ
  1853ÔćĺExplicit R-symmetry breaking in the O'Raifeartaigh model (adding a
  1854Ôćĺterm $\delta W \sim \varepsilon\,\Phi_0\Phi_2$) lifts the goldstino
  1855Ôćĺmass as $m_0 \sim \varepsilon^2/m$, but simultaneously pushes
  1856Ôćĺ$Q$ away from $2/3$---no nonzero $\varepsilon$ preserves the Koide
  1857Ôćĺcondition. The Koide seed is an \emph{unstable fixed point} of the
  1858ÔćĺR-breaking perturbation. In the dynamical (SQCD) realization, the
  1859Ôćĺbloom must therefore be a nonperturbative rearrangement---not a
  1860Ôćĺsmooth deformation of the O'Raifeartaigh spectrum.
  1861Ôćĺ
  1862ÔćĺA striking difference from standard O'Raifeartaigh: the 
  1863Ôćĺpseudo-modulus $\Phi_0$ normally acquires the \emph{smallest} mass 
  1864Ôćĺin the spectrum (a loop-suppressed Coleman--Weinberg mass, 
  1865Ôćĺparametrically $\sim g^2 f/16\pi^2 m$). In the sBootstrap bloom, 
  1866Ôćĺthe $b$-direction acquires the \emph{largest} mass 
  1867Ôćĺ($m_b \gg m_s$ and $m_b > m_c$). The bloom is far more dramatic than a 
  1868Ôćĺperturbative CW lifting---it is a nonperturbative, strong-dynamics 
  1869Ôćĺeffect in which confinement generates a mass of order $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/m_q$, 
  1870Ôćĺnot $g^2/(16\pi^2)$. Numerically, the one-loop CW mass for the
  1871Ôćĺpseudo-modulus at the Seiberg vacuum is $m_{\text{CW}} \approx 13\MeV$,
  1872Ôćĺmore than two orders of magnitude below the physical $m_b = 4{,}180\MeV$.
  1873ÔćĺThis inversion is a signature of the dynamical (SQCD) realization
  1874Ôćĺversus the perturbative (superpotential) one.
  1875Ôćĺ
  1876Ôćĺ\paragraph{A candidate mechanism: fractional instantons.}
  1877ÔćĺOn $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, SU($N_c$) instantons fractionalize into
  1878Ôćĺ$N_c$ monopole-instantons \cite{Unsal2008}. Each monopole-instanton
  1879Ôćĺcarries a single fermionic zero mode per flavor; when the zero mode is
  1880Ôćĺlifted by the quark mass $m_i$, the amplitude acquires a factor
  1881Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_i}$ (not $m_i$). Since the $N_c = 3$ monopole contributions
  1882Ôćĺenter \emph{additively} in the effective superpotential,
  1883Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  1884Ôćĺ  W_{\text{mon}} \sim \zeta\,\bigl(\sqrt{m_1} + \sqrt{m_2} + \sqrt{m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1885Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  1886Ôćĺwhere $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/3}$ is the monopole fugacity. This is
  1887Ôćĺ\emph{exactly} the sum-of-square-roots structure of the vacuum charge
  1888Ôćĺ$v_0 = (\sum \sqrt{m_k})/3$.
  1889ÔćĺThe $v_0$-doubling then maps to the counting of active
  1890Ôćĺmonopole-instanton contributions: the seed (two active flavors) involves
  1891Ôćĺtwo monopole amplitudes, while the bloom (three active flavors, one with
  1892Ôćĺa sign flip) involves three, doubling $v_0$.
  1893Ôćĺ
  1894ÔćĺThis mechanism is non-holomorphic in the sense required: the
  1895ÔćĺK\"ahler potential receives monopole--anti-monopole (bion) cross terms
  1896Ôćĺof the form $|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$, where $m_k$ are the
  1897Ôćĺcurrent quark masses. Expanding for $N_c = 3$:
  1898Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  1899Ôćĺ  K_{\text{bion}} = \frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\bigl(
  1900Ôćĺ  m_1 + m_2 + m_3
  1901Ôćĺ  + 2s_1 s_2\sqrt{m_1 m_2}
  1902Ôćĺ  + 2s_1 s_3\sqrt{m_1 m_3}
  1903Ôćĺ  + 2s_2 s_3\sqrt{m_2 m_3}\bigr)\,,
  1904Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  1905Ôćĺwhere the diagonal terms $s_k^2 m_k = m_k$ are self-bions and the
  1906Ôćĺcross terms $\sqrt{m_i m_j}$ are mixed bion amplitudes---their
  1907Ôćĺgeometric mean structure is what produces $\sum\sqrt{m_k}$.
  1908ÔćĺNote that $m_k$ here are the quark masses (superpotential couplings),
  1909Ôćĺ\emph{not} the meson VEVs $M^k{}_k \propto 1/m_k$ of the Seiberg
  1910Ôćĺseesaw; the bion amplitude involves quark zero modes, not meson fields.
  1911ÔćĺOrganizing the bion amplitudes into
  1912Ôćĺseed-sector ($S_{\text{seed}} = \sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$) and
  1913Ôćĺbloom-sector ($S_{\text{bloom}} = -\!\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b}$)
  1914Ôćĺchannels, the K\"ahler correction takes the form
  1915Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  1916Ôćĺ  \delta K \sim \lambda_1\,|S_{\text{seed}}|^2
  1917Ôćĺ  + \lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2\,.
  1918Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  1919ÔćĺThe second term is a perfect square that vanishes
  1920Ôćĺ\emph{at the minimum} when $S_{\text{bloom}} = 2\,S_{\text{seed}}$,
  1921Ôćĺi.e., $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$---the
  1922Ôćĺ$v_0$-doubling condition. The relation between bion couplings
  1923Ôćĺ($\lambda_3 = -4\lambda_2$ for cross-channel bions) is what
  1924Ôćĺenforces the doubling. Numerically,
  1925Ôćĺ$S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}} = 0.023\MeV^{1/2}$
  1926Ôćĺ(effectively zero: $m_b = 4{,}177$ vs.\ PDG $4{,}180$).
  1927ÔćĺThe Hessian of $\lambda_2\,|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2\,S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  1928Ôćĺhas rank~1: its single nonzero eigenvector is
  1929Ôćĺ$\partial f/\partial m_i$ (the gradient of
  1930Ôćĺ$f = \sqrt{m_b} - 3\sqrt{m_s} - \sqrt{m_c}$), while the two flat
  1931Ôćĺdirections correspond to independent variation of $m_s$ and $m_c$
  1932Ôćĺat fixed $f$.  Thus the bion K\"ahler constrains exactly \emph{one}
  1933Ôćĺmass combination; the remaining two masses are fixed by the Koide
  1934Ôćĺseed in the superpotential. Including propagated uncertainties
  1935Ôćĺ($\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$, $\delta m_c = 20\MeV$), the prediction
  1936Ôćĺis $m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$, consistent with the PDG value
  1937Ôćĺ$4{,}180 \pm 30\MeV$ at $0.06\sigma$.
  1938Ôćĺ
  1939ÔćĺThe principal caveat is that this analysis
  1940Ôćĺapplies on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$; connecting to $\mathbb{R}^4$
  1941Ôćĺphysics requires \"Unsal's continuity conjecture~\cite{Unsal2012},
  1942Ôćĺwhich posits that center-symmetric confinement at small $S^1$ is
  1943Ôćĺcontinuously connected to the large-$S^1$ confining vacuum.
  1944ÔćĺThe conjecture is supported by lattice evidence for $SU(N)$ pure
  1945ÔćĺYang--Mills and by the absence of phase transitions in the
  1946Ôćĺcenter-stabilized theory, but remains unproven for theories with
  1947Ôćĺmatter.
  1948Ôćĺ
  1949ÔćĺThe identification $\langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle = f \sim \fpi^2$ is the
  1950ÔćĺsBootstrap's central claim: the SUSY-breaking $F$-term is the chiral
  1951Ôćĺcondensate parameter.
  1952Ôćĺ
  1953ÔćĺIn SQCD, this is realized dynamically rather than through a 
  1954Ôćĺsuperpotential: the chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ acts as the 
  1955Ôćĺnonzero $F$-term, the impossibility of restoring chiral symmetry 
  1956Ôćĺin the QCD vacuum is the dynamical analogue of $f \neq 0$, and the 
  1957Ôćĺpion---the would-be Goldstone boson that acquires mass from explicit 
  1958Ôćĺchiral breaking---plays the role of $\Phi_0$ whose mass is lifted 
  1959Ôćĺby loop corrections (here, by quark masses).
  1960Ôćĺ
  1961Ôćĺ\subsection{The Goldstino direction and the Volkov--Akulov realization}
  1962Ôćĺ\label{subsec:va}
  1963Ôćĺ
  1964ÔćĺWhen SUSY is spontaneously broken, a massless fermion appears: the 
  1965ÔćĺGoldstino, the fermionic analogue of the Goldstone boson. Its decay 
  1966Ôćĺconstant $f_{\text{VA}}$ sets the SUSY-breaking scale through the 
  1967ÔćĺVolkov--Akulov Lagrangian \cite{VolkovAkulov1973}:
  1968Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1969Ôćĺ\mathcal{L}_{\text{VA}} = -f_{\text{VA}}^2 \det\!\left(
  1970Ôćĺ\delta_\mu^\nu + \frac{i}{f_{\text{VA}}^2}\,\bar{\lambda}\bar{\sigma}^\nu
  1971Ôćĺ\partial_\mu\lambda\right),
  1972Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1973Ôćĺwhere $\lambda$ is the Goldstino field. At low energies, the Goldstino 
  1974Ôćĺcouples derivatively with strength $\sim 1/f_{\text{VA}}^2$, exactly 
  1975Ôćĺas the pion couples with strength $\sim 1/f_\pi^2$ in the chiral 
  1976ÔćĺLagrangian.
  1977Ôćĺ
  1978ÔćĺThe sBootstrap identification is:
  1979Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1980Ôćĺf_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi\,, \qquad 
  1981Ôćĺ\text{Goldstino} = \text{muon (partner of }\pi\text{)}\,.
  1982Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1983ÔćĺMore precisely, the muon is a \emph{pseudo-Goldstino}: in the 
  1984Ôćĺlimit $m_q \to 0$ where the pion becomes exactly massless (a true 
  1985ÔćĺGoldstone boson), its fermionic partner also becomes massless (a 
  1986Ôćĺtrue Goldstino). The finite quark masses explicitly break both 
  1987Ôćĺchiral symmetry (giving $m_\pi \neq 0$) and the sBootstrap SUSY 
  1988Ôćĺ(giving $m_\mu \neq m_\pi$), with the splitting controlled by 
  1989Ôćĺ$f_\pi$:
  1990Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1991Ôćĺm_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2\,.
  1992Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1993Ôćĺ
  1994ÔćĺThis is the Volkov--Akulov relation for the pseudo-Goldstino: the 
  1995Ôćĺmass splitting between the scalar (pion) and its fermionic partner 
  1996Ôćĺ(muon) is set by the SUSY-breaking scale squared.
  1997Ôćĺ
  1998ÔćĺThe VA mechanism is not a separate breaking from the F-term: it is 
  1999Ôćĺthe \emph{low-energy, composite-level description} of the same 
  2000Ôćĺbreaking. At the microscopic level, $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ 
  2001Ôćĺin the meson superfield; at the composite level, this manifests 
  2002Ôćĺas the Goldstino coupling with $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$. These 
  2003Ôćĺtwo quantities---the Goldstino decay constant and the pion decay 
  2004Ôćĺconstant---are \emph{identified} in the sBootstrap; this is the 
  2005Ôćĺcentral claim, not a derivation.
  2006Ôćĺ
  2007ÔćĺThe identification should not be confused with the standard relation
  2008Ôćĺbetween the $F$-term VEV and the chiral condensate. In standard
  2009ÔćĺSQCD,
  2010Ôćĺ%
  2011Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2012Ôćĺ\langle F\rangle \sim \frac{|\qqbar|}{\fpi} \sim
  2013Ôćĺ\frac{(250\MeV)^3}{92\MeV} \approx 1.7\times 10^5\MeV^2\,,
  2014Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2015Ôćĺ%
  2016Ôćĺwhile $\fpi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2$---a factor of ${\sim}20$
  2017Ôćĺdiscrepancy. The GOR relation \cite{GOR1968},
  2018Ôćĺ$m_\pi^2 \fpi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$, connects these through the
  2019Ôćĺsmall quark masses: the ratio $|\qqbar|/\fpi \sim 20$ is exactly
  2020Ôćĺ$(m_u + m_d)^{-1} m_\pi^2 \fpi \approx 20$, so the discrepancy is
  2021Ôćĺthe chiral suppression factor, not a tuning. The sBootstrap
  2022Ôćĺidentification $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is therefore a
  2023Ôćĺ\emph{low-energy effective} statement: the breaking scale appearing
  2024Ôćĺin the meson--lepton mass relation is $f_\pi$ (the Goldstone decay
  2025Ôćĺconstant), not the microscopic condensate $|\qqbar|^{1/3}$.
  2026Ôćĺ
  2027ÔćĺThe VA realization gives more than a mass formula---it organizes the 
  2028Ôćĺentire breaking narrative. In the chiral limit ($m_q \to 0$), both 
  2029Ôćĺpion and muon are exactly massless: the pion as the Goldstone boson 
  2030Ôćĺof broken chiral symmetry, the muon as the Goldstino of broken 
  2031ÔćĺsBootstrap SUSY. This is the \emph{exact seed}: the $B$-meson 
  2032Ôćĺdirection sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$), and the 
  2033Ôćĺ$\pi$--$\mu$ pair is massless. Turning on quark masses ($m_q \neq 0$) 
  2034Ôćĺdoes three things simultaneously:
  2035Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  2036Ôćĺ\item Gives the pion its GOR mass (lifting the flat direction for $\pi$).
  2037Ôćĺ\item Gives the muon its mass (the pseudo-Goldstino acquires mass 
  2038Ôćĺwhen the explicit breaking parameter $m_q$ is nonzero).
  2039Ôćĺ\item Generates the $B$-meson mass (the flat direction for $\Phi_0$ 
  2040Ôćĺis lifted by the Coleman--Weinberg potential, i.e., by the same 
  2041Ôćĺquark masses that feed the GOR formula).
  2042Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  2043ÔćĺThe splitting between (1) and (2) is $f_\pi^2$---the VA relation. 
  2044ÔćĺThe seed-to-bloom transition of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples} is the same 
  2045Ôćĺphysics seen from the charge-space perspective: $m_q \neq 0$ lifts 
  2046Ôćĺthe zero, generates the $b$/$B$ mass, flips the sign of $\zch_s$ 
  2047Ôćĺand $\zch_\pi$, and produces the full triple. The VA equation 
  2048Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:pimu} is not one fact among many; it is the generating 
  2049Ôćĺrelation from which the bloom follows.
  2050Ôćĺ
  2051ÔćĺA note on quantum numbers: the Goldstino of spontaneously broken 
  2052Ôćĺspacetime SUSY is a neutral fermion (the fermionic component of the 
  2053Ôćĺsuperfield whose $F$-term breaks SUSY). The muon carries electric 
  2054Ôćĺcharge and lepton number. The sBootstrap SUSY, however, is not a 
  2055Ôćĺspacetime symmetry but an emergent hadronic symmetry---a 
  2056ÔćĺMiyazawa-type superalgebra \cite{Miyazawa} acting on composite 
  2057Ôćĺstates. In this context, the ``Goldstino'' need not carry the 
  2058Ôćĺquantum numbers of a spacetime supercharge; it carries the quantum 
  2059Ôćĺnumbers appropriate to the broken hadronic generator. The muon's 
  2060Ôćĺcharge is inherited from the pion multiplet it partners.
  2061Ôćĺ
  2062Ôćĺ\subsection{D-term breaking: electromagnetic splittings}
  2063ÔćĺThe unbroken $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ gauge symmetry allows a 
  2064ÔćĺFayet--Iliopoulos (FI) term \cite{FayetIliopoulos}:
  2065Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2066Ôćĺ\mathcal{L}_{\text{FI}} = \xi\,D_{\text{em}}\,,
  2067Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2068Ôćĺwhich shifts scalar masses by $\delta m^2 = Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi$, 
  2069Ôćĺwhere $Q_{\text{em}}$ is the electric charge. This is the 
  2070Ôćĺsupersymmetric version of the electromagnetic mass difference.
  2071Ôćĺ
  2072ÔćĺIn QCD without SUSY, the electromagnetic mass splitting of the 
  2073Ôćĺpion is given by the Das--Guralnik--Mathur--Low--Young (DGMLY) 
  2074Ôćĺformula \cite{DGMLY1967}:
  2075Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2076Ôćĺm_{\pi^\pm}^2 - m_{\pi^0}^2 = \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,
  2077Ôćĺm_\rho^2
  2078Ôćĺ\approx (35.5\MeV)^2\,,
  2079Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2080Ôćĺwhich in the SUSY framework becomes a D-term contribution:
  2081Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2082Ôćĺ\xi \sim \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,m_\rho^2 
  2083Ôćĺ\sim (35\MeV)^2\,.
  2084Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2085Ôćĺ
  2086ÔćĺIn charge space, the D-term shifts $\zch$ by an amount proportional 
  2087Ôćĺto $Q_{\text{em}}$:
  2088Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2089Ôćĺ\zch \to \zch + \delta\zch\,, \qquad

content.text: Now let me read the remaining critical sections of sbootstrap_v4d.tex.

content.content:
2090Ôćĺ\delta\zch \approx \frac{Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi}{2\zch}\,.
  2091Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2092ÔćĺFor the pion ($Q_{\text{em}} = \pm 1$, $\zch \approx 11.8$): 
  2093Ôćĺ$\delta\zch \approx 0.2\MeV^{1/2}$, matching the observed 
  2094Ôćĺ$\zch_{\pi^\pm} - \zch_{\pi^0} = 11.82 - 11.62 = 0.20\MeV^{1/2}$.
  2095Ôćĺ
  2096ÔćĺThe D-term effect is subleading to the F-term: 
  2097Ôćĺ$\xi \sim (35\MeV)^2$ versus $f_\pi^2 \sim (92\MeV)^2$. It 
  2098Ôćĺoperates \emph{within} each supermultiplet entry, splitting charged 
  2099Ôćĺfrom neutral mesons, while the F-term splits bosons from fermions 
  2100Ôćĺacross the supermultiplet.
  2101Ôćĺ
  2102ÔćĺWitten's inequality \cite{Witten1983} guarantees 
  2103Ôćĺ$m_{\pi^\pm} > m_{\pi^0}$ in QCD with electromagnetism---the 
  2104Ôćĺcharged pion is always heavier. In the SUSY framework this 
  2105Ôćĺbecomes the statement that the D-term contribution is positive 
  2106Ôćĺfor $Q_{\text{em}} \neq 0$, consistent with the FI sign.
  2107Ôćĺ
  2108Ôćĺ\subsection{Mapping the breaking to the tuples}
  2109ÔćĺThe tuple structure maps onto a layered breaking with two independent 
  2110Ôćĺmechanisms (F-term and D-term) plus a separate heavy-sector origin. 
  2111ÔćĺThe Volkov--Akulov realization of \S\ref{subsec:va} is not a third 
  2112Ôćĺmechanism: it is the low-energy, composite-level description of the 
  2113Ôćĺsame F-term breaking. The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} \emph{sets the 
  2114Ôćĺscale} ($f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$); the O'Raifeartaigh/SQCD dynamics 
  2115Ôćĺof \S\ref{subsec:fterm} \emph{provides the mechanism} 
  2116Ôćĺ($\langle F\rangle \neq 0$).
  2117Ôćĺ
  2118Ôćĺ\paragraph{Layer 1: F-term/VA (chiral condensate, scale $\sim f_\pi$).}
  2119ÔćĺThe chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry 
  2120Ôćĺand the sBootstrap SUSY. This is the primary breaking, affecting 
  2121Ôćĺthe light sector. The seed triples $(0, s, c)$ and $(0, \pi, D_s)$ 
  2122Ôćĺare the pre-breaking configurations; the massless member is the flat 
  2123Ôćĺdirection whose $F$-term is forced nonzero by confinement. After 
  2124Ôćĺbreaking:
  2125Ôćĺ\begin{itemize}
  2126Ôćĺ\item The zero member is lifted and acquires negative charge 
  2127Ôćĺ($\zch < 0$), becoming the sign-flipped member of the full triple 
  2128Ôćĺ($-s$, $-\pi$).
  2129Ôćĺ\item The full triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ emerge, 
  2130Ôćĺincorporating the $b$-quark and $B$-meson sectors.
  2131Ôćĺ\item The lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the fermionic shadow: 
  2132Ôćĺthe leptons acquire masses through the same condensate, with the 
  2133Ôćĺmuon as pseudo-Goldstino.
  2134Ôćĺ\end{itemize}
  2135Ôćĺ
  2136Ôćĺ\paragraph{Layer 2: D-term (electromagnetism, scale $\sim \alpha_{\text{em}} m_\rho$).}
  2137ÔćĺThe $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI term generates electromagnetic mass 
  2138Ôćĺsplittings within each multiplet. This is a perturbative correction 
  2139Ôćĺon top of the F-term breaking, affecting only charged particles. 
  2140ÔćĺIt does not create new Koide triples but shifts existing ones by 
  2141Ôćĺ$O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$.
  2142Ôćĺ
  2143Ôćĺ\paragraph{Layer 3: The heavy sector (scale $\sim v_0^{(cbt)}$).}
  2144ÔćĺThe $(c,b,t)$ triple at $v_0^2 \approx 29.6\GeV$ operates far 
  2145Ôćĺabove the chiral scale. It has no seed (no massless member), no 
  2146Ôćĺmeson shadow (top does not hadronize), and its breaking may be 
  2147Ôćĺconnected to a different sector of the theory---possibly the 
  2148Ôćĺelectroweak Higgs mechanism.
  2149Ôćĺ
  2150ÔćĺThis layered structure involves three distinct scales, each with 
  2151Ôćĺdifferent physical meaning:
  2152Ôćĺ\begin{align}
  2153Ôćĺ\text{F-term (boson--fermion splitting):} &\quad 
  2154Ôćĺf_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \quad f_\pi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2\,; \\
  2155Ôćĺ\text{D-term (EM fine structure):} &\quad 
  2156Ôćĺ\xi^{1/2} \approx 35\MeV\,, \quad \xi \approx 1{,}200\MeV^2\,; \\
  2157Ôćĺ\text{Heavy sector (Koide scale):} &\quad 
  2158Ôćĺv_0^{(cbt)} \approx 172\MeV^{1/2}\,, \quad (v_0^{(cbt)})^2 \approx 29{,}600\MeV\,.
  2159Ôćĺ\end{align}
  2160ÔćĺComparing the squared scales: $f_\pi^2 \gg \xi$ (F-term dominates 
  2161Ôćĺover D-term by a factor $\sim 7$, consistent with the D-term being 
  2162Ôćĺa perturbative $O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$ correction). The heavy-sector 
  2163Ôćĺscale $(v_0^{(cbt)})^2$ carries dimensions of $\MeV$ (not $\MeV^2$) 
  2164Ôćĺbecause $v_0$ is a charge ($\MeV^{1/2}$), not an energy; direct 
  2165Ôćĺcomparison with $f_\pi$ requires specifying which physical quantity 
  2166Ôćĺis being compared.
  2167Ôćĺ
  2168Ôćĺ\subsection{The O'Raifeartaigh structure in SQCD}
  2169Ôćĺ
  2170ÔćĺIn Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, the quantum constraint 
  2171Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:seiberg} provides a natural setting for the 
  2172ÔćĺO'Raifeartaigh mechanism. The constraint $\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ 
  2173Ôćĺmeans the moduli space has a specific geometry: not all field directions 
  2174Ôćĺare flat. When quark masses are added via a tree-level superpotential 
  2175Ôćĺ$W_{\text{tree}} = m_i M^i{}_i$ (sum over flavors), the $F$-terms become:
  2176Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2177ÔćĺF_{M^i{}_j} = m_i\,\delta^i_j + \Lambda^6\,(\text{constraint terms})\,.
  2178Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2179Ôćĺ
  2180ÔćĺFor the seed triple $(0, s, c)$, the ``$0$'' member corresponds to a 
  2181Ôćĺdirection in the meson matrix where the tree-level mass vanishes---the 
  2182Ôćĺentry involving the lightest quark combination. In the exact chiral 
  2183Ôćĺlimit ($m_u = m_d = 0$), this entry is truly massless, and the 
  2184Ôćĺconstraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} forces $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ for 
  2185Ôćĺthat entry. This is the dynamical O'Raifeartaigh mechanism:
  2186Ôćĺconfinement (encoded in $\Lambda^6$) plays the role of the parameter
  2187Ôćĺ$f$ in the superpotential, and the impossibility of setting all
  2188Ôćĺ$F$-terms to zero is a consequence of the strong dynamics rather
  2189Ôćĺthan a choice of superpotential.
  2190Ôćĺ
  2191Ôćĺ\paragraph{The pseudo-modulus and the Koide seed.}
  2192ÔćĺIn the ISS magnetic dual, the tree-level superpotential contains a
  2193ÔćĺYukawa coupling $h\,\mathrm{Tr}(q\,M\,\tilde q)$ between the magnetic
  2194Ôćĺquarks $q$, $\tilde q$ and the meson matrix $M$. The O'Raifeartaigh
  2195Ôćĺparameters map as $g \to h$, $m \to h\mu$, $v \to \langle X\rangle$
  2196Ôćĺ(the pseudo-modulus VEV), so the seed Koide condition
  2197Ôćĺ$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ becomes $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$---the
  2198Ôćĺmagnetic Yukawa~$h$ cancels. The Koide seed fixes the
  2199Ôćĺpseudo-modulus VEV, not the coupling~$h$.
  2200ÔćĺAt one-loop Coleman--Weinberg level $\langle X\rangle = 0$; a
  2201Ôćĺnon-canonical K\"ahler correction provides the mechanism.
  2202Ôćĺ
  2203Ôćĺ\paragraph{K\"ahler stabilization of the pseudo-modulus.}
  2204ÔćĺIn the minimal O'Raifeartaigh model ($W = fX + m\phi\tilde\phi
  2205Ôćĺ+ gX\phi^2$) with canonical K\"ahler potential,
  2206Ôćĺthe CW potential is monotonically increasing and selects
  2207Ôćĺ$\langle X\rangle = 0$.  Polynomial superpotential deformations
  2208Ôćĺ(including cubic, quartic, and dimension-five operators)
  2209Ôćĺeither vanish at the vacuum, reinforce $v = 0$, or produce
  2210Ôćĺminima at $gv/m \ll \sqrt{3}$.
  2211Ôćĺ
  2212ÔćĺA negative quartic K\"ahler correction resolves this:
  2213Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2214Ôćĺ  K = |X|^2 + \frac{c}{\Lambda_K^2}\,|X|^4\,,
  2215Ôćĺ  \qquad c < 0\,.
  2216Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2217ÔćĺThe tree-level potential becomes
  2218Ôćĺ$V_{\text{tree}} = f^2/(1 + 4c\,v^2/\Lambda_K^2)$, which has
  2219Ôćĺa pole at $v_{\text{pole}} = \Lambda_K/(2\sqrt{|c|})$.
  2220ÔćĺNear the pole the CW contribution is negligible
  2221Ôćĺ($V_{\text{CW}} \sim 10^{-4}\,m^4 \ll V_{\text{tree}}$),
  2222Ôćĺand the effective potential is minimized at $v_{\text{pole}}$.
  2223ÔćĺDemanding $v_{\text{pole}} = \sqrt{3}\,m/g$ gives the exact condition
  2224Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2225Ôćĺ  c = -\,\frac{\Lambda_K^2}{12\,m^2}\,.
  2226Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2227ÔćĺWith $\Lambda_K = m$ this is $c = -1/12$, an algebraic result:
  2228Ôćĺ$\Lambda_K/(2\sqrt{1/12}) = \sqrt{12}/2 = \sqrt{3}$.
  2229ÔćĺNo numerical tuning is required.
  2230ÔćĺAt this VEV the fermion spectrum is
  2231Ôćĺ$(0,\, (2{-}\sqrt{3})\,m,\, (2{+}\sqrt{3})\,m)$
  2232Ôćĺwith $Q = 4/6 = 2/3$ exactly
  2233Ôćĺ(since $(\sqrt{2{-}\sqrt{3}} + \sqrt{2{+}\sqrt{3}})^2 = 4 + 2 = 6$),
  2234Ôćĺconfirming the Koide seed. The K\"ahler metric
  2235Ôćĺ$K_{X\bar X} = 1 - v^2/(3m^2)$ vanishes at $v = \sqrt{3}\,m$,
  2236Ôćĺsignaling the boundary of the effective field theory.
  2237Ôćĺ
  2238ÔćĺThe ISS mechanism (Intriligator--Seiberg--Shih \cite{ISS2006}) 
  2239Ôćĺestablishes metastable SUSY-breaking vacua for $N_c < N_f < 3N_c/2$, 
  2240Ôćĺwhere the rank condition in the magnetic dual prevents all $F$-terms 
  2241Ôćĺfrom vanishing. The sBootstrap case $N_f = N_c = 3$ is strictly 
  2242Ôćĺ\emph{below} the ISS window---at $N_f = N_c$ the low-energy 
  2243Ôćĺdescription is qualitatively different, governed by the 
  2244Ôćĺquantum-modified constraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} on the moduli space 
  2245Ôćĺrather than by a free magnetic dual. However, physical QCD has
  2246Ôćĺ$N_f = 3$ light flavors and $N_f = 4$--$5$ if charm and bottom are
  2247Ôćĺincluded at their respective thresholds. With charm included,
  2248Ôćĺ$N_f = 4$ falls within the ISS window ($3 < 4 < 4.5$); with both
  2249Ôćĺcharm and bottom, $N_f = 5$ falls in the conformal window
  2250Ôćĺ($4.5 < 5 < 9$), where the Seiberg seesaw constraint does not apply
  2251Ôćĺdirectly. The sBootstrap's multi-scale structure---with
  2252Ôćĺthe seed triples involving $s$, $c$, and $b$ quarks at different
  2253Ôćĺscales---requires treating the effective $N_f$ as
  2254Ôćĺscale-dependent: $N_f = 3$ below $m_c$ (confining, Seiberg
  2255Ôćĺconstraint), $N_f = 4$ above $m_c$ (ISS metastable SUSY breaking),
  2256Ôćĺand $N_f = 5$ above $m_b$ (conformal window).
  2257ÔćĺNote that $N_f = 4$ is the \emph{unique} integer satisfying
  2258Ôćĺ$3 < N_f < 4.5$ for $N_c = 3$, providing a dynamical rationale for
  2259Ôćĺexactly four light flavors $(u,d,s,c)$ in the SUSY-breaking sector.
  2260Ôćĺ
  2261Ôćĺ\paragraph{ISS vacuum lifetime.}
  2262ÔćĺThe metastable ISS vacuum decays to the SUSY-preserving ground state
  2263Ôćĺby tunneling with bounce action
  2264Ôćĺ$S_{\text{bounce}} \sim (8\pi^2/3)\,N_c\,(\Lambda_{\text{mag}}/h\mu)^4$,
  2265Ôćĺwhere $\mu$ is the lightest quark mass controlling the tunneling.
  2266ÔćĺFor $\mu = m_s = 93\MeV$, $h \sim O(1)$, and
  2267Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{mag}} \sim 300\MeV$:
  2268Ôćĺ$S_{\text{bounce}} \sim 10^2$--$10^3$ (depending on~$h$),
  2269Ôćĺgiving a lifetime exceeding the age of the universe by hundreds of
  2270Ôćĺorders of magnitude for any $h$ of order unity.
  2271ÔćĺThe metastable vacuum is cosmologically acceptable.
  2272Ôćĺ
  2273Ôćĺ\paragraph{The Seiberg seesaw and dual Koide.}
  2274ÔćĺAt the Seiberg vacuum with $B = \bar B = 0$, the diagonal meson VEVs
  2275Ôćĺscale as $\langle M^j{}_j\rangle \propto 1/m_j$: heavy quarks produce
  2276Ôćĺlight mesons and vice versa. This seesaw prompts a dual question: does
  2277Ôćĺthe inverse-mass spectrum $\{1/m_i\}$ satisfy a Koide condition?
  2278ÔćĺEvaluating $Q = \sum(1/m_i)/(\sum\sqrt{1/m_i})^2$ for the pure
  2279Ôćĺdown-type quarks $(d,s,b)$:
  2280Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2281Ôćĺ  Q(1/m_d,\, 1/m_s,\, 1/m_b) = 0.6652\,,
  2282Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2283Ôćĺa 0.22\% deviation from $2/3$---better than any direct quark Koide
  2284Ôćĺtriple. No other quark triple comes close under
  2285Ôćĺthe Seiberg seesaw (the next best, $(d,s,c)$, deviates by 4.2\%).
  2286ÔćĺThis ``dual Koide'' on $(d,s,b)$ (first reported here) connects
  2287Ôćĺthe UV mass hierarchy to the IR meson VEVs: the Seiberg constraint
  2288Ôćĺmaps the down-type quark masses into a near-Koide spectrum of
  2289Ôćĺmeson condensates. However, the
  2290Ôćĺresult is sensitive to $m_d$: within $\pm 1\sigma$ ($m_d = 4.67
  2291Ôćĺ\pm 0.48\MeV$), $Q$ ranges from $0.655$ to $0.676$, and the deviation
  2292Ôćĺfrom $2/3$ varies from $0.2\%$ to $1.7\%$. The dual Koide is an
  2293Ôćĺobservation at the edge of the current $m_d$ uncertainty.
  2294ÔćĺSetting $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 2/3$ exactly predicts
  2295Ôćĺ$m_b = 4562\MeV$ ($9\%$ from PDG, $12.7\sigma$)---much worse
  2296Ôćĺthan $v_0$-doubling ($m_b = 4177\MeV$, $0.07\%$).  The $0.22\%$
  2297Ôćĺcloseness of $Q$ to $2/3$ does not translate to a precise mass
  2298Ôćĺprediction because the Koide quadratic amplifies small $Q$
  2299Ôćĺdeviations into large mass deviations.
  2300Ôćĺ
  2301ÔćĺA simple algebraic identity connects the seed to the seesaw:
  2302Ôćĺfor any two masses, the Koide ratio
  2303Ôćĺ$Q(a,b) = (a+b)/(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})^2$ satisfies
  2304Ôćĺ$Q(a,b) = Q(1/a,\,1/b)$ identically.
  2305ÔćĺThe seed $(0, m_s, m_c)$ sits at this self-dual point:
  2306Ôćĺits Koide ratio is the same whether evaluated on quark masses or
  2307Ôćĺon Seiberg-dual meson VEVs $\propto 1/m_i$.  Thus the seed Koide
  2308Ôćĺin the electric (UV) theory is \emph{automatically} a seed Koide
  2309Ôćĺin the magnetic (IR) theory---the Seiberg duality preserves the
  2310Ôćĺseed condition without fine-tuning.
  2311ÔćĺFor the full bloomed triple $(-s,c,b)$, self-duality breaks:
  2312Ôćĺ$Q(-s,c,b) \neq Q(1/m_s,1/m_c,1/m_b)$ in general.
  2313ÔćĺThe dual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 0.665$ is therefore a
  2314Ôćĺ\emph{nontrivial} constraint on the down-type sector, not a
  2315Ôćĺtautological restatement of the seed.
  2316ÔćĺAmong all $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ quark triples, $(d,s,b)$ is the
  2317Ôćĺunique triple with inverse-mass Koide ratio within 1\% of $2/3$;
  2318Ôćĺthe next closest is $(d,s,c)$ at 4.2\% deviation---a 19:1 margin.
  2319Ôćĺ
  2320Ôćĺ\subsection{How the three supermultiplets join}
  2321Ôćĺ
  2322ÔćĺThe seed-to-bloom transition generates three separate Koide triples. 
  2323ÔćĺBut the full sBootstrap requires these triples to be \emph{embedded 
  2324Ôćĺin a single meson matrix}. The $3\times 3$ light-flavor matrix is:
  2325Ôćĺ
  2326Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2327ÔćĺM^{(3)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2328Ôćĺu\bar{u} & u\bar{d} & u\bar{s} \\
  2329Ôćĺd\bar{u} & d\bar{d} & d\bar{s} \\
  2330Ôćĺs\bar{u} & s\bar{d} & s\bar{s}
  2331Ôćĺ\end{pmatrix}
  2332Ôćĺ\;\longleftrightarrow\;
  2333Ôćĺ\begin{pmatrix}
  2334Ôćĺ\pi^0/\eta & \pi^+ & K^+ \\
  2335Ôćĺ\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 \\
  2336ÔćĺK^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta'
  2337Ôćĺ\end{pmatrix}.
  2338Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2339Ôćĺ
  2340ÔćĺWhen charm and bottom are included, the matrix extends to $5\times 5$. 
  2341ÔćĺWe display it schematically, with the members of the meson Koide 
  2342Ôćĺtriple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ marked by boxes:
  2343Ôćĺ
  2344Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2345ÔćĺM^{(5)} = \begin{pmatrix}
  2346Ôćĺ\pi^0/\eta & \boxed{\pi^+} & K^+ & D^0 & \boxed{B^+} \\
  2347Ôćĺ\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 & D^- & B^0 \\
  2348ÔćĺK^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta' & \boxed{D_s^-} & B_s \\
  2349Ôćĺ\bar{D}^0 & D^+ & D_s^+ & \eta_c & B_c \\
  2350ÔćĺB^- & \bar{B}^0 & \bar{B}_s & \bar{B}_c & \eta_b
  2351Ôćĺ\end{pmatrix},
  2352Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2353Ôćĺwhere rows are indexed by $(u, d, s, c, b)$ and columns by 
  2354Ôćĺ$(\bar{u}, \bar{d}, \bar{s}, \bar{c}, \bar{b})$.
  2355Ôćĺ
  2356ÔćĺThe three members of the meson Koide triple occupy different 
  2357Ôćĺoff-diagonal blocks of this matrix: $\pi^+ = u\bar{d}$ is in the 
  2358Ôćĺlight $(u,d)$ block, $D_s^- = s\bar{c}$ bridges the light-charm 
  2359Ôćĺsector, and $B^+ = u\bar{b}$ bridges the light-bottom sector. They 
  2360Ôćĺdo not form a ``slice'' in any simple linear-algebraic sense---they 
  2361Ôćĺare scattered entries connected by the Koide condition on their 
  2362Ôćĺ\emph{masses}, not by their positions in the matrix. The meson seed 
  2363Ôćĺ$(0, \pi, D_s)$ drops the $B^+$ and replaces it with zero (the 
  2364Ôćĺentry that would involve the top quark, absent from the matrix).
  2365Ôćĺ
  2366ÔćĺThis scattered structure is the central puzzle: the Koide triples 
  2367Ôćĺare \emph{mass relations} among matrix entries, not submatrices. 
  2368ÔćĺThe question is whether the Seiberg constraint 
  2369Ôćĺ$\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ (appropriately generalized to 
  2370Ôćĺ$5 \times 5$), combined with the SUSY-breaking dynamics, produces 
  2371Ôćĺcorrelations among these scattered entries that enforce the 
  2372Ôćĺenergy-balance condition $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
  2373Ôćĺ
  2374Ôćĺ\subsection{The energy balance is not an RG attractor}
  2375Ôćĺ\label{subsec:fixedpoint}
  2376ÔćĺOne might hope that the energy-balance condition
  2377Ôćĺ$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is a \emph{dynamical attractor}:
  2378Ôćĺthat three masses evolving under SUSY-breaking dynamics flow toward
  2379Ôćĺthe Koide surface $Q = 2/3$. However, the one-loop soft-mass RG
  2380Ôćĺequations in $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with universal Casimirs and
  2381Ôćĺnegligible Yukawas give $dm_i/dt = \gamma(M_g^2 - m_i)$, which
  2382Ôćĺdrives all masses to a common value $m_i \to M_g^2$, yielding
  2383Ôćĺ$Q \to 1/3$---the \emph{opposite} extreme. The Koide preservation
  2384Ôćĺcondition $\sum_i F_i(3 - 2S/\sqrt{m_i}) = 0$ on the $Q = 2/3$
  2385Ôćĺsurface is algebraically incompatible with any uniform attractor
  2386Ôćĺflow (by the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality, the required identity
  2387Ôćĺ$S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} = 9/2$ contradicts $S\sum 1/\sqrt{m_i} \geq 9$).
  2388Ôćĺ
  2389ÔćĺThe energy-balance condition therefore does \emph{not} arise from
  2390Ôćĺsimple RG running. It must instead be a \emph{boundary condition}
  2391Ôćĺon the superpotential---as in the O'Raifeartaigh realization of
  2392Ôćĺ\S\ref{subsec:fterm}, where $gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$ produces the exact
  2393ÔćĺKoide seed. The condition is set at the scale of SUSY breaking and
  2394Ôćĺthen preserved by the QCD-invariant mass ratios, rather than being
  2395Ôćĺan RG attractor.
  2396Ôćĺ
  2397Ôćĺ\subsection{The exact seed and what breaks it}
  2398Ôćĺ\label{subsec:exactseed}
  2399Ôćĺ
  2400ÔćĺThe seed configuration $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ requires \emph{two} 
  2401Ôćĺindependent conditions:
  2402Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  2403Ôćĺ\item One member at $\zch = 0$ exactly (the flat direction).
  2404Ôćĺ\item The ratio of the massive members equals $2 - \sqrt{3}$:
  2405Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2406Ôćĺ\frac{\sqrt{m_a}}{\sqrt{m_b}} = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.
  2407Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2408Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  2409ÔćĺThese are logically distinct. The chiral limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$, 
  2410Ôćĺ$\qqbar \neq 0$) enforces condition (1) for the meson sector: the 
  2411Ôćĺpion is an exact Goldstone boson, $m_\pi = 0$, and in the 
  2412Ôćĺpicture of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} the $B$-meson direction sits at 
  2413Ôćĺthe flat direction before acquiring mass. But the chiral limit says 
  2414Ôćĺ\emph{nothing} about condition (2)---it does not constrain 
  2415Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_{D_s}}/\sqrt{m_B}$ or $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ 
  2416Ôćĺto equal $2 - \sqrt{3}$.
  2417Ôćĺ
  2418ÔćĺThe physical deviations from the exact ratio are small but 
  2419Ôćĺmeasurable:
  2420Ôćĺ
  2421Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  2422Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{lccc}
  2423Ôćĺ\toprule
  2424ÔćĺSeed & Exact ratio & Physical ratio & Deviation \\
  2425Ôćĺ\midrule
  2426Ôćĺ$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2427Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_\pi}/\sqrt{m_{D_s}} = 0.2663$ & $-0.6\%$ \\
  2428Ôćĺ$(0, s, c)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2429Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} = 0.2712$ & $+1.2\%$ \\
  2430Ôćĺ$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.2680 & 
  2431Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 0.2439$ & $-9.0\%$ \\
  2432Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  2433Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  2434Ôćĺ\end{center}
  2435Ôćĺ
  2436ÔćĺThe meson seed is closest to the exact ratio (0.6\% deviation). 
  2437ÔćĺThe quark seed deviates by 1.2\%. A natural thought is that QCD 
  2438Ôćĺrunning might shift the quark ratio: perhaps at some scale $\mu^*$ 
  2439Ôćĺthe ratio $\sqrt{m_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{m_c(\mu^*)}$ hits $2 - \sqrt{3}$ 
  2440Ôćĺexactly. But this is \emph{not possible within QCD alone}. At leading 
  2441Ôćĺorder, all quark masses run with the same anomalous dimension 
  2442Ôćĺ$\gamma_m$:
  2443Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2444Ôćĺm_i(\mu) = m_i(\mu_0)\left(\frac{\alpha_s(\mu)}{\alpha_s(\mu_0)}\right)^{\gamma_m/\beta_0},
  2445Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  2446Ôćĺso the ratio $m_s(\mu)/m_c(\mu)$---and therefore 
  2447Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$---is an RG invariant of QCD. Higher-order 
  2448Ôćĺcorrections break this invariance, but only at the $O(\alpha_s^2)$ 
  2449Ôćĺlevel, far too small to account for 1.2\%.
  2450Ôćĺ
  2451ÔćĺThe quark seed deviation is therefore \emph{intrinsic}: the 
  2452Ôćĺ$\overline{\text{MS}}$ mass ratio $m_s/m_c$ is a fixed number 
  2453Ôćĺ(at any QCD scale), and it misses the Koide target by 1.2\%. 
  2454ÔćĺThree possible explanations present themselves:
  2455Ôćĺ
  2456Ôćĺ\paragraph{Electroweak running.} Above the electroweak scale, 
  2457Ôćĺquark masses are controlled by Yukawa couplings $y_i$ to the 
  2458ÔćĺHiggs, which \emph{do} run differently for different flavors: 
  2459Ôćĺthe $\beta$-functions for $y_s$ and $y_c$ involve different 
  2460Ôćĺelectroweak quantum numbers ($SU(2)_L$ doublet structure, 
  2461Ôćĺhypercharge). The ratio $y_s/y_c$ evolves with scale, and 
  2462Ôćĺthere may exist a scale $\mu^*$ above the electroweak 
  2463Ôćĺthreshold where $\sqrt{y_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{y_c(\mu^*)} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$. 
  2464ÔćĺThis would identify the ``seed scale'' as an electroweak-sector 
  2465Ôćĺphenomenon, not a QCD one---consistent with the sBootstrap's 
  2466Ôćĺidentification of the breaking with $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ 
  2467Ôćĺrather than $SU(3)_c$.
  2468Ôćĺ
  2469Ôćĺ\paragraph{Charge running.} Even if mass ratios are QCD-invariant,
  2470Ôćĺthe Koide decomposition $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k$ may have its own
  2471Ôćĺflow. The Koide scale $v_0 = z_0^2$ sets the common scale,
  2472Ôćĺand the fluctuations $z_k$ encode the deviations. As shown in
  2473Ôćĺ\S\ref{subsec:fixedpoint}, simple RG running drives $Q \to 1/3$
  2474Ôćĺrather than $2/3$, so the energy-balance condition cannot be an
  2475Ôćĺattractor in the usual sense. However, the O'Raifeartaigh
  2476Ôćĺrealization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) shows that the condition is a
  2477Ôćĺboundary condition on the superpotential ($gv = \sqrt{3}\,m$).
  2478ÔćĺThe 1.2\% deviation of the quark seed ratio may then reflect
  2479Ôćĺthe distance between the physical quark masses and the exact
  2480Ôćĺsuperpotential constraint.
  2481Ôćĺ
  2482Ôćĺ\paragraph{Meson vs.\ quark deviations.} The meson deviation 
  2483Ôćĺ(0.6\%) is smaller than the quark deviation (1.2\%), and for 
  2484Ôćĺa different reason: meson masses are \emph{not} simple functions 
  2485Ôćĺof quark mass ratios. The pion mass is given by the GOR 
  2486Ôćĺrelation $m_\pi^2 f_\pi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$; the $D_s$ 
  2487Ôćĺmass involves heavy-quark effective theory; and both receive 
  2488Ôćĺchiral perturbation theory corrections. These nonperturbative 
  2489ÔćĺQCD effects are the dominant source of the meson seed deviation, 
  2490Ôćĺand are in principle calculable at NLO in ChPT.
  2491Ôćĺ
  2492Ôćĺ\paragraph{A remarkable feature of the VA relation.} The 
  2493Ôćĺfoundational equation $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2$ contains 
  2494Ôćĺno QCD coupling constant: no $\alpha_s$, no $N_c$, no 
  2495Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$. It is a pure mass relation among three 
  2496Ôćĺexperimentally measured quantities---two masses and a decay 
  2497Ôćĺconstant---holding to 2.2\%. In the sBootstrap, this is natural: 
  2498Ôćĺthe relation is a SUSY mass splitting, set by the breaking 
  2499Ôćĺscale $f_\pi$, and SUSY mass relations do not involve the gauge 
  2500Ôćĺcoupling of the confining theory. Standard QCD \emph{explains} 
  2501Ôćĺ$f_\pi$ in terms of $\qqbar$ and $\alpha_s$; the sBootstrap 
  2502Ôćĺ\emph{uses} $f_\pi$ as the fundamental parameter, with QCD 
  2503Ôćĺproviding the dynamical origin but not appearing in the mass 
  2504Ôćĺformula. The absence of QCD factors in \eqref{eq:pimu} is a 
  2505Ôćĺhint that the relevant symmetry is not $SU(3)_c$ but the 
  2506Ôćĺhadronic superalgebra that connects pions to leptons.
  2507Ôćĺ
  2508ÔćĺThe lepton ``seed'' has the worst ratio match ($-9\%$), which 
  2509Ôćĺis consistent with the observation that the lepton triple is 
  2510Ôćĺ\emph{not} a true seed: $m_e \neq 0$, and the electron was never 
  2511Ôćĺat the flat direction. The leptons are an ``almost-seed'' whose 
  2512Ôćĺexcellent $Q = 0.66666$ arises not from the seed structure but 
  2513Ôćĺfrom the smallness of $m_e$, which makes the two conditions 
  2514Ôćĺapproximately degenerate. For the leptons, condition (1) is 
  2515Ôćĺapproximate rather than exact, and condition (2) is correspondingly 
  2516Ôćĺrelaxed.
  2517Ôćĺ
  2518Ôćĺ\subsection{What the $(c,b,t)$ triple tells us}
  2519Ôćĺ
  2520ÔćĺThe $(c,b,t)$ triple ($Q = 0.6695$, $v_0^2 = 29.6\GeV$) has 
  2521Ôćĺneither a seed nor a meson shadow. It operates at a scale far 
  2522Ôćĺabove $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, suggesting its origin is different:
  2523Ôćĺ
  2524Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  2525Ôćĺ\item It may be governed by the electroweak Higgs mechanism rather
  2526Ôćĺthan the chiral condensate. In a two-Higgs-doublet model with
  2527Ôćĺ$\tan\beta = 1$ (as predicted by the mixed-triple Koide condition,
  2528Ôćĺ\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}), $y_t \approx \sqrt{2}$;
  2529Ôćĺthe Koide condition on $(c,b,t)$ would then constrain the Yukawa
  2530Ôćĺcoupling matrix.
  2531Ôćĺ\item It may represent a Volkov--Akulov realization at the 
  2532Ôćĺelectroweak scale: if there is a second SUSY breaking at 
  2533Ôćĺ$f' \sim v_{\text{EW}}$, the $(c,b,t)$ triple would be its 
  2534Ôćĺanalog of the $(\pi, \mu)$ system, but with $f'$ replacing $f_\pi$.
  2535Ôćĺ\item Or it may be a purely kinematic accident---the quark masses 
  2536Ôćĺhappen to satisfy the Koide condition without a dynamical reason. 
  2537ÔćĺThe 0.42\% precision makes this unlikely but not impossible.
  2538Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  2539Ôćĺ
  2540ÔćĺInterpretation (1) is most natural within the sBootstrap: the
  2541Ôćĺ$(c,b,t)$ triple involves quarks whose masses are dominated by
  2542ÔćĺYukawa couplings to the Higgs, not by the chiral condensate.
  2543ÔćĺSince $m_i = y_i\,v_{\text{EW}}/\sqrt{2}$, the Koide condition
  2544Ôćĺ$Q = 2/3$ translates into a constraint on the Yukawa couplings
  2545Ôćĺ(see \S\ref{subsec:superpotential}):
  2546Ôćĺ$\sum y_i/(\sum \sqrt{y_i})^2 = 2/3$.
  2547ÔćĺWith $y_t \approx 1$ fixed by the top mass, this determines
  2548Ôćĺ$y_b/y_t$ and $y_c/y_t$ through the Koide parametrization.
  2549ÔćĺThe top Yukawa $y_t = 0.99$ is the only coupling of order unity;
  2550Ôćĺthe charm and bottom Yukawas ($y_c = 0.010$, $y_b = 0.034$) are
  2551Ôćĺeffective couplings generated by the Seiberg seesaw, not independent
  2552Ôćĺfundamental parameters.  In this picture, the entire fermion mass
  2553Ôćĺhierarchy derives from a single Yukawa coupling ($y_t \approx 1$)
  2554Ôćĺplus confining dynamics at $\Lambda \sim 300\MeV$.
  2555ÔćĺThe chiral sector and the electroweak sector would each
  2556Ôćĺindependently produce a Koide-type energy balance, with the charm
  2557Ôćĺand bottom quarks serving as the bridge between the two.
  2558Ôćĺ
  2559Ôćĺ\paragraph{CKM mixing and the Koide triples.}
  2560ÔćĺA striking feature of the quark Koide triples is that they
  2561Ôćĺ\emph{obligatorily mix up-type and down-type quarks}: $(-s,c,b)$
  2562Ôćĺcontains one up-type ($c$) and two down-type ($s,b$), while
  2563Ôćĺ$(c,b,t)$ contains two up-type ($c,t$) and one down-type ($b$). An
  2564Ôćĺexhaustive scan of all $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ quark triples with all
  2565Ôćĺsign choices confirms that \emph{every} triple close to $Q = 2/3$ is
  2566Ôćĺmixed---neither the pure down-type $(d,s,b)$ nor the pure up-type
  2567Ôćĺ$(u,c,t)$ comes close ($Q = 0.73$ and $Q = 0.85$ respectively).
  2568ÔćĺThis mixing of mass eigenvalues from different Yukawa sectors is
  2569Ôćĺpuzzling in the Standard Model, where up-type and down-type masses are
  2570Ôćĺeigenvalues of independent matrices $Y_u$ and $Y_d$. It is natural,
  2571Ôćĺhowever, in an $SU(5)_f$ flavor-symmetric framework where all five
  2572Ôćĺquarks are treated democratically.
  2573Ôćĺ
  2574ÔćĺThe Koide constraints on the two overlapping triples, together with
  2575Ôćĺthe seed condition on $(0,s,c)$, determine $(s,c,b)$ from a single
  2576Ôćĺinput ($m_t$). The unconstrained masses are $m_u$ and $m_d$---the
  2577Ôćĺfirst generation. The Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation~\cite{GST1968} $|V_{us}| \approx
  2578Ôćĺ\sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ connects precisely these free parameters to the
  2579ÔćĺCabibbo angle ($|V_{us}| = 0.224$ vs.\ PDG $0.2243$,
  2580ÔćĺTable~\ref{tab:predictions}), suggesting that the CKM matrix
  2581Ôćĺencodes the residual freedom left undetermined by the Koide
  2582Ôćĺstructure.
  2583Ôćĺ
  2584ÔćĺIf interpretation (2) is also invoked, the sBootstrap has two breaking 
  2585Ôćĺscales:
  2586Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  2587Ôćĺf_{\text{chiral}} = f_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \qquad
  2588Ôćĺf_{\text{EW}} \sim v_{\text{EW}} \approx 246\GeV\,,
  2589Ôćĺ\end{equation}
