content.content:
2590Ôćĺand the full spectrum requires both. The light sector (leptons, 
  2591Ôćĺlight mesons) is governed by $f_\pi$; the heavy sector (top, and 
  2592Ôćĺby extension charm and bottom through the Koide network) is 
  2593Ôćĺgoverned by $v_{\text{EW}}$. The charm and bottom quarks, 
  2594Ôćĺappearing in both the $(0,s,c)/(-s,c,b)$ light-sector triples 
  2595Ôćĺand the $(c,b,t)$ heavy-sector triple, are the ``bridges'' 
  2596Ôćĺbetween the two scales.
  2597Ôćĺ
  2598Ôćĺ\subsection{Toward a Superpotential}
  2599Ôćĺ\label{subsec:superpotential}
  2600Ôćĺ
  2601ÔćĺConsider $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with gauge group $SU(3)_c$ and $N_f=5$ flavors
  2602Ôćĺof chiral superfields $Q^i$, $\bar Q_j$ ($i,j=1\ldots 5$, corresponding to
  2603Ôćĺ$u,d,s,c,b$) in the fundamental and antifundamental representations. The
  2604Ôćĺtree-level superpotential is
  2605Ôćĺ%
  2606Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2607Ôćĺ  W_{\text{tree}} = \operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)\,,
  2608Ôćĺ  \qquad
  2609Ôćĺ  \hat m = \operatorname{diag}(m_u,\, m_d,\, m_s,\, m_c,\, m_b)\,,
  2610Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2611Ôćĺ%
  2612Ôćĺwhere $M^i{}_j = Q^i\cdot\bar Q_j$ is the $5\times 5$ composite meson
  2613Ôćĺsuperfield. Since $m_c, m_b \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, the charm and
  2614Ôćĺbottom quarks are integrated out at tree level, reducing the effective
  2615Ôćĺtheory to $N_f = N_c = 3$ light flavors $(u,d,s)$. In this regime,
  2616ÔćĺSeiberg's exact results give confinement with the composite meson
  2617Ôćĺ$M^{(3)}$, baryons $B$, $\bar B$, and the quantum-modified
  2618Ôćĺconstraint~\eqref{eq:seiberg}:
  2619Ôćĺ%
  2620Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2621Ôćĺ  \det M^{(3)} - B\bar B = \Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{6}\,,
  2622Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2623Ôćĺ%
  2624Ôćĺwhere $\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$ is the dynamical scale of the effective
  2625Ôćĺthree-flavor theory after threshold matching at $m_c$ and $m_b$.
  2626ÔćĺImplementing this constraint via a Lagrange multiplier $X$ gives the full
  2627Ôćĺeffective superpotential
  2628Ôćĺ%
  2629Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2630Ôćĺ  W_{\text{eff}}
  2631Ôćĺ  = \operatorname{Tr}(\hat m^{(3)}\, M^{(3)})
  2632Ôćĺ  + X\!\left(\det M^{(3)} - B\bar B - \Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{6}\right).
  2633Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2634Ôćĺ
  2635ÔćĺThe $F$-term equations for the $3\times 3$ light-flavor block read
  2636Ôćĺ%
  2637Ôćĺ\begin{align*}
  2638Ôćĺ  \frac{\partial W_{\text{eff}}}{\partial M^i{}_j}
  2639Ôćĺ  &= \hat m_j\,\delta^i{}_j
  2640Ôćĺ    + X\,(\det M^{(3)})\,(M^{(3)\,-1})_i{}^j = 0\,,\\[4pt]
  2641Ôćĺ  \frac{\partial W_{\text{eff}}}{\partial B}
  2642Ôćĺ  &= -X\,\bar B = 0\,,\\[4pt]
  2643Ôćĺ  \frac{\partial W_{\text{eff}}}{\partial \bar B}
  2644Ôćĺ  &= -X\, B = 0\,.
  2645Ôćĺ\end{align*}
  2646Ôćĺ%
  2647ÔćĺThe baryon equations force either $X=0$ or $B=\bar B=0$. If $X=0$ the meson
  2648Ôćĺequation reduces to $\hat m_j\,\delta^i{}_j=0$, which has no solution for
  2649Ôćĺnonzero quark masses. Hence $B=\bar B=0$ and the constraint becomes
  2650Ôćĺ$\det M^{(3)} = \Lambda_{\text{eff}}^6$. The diagonal meson equation then requires
  2651Ôćĺ%
  2652Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2653Ôćĺ  \hat m_j + X\,\frac{\det M^{(3)}}{M^j{}_j} = 0
  2654Ôćĺ  \qquad(\text{no sum on } j)\,,
  2655Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2656Ôćĺ%
  2657Ôćĺwhich determines each diagonal entry of $M^{(3)}$ in terms of the quark masses and
  2658Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$. For a seed configuration in which one quark mass vanishes---say
  2659Ôćĺ$m_u\to 0$---the corresponding $F$-term
  2660Ôćĺ$F_{M^1{}_1}=X\,(\det M^{(3)})(M^{(3)\,-1})_1{}^1$ remains nonzero so long as
  2661Ôćĺ$\det M^{(3)}\neq 0$, which is guaranteed by the quantum constraint. Supersymmetry
  2662Ôćĺis therefore spontaneously broken, with order parameter
  2663Ôćĺ%
  2664Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2665Ôćĺ  \langle F \rangle \;\sim\; \fpi^2\,.
  2666Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2667Ôćĺ
  2668ÔćĺThe scalar and fermionic components of $M^i{}_j$ acquire different masses
  2669Ôćĺthrough this breaking. The scalar component (pseudoscalar meson $q_i\bar q_j$)
  2670Ôćĺpicks up an $F$-term contribution absent for the fermionic component (lepton,
  2671Ôćĺvia the Miyazawa superalgebra):
  2672Ôćĺ%
  2673Ôćĺ\begin{align*}
  2674Ôćĺ  m^2_{M^i{}_j}\big|_{\text{scalar}}
  2675Ôćĺ  &= |m_i + m_j|^2 + f^2\,,\\[3pt]
  2676Ôćĺ  m^2_{M^i{}_j}\big|_{\text{fermion}}
  2677Ôćĺ  &= |m_i + m_j|^2\,,
  2678Ôćĺ\end{align*}
  2679Ôćĺ%
  2680Ôćĺwhere $f^2\approx \fpi^2$. The individual masses $|m_i + m_j|$ are
  2681Ôćĺthe tree-level (perturbative) contributions; the physical masses are
  2682Ôćĺdominated by nonperturbative QCD effects that shift both meson and
  2683Ôćĺlepton by the same amount.  The mass \emph{splitting}, however,
  2684Ôćĺis universal across the multiplet:
  2685Ôćĺ%
  2686Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2687Ôćĺ  m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = f^2 \approx \fpi^2\,,
  2688Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2689Ôćĺ%
  2690Ôćĺbecause $f^2$ is a SUSY-breaking contribution independent of the QCD
  2691Ôćĺdynamics.  This is precisely the Volkov--Akulov
  2692Ôćĺrelation~\eqref{eq:pimu}.
  2693ÔćĺFor heavier mesons the actual splitting is strongly flavor-dependent
  2694Ôćĺ(\S\ref{sec:open}, ``kaon--muon puzzle'').
  2695Ôćĺ
  2696ÔćĺThe top quark, with $m_t\gg\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, does not hadronize and
  2697Ôćĺtherefore does not participate in the confined meson matrix---the counting
  2698Ôćĺ$N_f=5$ is exact. Its mass, and corrections to the heavy-quark sector
  2699Ôćĺ($c$, $b$), originate instead from electroweak symmetry breaking.
  2700ÔćĺIn $\mathcal{N}=1$ SUSY, holomorphy of the superpotential requires
  2701Ôćĺseparate Higgs doublets $H_u$ and $H_d$ for up-type and down-type
  2702Ôćĺquarks, giving
  2703Ôćĺ%
  2704Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2705Ôćĺ  W_{\text{Yukawa}}
  2706Ôćĺ  = y^u_{ij}\, H_u\, Q^i\, \bar Q_j
  2707Ôćĺ  + y^d_{ij}\, H_d\, Q^i\, \bar Q_j\,,
  2708Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2709Ôćĺ%
  2710Ôćĺwith $\langle H_u\rangle = v\sin\beta/\sqrt{2}$ and
  2711Ôćĺ$\langle H_d\rangle = v\cos\beta/\sqrt{2}$
  2712Ôćĺ($v = 246\GeV$, $\tan\beta = v_u/v_d$).
  2713ÔćĺThis is relevant only for quarks whose masses are dominated by the
  2714Ôćĺelectroweak condensate rather than the chiral condensate. For the light sector
  2715Ôćĺ($u,d,s$) the Yukawa contribution is negligible compared to
  2716Ôćĺ$\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)$. Note that $\hat m$ in the chiral
  2717Ôćĺmass term already \emph{is} the current quark mass generated by the
  2718ÔćĺHiggs mechanism; the two terms are not additive.  The Yukawa
  2719Ôćĺsuperpotential is written separately to make explicit the dependence
  2720Ôćĺon the Higgs doublets, which are needed for the heavy sector where
  2721Ôćĺ$m_i \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ and the mass cannot be attributed to
  2722Ôćĺchiral symmetry breaking.
  2723Ôćĺ
  2724ÔćĺThe two-doublet structure has a direct consequence for the Koide
  2725Ôćĺtriples. Since the quark Koide triples obligatorily mix up-type
  2726Ôćĺand down-type quarks (\S\ref{subsec:cbt}), the translation from
  2727Ôćĺmasses to Yukawa couplings depends on~$\tan\beta$.
  2728ÔćĺFor the $(c,b,t)$ triple, with $c$ and $t$ receiving mass
  2729Ôćĺfrom $H_u$ and $b$ from~$H_d$:
  2730Ôćĺ%
  2731Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2732Ôćĺ  Q(m_c, m_b, m_t)
  2733Ôćĺ  = Q(y_c\sin\beta,\; y_b\cos\beta,\; y_t\sin\beta)\,,
  2734Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2735Ôćĺ%
  2736Ôćĺwhich equals $Q(y_c, y_b, y_t)$ only when $\sin\beta = \cos\beta$,
  2737Ôćĺi.e., $\tan\beta = 1$. The same conclusion holds for the $(-s,c,b)$
  2738Ôćĺtriple, where $s$ and $b$ come from $H_d$ and $c$ from $H_u$.
  2739ÔćĺThus $\tan\beta = 1$ is the unique value at which the Koide condition
  2740Ôćĺon \emph{masses} is equivalent to a Koide condition on
  2741Ôćĺ\emph{Yukawa couplings} for mixed up/down-type triples.
  2742Ôćĺ
  2743ÔćĺThis prediction is independently motivated by the sBootstrap's
  2744Ôćĺ$SU(5)_f$ flavor symmetry, which treats all five quarks
  2745Ôćĺdemocratically.  A ratio $\tan\beta \neq 1$ would distinguish
  2746Ôćĺup-type from down-type quarks in the vacuum, breaking $SU(5)_f$
  2747Ôćĺat the electroweak scale in a way not present in the chiral sector.
  2748ÔćĺEqual Higgs VEVs, $v_u = v_d = v/\sqrt{2} = 174\GeV$,
  2749Ôćĺpreserve the democratic structure.
  2750ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$, the top Yukawa is
  2751Ôćĺ$y_t = \sqrt{2}\,m_t/v_u = 2m_t/v \approx 1.40 \approx \sqrt{2}$.
  2752Ôćĺ
  2753ÔćĺThe Koide condition on the $(c,b,t)$ triple constrains the diagonal
  2754ÔćĺYukawa matrix $Y = \operatorname{diag}(y_c,y_b,y_t)$.
  2755ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$, all Yukawas are
  2756Ôćĺ$y_i = 2m_i/v$ regardless of type, and the Koide relation reads
  2757Ôćĺ%
  2758Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2759Ôćĺ  \frac{y_c + y_b + y_t}
  2760Ôćĺ       {(\sqrt{y_c}+\sqrt{y_b}+\sqrt{y_t})^2}
  2761Ôćĺ  = \frac{2}{3}\,,
  2762Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2763Ôćĺ%
  2764Ôćĺwhich is equivalent to writing the square roots of the Yukawa
  2765Ôćĺcouplings as
  2766Ôćĺ%
  2767Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2768Ôćĺ  \sqrt{y_k} = \sqrt{y_0}\,
  2769Ôćĺ  \bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\,\cos(2\pi k/3 + \delta)\bigr)\,,
  2770Ôćĺ  \qquad k = 0,1,2\,,
  2771Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2772Ôćĺ%
  2773Ôćĺwhere $y_0 = \bigl(\sum\sqrt{y_k}\bigr)^2\!/9$ and $\delta$ is
  2774Ôćĺthe Koide phase. For the physical $(c,b,t)$ masses,
  2775Ôćĺ$\delta \approx 0.069$ and $Q = 0.6695$ (a 0.42\% deviation from
  2776Ôćĺ$2/3$). The traceless part of $\sqrt{Y}$ lies in the Cartan
  2777Ôćĺsubalgebra of $SU(3)_f$:
  2778Ôćĺ%
  2779Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2780Ôćĺ  \sqrt{Y} = \sqrt{y_0}\,
  2781Ôćĺ  \Bigl(I_3 + \tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{2}\,
  2782Ôćĺ  \hat n\cdot\boldsymbol\lambda_{\text{diag}}\Bigr)\,,
  2783Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2784Ôćĺ%
  2785Ôćĺwhere $\boldsymbol\lambda_{\text{diag}} = (\lambda_3, \lambda_8)$
  2786Ôćĺare the diagonal Gell-Mann matrices and
  2787Ôćĺ$\hat n = (\cos\theta,\,\sin\theta)$ is a unit vector
  2788Ôćĺin the Cartan plane.  An exact trigonometric identity gives
  2789Ôćĺ$\theta = \pi/6 - \delta$, so the Koide phase is literally
  2790Ôćĺthe angle of the Yukawa matrix in flavor space.
  2791ÔćĺThe Koide condition fixes the amplitude at $\sqrt{6}/2$; only
  2792Ôćĺ$\hat n$ (equivalently $\delta$) is free.
  2793ÔćĺThe bloom from seed to physical triple is a rigid rotation in
  2794Ôćĺthe $(\lambda_3,\lambda_8)$ plane by $-\Delta\delta$.
  2795Ôćĺ
  2796ÔćĺThe total Lagrangian is
  2797Ôćĺ%
  2798Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2799Ôćĺ  \mathcal{L}
  2800Ôćĺ  = \int\!d^4\theta\,K
  2801Ôćĺ  + \left[\int\!d^2\theta\,W + \text{h.c.}\right]
  2802Ôćĺ  + \mathcal{L}_D
  2803Ôćĺ  + \mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}\,,
  2804Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2805Ôćĺ%
  2806Ôćĺwith K\"ahler potential (canonical plus stabilization and bion corrections),
  2807Ôćĺ%
  2808Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2809Ôćĺ  K = \underbrace{\operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M) + |X|^2 + |B|^2
  2810Ôćĺ    + |\bar B|^2 + |H_u|^2 + |H_d|^2 + |S|^2}_{K_{\text{can}}}
  2811Ôćĺ  \underbrace{{}-\,\frac{|X|^4}{12\,\mu^2}}_{K_{\text{stab}}}
  2812Ôćĺ  + \underbrace{\frac{\zeta^2}{\Lambda^2}\,
  2813Ôćĺ    \Bigl|\sum_{k=1}^{N_c} s_k \sqrt{\hat m_k}\,\Bigr|^2}_{K_{\text{bion}}}\,,
  2814Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2815ÔćĺThe stabilization term $K_{\text{stab}} = -|X|^4/(12\mu^2)$ is the
  2816Ôćĺnegative quartic K\"ahler correction from \S\ref{subsec:superpotential}
  2817Ôćĺ(with $\Lambda_K = \mu$, $c = -1/12$); it pins the pseudo-modulus at
  2818Ôćĺ$\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ via the K\"ahler pole.
  2819ÔćĺThe monopole fugacity is $\zeta \propto e^{-S_0/(2N_c)}$,
  2820Ôćĺ$\hat m_k$ are the current quark masses (the couplings in
  2821Ôćĺ$W \ni \operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\,M)$), and
  2822Ôćĺ$s_k = \pm 1$ are monopole-instanton signs (encoding the seed-to-bloom
  2823Ôćĺphase). The bion term is non-holomorphic (containing $\sqrt{\hat m}$) and
  2824Ôćĺnon-perturbatively suppressed by $e^{-2S_0/3} \sim 10^{-2}$ at the
  2825Ôćĺconfinement scale. Its minimum enforces the $v_0$-doubling condition
  2826Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$
  2827Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:fterm});
  2828Ôćĺ%
  2829Ôćĺsuperpotential combining mass, constraint, and Yukawa terms,
  2830Ôćĺ%
  2831Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2832Ôćĺ  W = \underbrace{\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)}_{\text{chiral mass}}
  2833Ôćĺ    + \underbrace{X\!\left(\det M^{(3)} - B\bar B
  2834Ôćĺ      - \Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{6}\right)}_{\text{Seiberg constraint}}
  2835Ôćĺ    + \underbrace{c_3\,\frac{(\det M^{(3)})^3}
  2836Ôćĺ      {\Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{18}}}_{\text{3-instanton}}
  2837Ôćĺ    + \underbrace{y_c\, H_u\, M^c{}_c
  2838Ôćĺ      + y_b\, H_d\, M^b{}_b
  2839Ôćĺ      + y_t\, H_u\, Q^t \bar Q_t\phantom{X}}_{\text{Yukawa}}
  2840Ôćĺ    + \underbrace{\lambda_S\, S\, H_u\!\cdot\!H_d
  2841Ôćĺ      + \tfrac{\kappa}{3}\,S^3}_{\text{NMSSM}}\,,
  2842Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2843Ôćĺ%
  2844Ôćĺwhere $M^{(3)}$ is the $3\times 3$ light-flavor block ($u,d,s$)
  2845Ôćĺand $\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$ is the dynamical scale of the confined
  2846Ôćĺ$N_f = N_c = 3$ theory (including threshold matching at $m_c$
  2847Ôćĺand $m_b$).  The Yukawa terms are written in the full five-flavor
  2848Ôćĺtheory above the heavy-quark thresholds, where $M^c{}_c = Q^c\bar Q_c$
  2849Ôćĺand $M^b{}_b = Q^b\bar Q_b$ are elementary bilinears;
  2850Ôćĺbelow threshold, they reduce to the matching conditions that generate
  2851Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{eff}}$ and the Seiberg constraint.
  2852ÔćĺThe top Yukawa $y_t\,H_u\,Q^t\bar Q_t$ stands apart because the
  2853Ôćĺtop does not confine into a meson.
  2854ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$, the tree-level lightest Higgs mass vanishes
  2855Ôćĺ($m_h = m_Z|\cos 2\beta| = 0$); the NMSSM coupling
  2856Ôćĺ$\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$ lifts the flat direction and gives
  2857Ôćĺ$m_h = \lambda_S v/\sqrt{2} = 125\GeV$ for $\lambda_S = 0.72$.
  2858ÔćĺThe singlet~$S$ is a \emph{separate} elementary field, distinct
  2859Ôćĺfrom the Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$
  2860Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:higgs_open}).
  2861ÔćĺThe constraint $\tan\beta = 1$ ($v_u = v_d = v/\sqrt{2}$) is imposed
  2862Ôćĺby the Koide condition on mixed triples
  2863Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}).
  2864ÔćĺThe $D$-term Lagrangian includes an optional Fayet--Iliopoulos
  2865Ôćĺparameter $\xi$ for $U(1)_{\text{em}}$:
  2866Ôćĺ%
  2867Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2868Ôćĺ  \mathcal{L}_D
  2869Ôćĺ  = -\frac{e^2}{2}\!\left(\sum_i Q_i\,|\phi_i|^2
  2870Ôćĺ    + \xi\right)^{\!2}.
  2871Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2872Ôćĺ%
  2873ÔćĺFinally, soft SUSY-breaking terms arise from a spurion
  2874Ôćĺ$\langle F_S\rangle = \fpi^2$:
  2875Ôćĺ%
  2876Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  2877Ôćĺ  \mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}
  2878Ôćĺ  = -\tilde m^2 \operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M)
  2879Ôćĺ  - \bigl(B_\mu\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\, M)
  2880Ôćĺ    + A_y\bigl(y_c\, H_u\, M^c{}_c + y_b\, H_d\, M^b{}_b\bigr) + \text{h.c.}\bigr)\,,
  2881Ôćĺ  \qquad
  2882Ôćĺ  \tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2\,.
  2883Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  2884Ôćĺ%
  2885ÔćĺThe soft scalar mass $\tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2 \approx (92\MeV)^2$
  2886Ôćĺgenerates the universal meson--lepton splitting
  2887Ôćĺ$m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = \fpi^2$.
  2888Ôćĺ
  2889Ôćĺ\paragraph{Scope of the Lagrangian.}
  2890ÔćĺThis Lagrangian produces: (i)~the seed Koide condition ($gv/m = \sqrt{3}$
  2891Ôćĺin the O'Raifeartaigh limit); (ii)~the meson--lepton mass splitting
  2892Ôćĺ($\fpi^2$); (iii)~the $v_0$-doubling condition via the bion K\"ahler
  2893Ôćĺcorrection $K_{\text{bion}}$, whose minimum enforces
  2894Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$; (iv)~the Yukawa couplings for
  2895Ôćĺthe heavy sector; (v)~the $\cos(9\delta)$ potential that selects
  2896Ôćĺthe lepton Koide phase, via the three-instanton operator
  2897Ôćĺ$(\det M^{(3)})^3/\Lambda_{\text{eff}}^{18}$. The bloom is
  2898Ôćĺnon-holomorphic and lives in the K\"ahler sector: the bion term
  2899Ôćĺ$|\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ is the leading monopole-instanton
  2900Ôćĺcontribution on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$
  2901Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:fterm}).
  2902Ôćĺ
  2903Ôćĺ\paragraph{Flavour-universal Yukawa identity.}
  2904ÔćĺAt the seesaw vacuum $M_j = C/m_j$, the product of the Yukawa
  2905Ôćĺcoupling times the meson VEV is flavour-independent:
  2906Ôćĺ\[
  2907Ôćĺ  y_j\,\langle M_j\rangle
  2908Ôćĺ  = \frac{m_j}{v_{\text{type}}}\,\frac{C}{m_j}
  2909Ôćĺ  = \frac{C}{v_{\text{type}}}
  2910Ôćĺ  = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,C}{v}
  2911Ôćĺ  = 5.07\;\MeV\,,
  2912Ôćĺ\]
  2913Ôćĺwhere $C = \Lambda^2(m_u m_d m_s)^{1/3}$.
  2914ÔćĺThe quark mass cancels exactly: heavy quarks couple weakly
  2915Ôćĺ(small~$y_j$) but have large meson VEVs (large~$M_j$), and
  2916Ôćĺvice versa.
  2917ÔćĺThis identity ensures that the Higgs $F$-terms from the meson
  2918Ôćĺsector are flavour-blind, automatically satisfying the
  2919ÔćĺGlashow--Weinberg condition for the absence of tree-level
  2920ÔćĺHiggs-mediated FCNCs.
  2921Ôćĺ
  2922Ôćĺ\paragraph{Three Yukawa sectors.}
  2923ÔćĺThe six quarks divide into three sectors with distinct coupling
  2924Ôćĺstructures:
  2925Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  2926Ôćĺ\small
  2927Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{llll}
  2928Ôćĺ\toprule
  2929ÔćĺSector & Quarks & Mechanism & $y$ range \\
  2930Ôćĺ\midrule
  2931ÔćĺConfined & $u,d,s$ & $\operatorname{Tr}(\hat m\,M)$: mass
  2932Ôćĺ  term is Yukawa & $10^{-5}$--$5\times 10^{-4}$ \\
  2933ÔćĺIntermediate & $c,b$ & Explicit $y_j H M^j{}_j$ & $7\times 10^{-3}$--$2.4\times 10^{-2}$ \\
  2934ÔćĺElementary & $t$ & $y_t H_u Q^t\bar Q_t$ & $0.99 \approx 1$ \\
  2935Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  2936Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  2937Ôćĺ\end{center}
  2938ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$, both Yukawa matrices
  2939Ôćĺ$Y^u = \operatorname{diag}(y_u,y_c,y_t)$ and
  2940Ôćĺ$Y^d = \operatorname{diag}(y_d,y_s,y_b)$ are diagonal at
  2941Ôćĺtree level; CKM mixing must originate in the UV quark mass
  2942Ôćĺtexture rather than in confined-phase meson dynamics
  2943Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:ckm_open}).
  2944Ôćĺ
  2945Ôćĺ\paragraph{Particle spectrum and supertrace.}
  2946ÔćĺThe Lagrangian defines a $16\times 16$ fermion mass matrix and a
  2947Ôćĺ$32\times 32$ scalar mass-squared matrix at the Seiberg vacuum.
  2948ÔćĺThe fermion spectrum spans twenty orders of magnitude, from the
  2949Ôćĺultra-heavy $X$--$M^s{}_s$ pair at $\sim\Lambda^6/(M_u M_d)
  2950Ôćĺ\sim 8\times 10^{10}\MeV$ down to baryonic fermions at
  2951Ôćĺ$\sim|X_0| \sim 10^{-9}\MeV$.  The off-diagonal meson fermion
  2952Ôćĺmasses follow the inverted seesaw hierarchy $|X_0|\langle M_k\rangle
  2953Ôćĺ\propto 1/m_k$, transmitting the quark mass ratios to the
  2954Ôćĺconfined spectrum.  The supertrace is
  2955Ôćĺ$\text{STr}[M^2] = 2\operatorname{Tr}(M^2_{\text{soft}})
  2956Ôćĺ= 18\,\fpi^2 = 152{,}352\MeV^2$:
  2957Ôćĺthe $W_{IJ}^2$ piece cancels between scalar and fermion sectors,
  2958Ôćĺand the holomorphic third-derivative piece cancels between real
  2959Ôćĺand imaginary scalar components, leaving only the nine
  2960Ôćĺsoft meson masses.
  2961ÔćĺDecomposed by electromagnetic charge, the supertrace
  2962Ôćĺbecomes $\text{STr}_Q[M^2] = 2\,n_Q\,\fpi^2$ where $n_Q$
  2963Ôćĺcounts meson fields with charge~$Q$: $n_0 = 5$, $n_{\pm 1} = 2$.
  2964ÔćĺThe EM-weighted sum $\sum Q_{\text{em}}^2\,\text{STr}_Q = 8\,\fpi^2$
  2965Ôćĺis nonzero because only mesons carry soft mass; this is the
  2966Ôćĺelectromagnetic trace that connects to the FI
  2967Ôćĺparameter~(\S\ref{subsec:dterm}).
  2968ÔćĺThe Yukawa-induced $F$-terms ($F_{M^d{}_d} = y_c v/\sqrt{2}$,
  2969Ôćĺ$F_{M^s{}_s} = y_b v/\sqrt{2}$) are nonzero at the seesaw
  2970Ôćĺvacuum but do not couple to the off-diagonal meson directions:
  2971Ôćĺ$\partial F_{M^d{}_d}/\partial M^u{}_s\big|_{\text{diag}} = 0$
  2972Ôćĺbecause the off-diagonal cofactors vanish at a diagonal matrix.
  2973ÔćĺAll six off-diagonal meson masses are positive
  2974Ôćĺ($m^2 \approx 2\fpi^2 = 16{,}928\;\MeV^2$),
  2975Ôćĺand one-loop Coleman--Weinberg corrections are stabilising
  2976Ôćĺ($\delta m^2_{\text{CW}} \sim 10^{-9}\,m^2_{\text{tree}}$).
  2977Ôćĺ
  2978Ôćĺ\paragraph{Mass spectrum from the Lagrangian.}
  2979ÔćĺThe mass of each particle is determined by a specific term:
  2980Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  2981Ôćĺ\small
  2982Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{lll}
  2983Ôćĺ\toprule
  2984ÔćĺMass & Origin & Equation \\
  2985Ôćĺ\midrule
  2986Ôćĺ$m_{\text{meson}}^2 - m_{\text{lepton}}^2 = \fpi^2$
  2987Ôćĺ  & $\mathcal{L}_{\text{soft}}$: $\tilde m^2 \sim \fpi^2$ & Universal splitting \\
  2988Ôćĺ$m_c/m_s = (2+\sqrt{3})^2$
  2989Ôćĺ  & $W$: O'Raifeartaigh at $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ & Seed Koide \\
  2990Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$
  2991Ôćĺ  & $K_{\text{bion}}$: minimum of $|S_{\text{bloom}} - 2S_{\text{seed}}|^2$
  2992Ôćĺ  & $v_0$-doubling \\
  2993Ôćĺ$m_t = y_t v_u/\sqrt{2}$, $\tan\beta = 1$
  2994Ôćĺ  & $W_{\text{Yukawa}}$: $y_t H_u Q^t\bar Q_t$ & External \\
  2995Ôćĺ$\delta m^2 = Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi$
  2996Ôćĺ  & $\mathcal{L}_D$: FI term & EM splitting \\
  2997Ôćĺ$\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$
  2998Ôćĺ  & $W$: $(\det M^{(3)})^3/\Lambda^{18}$ & 3-instanton \\
  2999Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  3000Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  3001Ôćĺ\end{center}
  3002ÔćĺThe first four rows account for six quark masses ($m_u$ and $m_d$ free,
  3003Ôćĺ$m_s$ sets the scale, $m_c$ from seed, $m_b$ from K\"ahler, $m_t$
  3004Ôćĺexternal). The meson--lepton splitting is universal across the
  3005Ôćĺmultiplet, and the $D$-term provides the subdominant electromagnetic
  3006Ôćĺcorrection.
  3007Ôćĺ
  3008ÔćĺThe parameters of this construction separate into those fixed by
  3009Ôćĺstructure and those that remain free.
  3010Ôćĺ
  3011Ôćĺ\paragraph{Fixed by the Koide seed.}
  3012ÔćĺThe O'Raifeartaigh condition $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ translates, in the
  3013ÔćĺISS magnetic dual, into $\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ for the
  3014Ôćĺpseudo-modulus.
  3015ÔćĺThis fixes the mass ratio $m_c/m_s = (2+\sqrt{3})^2$
  3016Ôćĺwithin the seed triple, predicting $m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$ from $m_s$ alone
  3017Ôćĺ(PDG: $1{,}270\MeV$, 2.4\% deviation).
  3018Ôćĺ
  3019Ôćĺ\paragraph{Fixed by the vacuum-charge doubling.}
  3020ÔćĺThe bloom from seed to full triple doubles $v_0$, giving
  3021Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ and predicting
  3022Ôćĺ$m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$ (0.1$\sigma$ from PDG). This constraint
  3023Ôćĺis \emph{non-holomorphic}: it is a linear relation among square
  3024Ôćĺroots of masses, while the superpotential is holomorphic in meson
  3025Ôćĺfields whose VEVs scale as $1/m_i$. The map
  3026Ôćĺ$\zch_k = \sqrt{m_k} \sim 1/\!\sqrt{\langle M^k{}_k\rangle}$
  3027Ôćĺis non-holomorphic. The $v_0$-doubling must therefore arise from the
  3028ÔćĺK\"ahler potential, nonperturbative effects, or emergent dynamics---it
  3029Ôćĺis not a tree-level superpotential term.
  3030Ôćĺ
  3031Ôćĺ\paragraph{Fixed by SUSY breaking.}
  3032ÔćĺThe supertrace relation $m^2_\pi - m^2_\mu = \fpi^2$ fixes the
  3033Ôćĺsplitting scale. At the Seiberg vacuum, $F_X = 0$ (SUSY is
  3034Ôćĺunbroken in the confined sector), but the Yukawa couplings
  3035Ôćĺproduce $F_{H_u} = y_c M^c{}_c$ and $F_{H_d} = y_b M^b{}_b$.
  3036ÔćĺBy the seesaw $y_j M_j = (m_j/v)(C/m_j) = C/v$, these
  3037ÔćĺF-terms are flavour-universal:
  3038Ôćĺ$F_{H_u} = F_{H_d} = C/v_{\text{EW}} = 290\MeV$.
  3039ÔćĺThe identification $\langle F\rangle \sim \fpi^2$
  3040Ôćĺlinks $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ to $m_s$ via the GOR relation.
  3041Ôćĺ
  3042Ôćĺ\paragraph{$D$-term effects on the Koide condition.}
  3043ÔćĺIn $\mathcal{N}=1$ SUSY, $D$-terms shift scalar masses but leave
  3044Ôćĺfermion masses untouched. The lepton Koide triples, being conditions
  3045Ôćĺon \emph{fermion} masses, are naturally protected against
  3046Ôćĺ$D$-term perturbations. The meson Koide triple
  3047Ôćĺ$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ involves scalar masses and \emph{is} in principle
  3048Ôćĺaffected by $D$-terms: a FI parameter $\xi$ shifts $m^2 \to m^2 + \xi Q_{\text{em}}^2$.
  3049ÔćĺSince all three mesons carry unit charge, each $m^2$ shifts by the
  3050Ôćĺsame $\xi$, and the relative correction to $Q$ is of order
  3051Ôćĺ$\xi/(4m_\pi^2) \sim 0.1\%$ for $\xi \approx -80\MeV^2$.
  3052ÔćĺNumerically, the $D$-term shifts the meson $Q$ by $0.03\%$,
  3053Ôćĺwell below the $0.10\%$ precision of the triple.
  3054Ôćĺ
  3055Ôćĺ\paragraph{Remaining free parameters.}
  3056ÔćĺAfter the seed Koide fixes $m_c/m_s$, the $v_0$-doubling fixes
  3057Ôćĺ$m_b$, and $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$ is imposed, only two to three free
  3058Ôćĺparameters remain:
  3059Ôćĺ\begin{itemize}
  3060Ôćĺ  \item $m_u$, $m_d$: unconstrained by any Koide triple; connected
  3061Ôćĺ    to the Cabibbo angle through the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation
  3062Ôćĺ    $|V_{us}| \approx \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ (giving $V_{us} = 0.224$,
  3063Ôćĺ    PDG: $0.2243$);
  3064Ôćĺ  \item $m_s$ (or equivalently $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$): the overall
  3065Ôćĺ    scale of the light sector. The lepton Koide scale
  3066Ôćĺ    $M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$ (\S\ref{sec:seeds}) ties the lepton
  3067Ôćĺ    scale to $\fpi$ and hence to $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$.
  3068Ôćĺ\end{itemize}
  3069ÔćĺThe top mass $m_t$ is external---it does not participate in the
  3070Ôćĺconfined meson matrix---but if $Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ is imposed as a
  3071Ôćĺconstraint rather than treated as a test, $m_t$ is predicted from
  3072Ôćĺ$m_s$ alone. The Yukawa couplings
  3073Ôćĺ$y_c = \sqrt{2}\,m_c/v_{\text{EW}}$,
  3074Ôćĺ$y_b = \sqrt{2}\,m_b/v_{\text{EW}}$ are fixed by the predicted masses.
  3075Ôćĺ
  3076ÔćĺThe $(c,b,t)$ Koide triple provides an \emph{overdetermination test}.
  3077ÔćĺUsing the self-consistent predictions
  3078Ôćĺ$m_c^{\text{seed}} = 1{,}301\MeV$ and
  3079Ôćĺ$m_b^{v_0} = 4{,}233\MeV$ (from the seed and $v_0$-doubling
  3080Ôćĺwith input $m_s$ only), together with the external $m_t$:
  3081Ôćĺ$Q(c,b,t) = 0.668$, a $0.14\%$ deviation from~$2/3$---closer
  3082Ôćĺthan the $0.42\%$ obtained with PDG masses.
  3083ÔćĺThe self-consistent predictions satisfy the Koide conditions
  3084Ôćĺbetter than the measured values.
  3085ÔćĺIf instead both overlapping triples are required to
  3086Ôćĺsatisfy $Q = 2/3$ exactly, the overlap system determines
  3087Ôćĺ$m_b^{\text{pred}} = 4{,}159\MeV$ (0.5\% from PDG) and
  3088Ôćĺ$m_c^{\text{pred}} = 1{,}369\MeV$ (7.8\% from PDG). The bion
  3089ÔćĺK\"ahler prediction $m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$ ($0.07\%$, $0.06\sigma$)
  3090Ôćĺis more precise, suggesting that $v_0$-doubling rather than exact
  3091Ôćĺ$Q = 2/3$ on both triples is the tighter constraint.
  3092Ôćĺ
  3093ÔćĺConversely, imposing $Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ exactly on the self-consistent
  3094Ôćĺ$m_c^{\text{seed}}$ and $m_b^{v_0}$ predicts
  3095Ôćĺ$m_t = 171.3 \pm 1.5\GeV$ (where the uncertainty comes from
  3096Ôćĺ$\delta m_s = 0.8\MeV$), within $0.9\%$ of the PDG pole mass
  3097Ôćĺ$m_t = 172.76 \pm 0.30\GeV$.  The entire third-generation mass
  3098Ôćĺspectrum then follows from a single input~$m_s$; using
  3099Ôćĺ$m_s = 94.2\MeV$ (within $1\sigma$ of PDG) gives $m_t$ exactly.
  3100Ôćĺ
  3101Ôćĺ\paragraph{Summary of predictions.}
  3102ÔćĺTable~\ref{tab:predictions} collects the quantitative predictions.
  3103Ôćĺ\begin{table}[ht]
  3104Ôćĺ\centering
  3105Ôćĺ\small
  3106Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{llll}
  3107Ôćĺ\toprule
  3108ÔćĺQuantity & Predicted & PDG 2024 & Source \\
  3109Ôćĺ\midrule
  3110Ôćĺ$m_c$ & $1{,}301\MeV$ & $1{,}270 \pm 20$ ($1.6\sigma$)
  3111Ôćĺ  & Seed: $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ \\
  3112Ôćĺ$m_b$ & $4{,}177\MeV$ & $4{,}180 \pm 30$ ($0.1\sigma$)
  3113Ôćĺ  & Bion K\"ahler \\
  3114Ôćĺ$m_t$ & $171.3 \pm 1.5\GeV$ & $172.76 \pm 0.30$
  3115Ôćĺ  & $Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ \\
  3116Ôćĺ$\sin^2\theta_W$ & $0.22310$ & $0.22320 \pm 0.00026$ ($0.4\sigma$)
  3117Ôćĺ  & Casimir equation \\
  3118Ôćĺ$V_{us}$ & $0.224$ & $0.2243 \pm 0.0008$ ($0.9\sigma$)
  3119Ôćĺ  & GST relation \\
  3120Ôćĺ$M_0$ (lepton) & $\fpi^2/27 = 314.0\MeV$ & $313.8\MeV$ (fitted)
  3121Ôćĺ  & Scale relation \\
  3122Ôćĺ$m_h$ & $125.4\GeV$ & $125.25 \pm 0.17$ ($0.6\sigma$)
  3123Ôćĺ  & NMSSM: $\lambda v/\sqrt{2}$ \\
  3124Ôćĺ$\tan\beta$ & $1$ & --- (not directly measured)
  3125Ôćĺ  & Mixed-triple Koide \\
  3126Ôćĺ$\text{STr}[M^2]$ & $18\,\fpi^2$ & --- (not directly measured)
  3127Ôćĺ  & Soft mass only \\
  3128Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  3129Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  3130Ôćĺ\caption{Predictions from the sBootstrap Lagrangian.
  3131ÔćĺThe quark masses use $m_s = 93.4\MeV$ as input;
  3132Ôćĺthe bion $m_b$ uses PDG $m_c$.
  3133ÔćĺThe Casimir and GST predictions are independent
  3134Ôćĺof~$m_s$.}
  3135Ôćĺ\label{tab:predictions}
  3136Ôćĺ\end{table}
  3137Ôćĺ
  3138Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3139Ôćĺ\section{The Electron Mass and the Lepton Seed}
  3140Ôćĺ\label{sec:electron}
  3141Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3142Ôćĺ
  3143ÔćĺThe charged lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the ``almost-seed'': 
  3144Ôćĺ$\zch_e = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}$ is small but nonzero. The electron's 
  3145Ôćĺcharge is near zero because of cancellation in $\zch_e = z_0 + z_e$:
  3146Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  3147Ôćĺv_0 = 17.716, \quad z_e = -17.001, \quad 
  3148Ôćĺ\zch_e = 17.716 - 17.001 = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}\,.
  3149Ôćĺ\end{equation}

content.text: Now let me read the open problems section and conclusions.

content.content:
3209Ôćĺ\section{Open Problems}
  3210Ôćĺ\label{sec:open}
  3211Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3212Ôćĺ
  3213Ôćĺ\subsection{The diquark sector}
  3214ÔćĺThe Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) proves that no $J=0$,
  3215Ôćĺcolor-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor.  The argument
  3216Ôćĺextends to all partial waves: $J = 0$ forces $L = S$, so the
  3217Ôćĺspatial--spin exchange sign is $(-1)^{L+S} = (-1)^{2S} = +1$,
  3218Ôćĺwhich combined with the antisymmetric color $\bar{3}$ gives
  3219Ôćĺoverall $+1$; Pauli then requires antisymmetric flavor
  3220Ôćĺ($\overline{\mathbf{10}}$).  The $\mathbf{15}$ is accessible
  3221Ôćĺonly for $J \geq 1$.  In SQCD, the same conclusion holds
  3222Ôćĺalgebraically: the $\varepsilon$-tensor on color indices in
  3223Ôćĺ$B^{abc} = \varepsilon_{ijk}Q^{ia}Q^{jb}Q^{kc}$ forces
  3224Ôćĺantisymmetric flavor regardless of statistics.
  3225ÔćĺThe sBootstrap's $\mathbf{15}$ diquark must therefore be non-composite:
  3226Ôćĺeither an algebraic image of the Miyazawa supercharge
  3227Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:baryon_open}) or a fundamental $SO(32)$ string state.
  3228ÔćĺHowever, the mixed anticommutator
  3229Ôćĺ$\{Q_\alpha, \mathcal{Q}_{[ij],\beta}\} = \epsilon_{\alpha\beta}\,
  3230Ôćĺ\mathcal{S}_{[ij]}$ produces a diquark generator in
  3231Ôćĺ$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ (antisymmetric), not the symmetric
  3232Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15}$; thus the Miyazawa route alone does not resolve the
  3233Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15}$~vs.~$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ conflict.
  3234ÔćĺThe exotic $+4/3$-charged
  3235Ôćĺdiquarks of the $\mathbf{15}$ would be a testable consequence:
  3236Ôćĺif their mass is set by the SUSY-breaking scale ($\sim f_\pi$), they
  3237Ôćĺwould be sub-GeV and accessible at fixed-target experiments; if by
  3238Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ or higher, LHC pair-production searches for
  3239Ôćĺexotic charges apply. No mass prediction can be made without a
  3240Ôćĺconcrete realization of the $\mathbf{15}$.
  3241Ôćĺ
  3242Ôćĺ\subsection{What determines the Koide phase?}
  3243ÔćĺThe energy balance constrains but does not uniquely fix $\delta$.
  3244ÔćĺThe O'Raifeartaigh realization (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}) fixes the
  3245Ôćĺseed ratio $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ but leaves $\delta$ undetermined---the
  3246Ôćĺphase controls how the seed blooms into a full triple. The bloom
  3247Ôćĺis a phase rotation $\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ at
  3248Ôćĺfixed $v_0$, but explicit R-breaking perturbations push $Q$ away
  3249Ôćĺfrom $2/3$ at any nonzero strength, making the seed an unstable
  3250Ôćĺfixed point. The bloom must therefore be nonperturbative.
  3251Ôćĺ
  3252ÔćĺThe lepton phase satisfies $\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ to
  3253Ôćĺ$33\,\text{ppm}$ ($0.9\sigma$ in $m_\tau$; $3.1\sigma$ after
  3254Ôćĺlook-elsewhere over fractions with denominator $\leq 20$). Since $2/9$
  3255Ôćĺis not a rational multiple of $\pi$, this is not a geometric angle.
  3256ÔćĺIt is, however, $2/9 = 2/(3\times 3)$, which points to a $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3257Ôćĺdiscrete symmetry in the Koide modulus space. The $\mathbb{Z}_3$
  3258Ôćĺmass-permutation symmetry ($\delta \to \delta + 2\pi/3$) is the
  3259Ôćĺunique subgroup; the quotient $\mathbb{Z}_9/\mathbb{Z}_3$ has three
  3260Ôćĺcosets, producing three inequivalent mass spectra from the same $M_0$.
  3261ÔćĺThe simplest potential with $\mathbb{Z}_9$ symmetry,
  3262Ôćĺ$V \supset -\cos\bigl(9(\delta - 2/9)\bigr)$, has the lepton value
  3263Ôćĺas one of its nine minima, with the $\mathbb{Z}_3$-equivalent minima
  3264Ôćĺat $\delta = 2/9 + 2k\pi/3$.
  3265Ôćĺ
  3266ÔćĺA dynamical origin for $\mathbb{Z}_9$ exists in SQCD: for $N_f = N_c = 3$,
  3267Ôćĺthe diagonal $U(1)_{R+2A}$ (R-symmetry plus twice the axial symmetry)
  3268Ôćĺhas anomaly coefficient $6N_c = 18 = 2 \times 9$. Instantons break
  3269Ôćĺ$U(1)_{R+2A} \to \mathbb{Z}_{18}$, which contains $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3270Ôćĺas a subgroup. The meson field $M$ carries $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge 4
  3271Ôćĺ(from two quark zero modes at charge 2 each), so $\det M$ carries
  3272Ôćĺcharge~12.  The lowest-order holomorphic $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$-singlet
  3273Ôćĺbeyond the Seiberg constraint is the three-instanton operator
  3274Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  3275Ôćĺ  \delta W_{3\text{-inst}}
  3276Ôćĺ    = c_3\,\frac{(\det M)^3}{\Lambda^{18}}\,,
  3277Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  3278Ôćĺsince $(\det M)^3$ has $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge $36 = 2\times 18$.
  3279ÔćĺNear the SUSY vacuum $\det M = \Lambda^6$, parametrizing
  3280Ôćĺ$\det M = \Lambda^6\,e^{3i\delta_M}$ yields
  3281Ôćĺ$\delta W \propto e^{9i\delta}$, and the cross-term with the
  3282Ôćĺtree-level superpotential gives
  3283Ôćĺ$V_{\text{eff}} \supset -V_0\cos\bigl(9(\delta - \delta_0)\bigr)$
  3284Ôćĺwith $\delta_0 = \arg(c_3)/9$.
  3285ÔćĺThis is exactly the $\cos(9\delta)$ potential identified above.
  3286ÔćĺThe one-instanton vertex ($\det M$, charge 12) is already captured
  3287Ôćĺby the Seiberg constraint; the two-instanton vertex ($(\det M)^2$,
  3288Ôćĺcharge 24, not a $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ singlet) is forbidden.
  3289ÔćĺThe three-instanton correction does not affect the meson mass
  3290Ôćĺspectrum: the $F$-term equations define an effective Lagrange
  3291Ôćĺmultiplier $X_{\text{eff}} = X + 3c_3\Lambda^{12}/\Lambda^{18}$,
  3292Ôćĺbut the constraint $\det M = \Lambda^6$ and the meson VEVs
  3293Ôćĺ$M_j = C/m_j$ are unmodified. The correction acts only on the
  3294Ôćĺphase structure (through $\cos(9\delta)$), not on the magnitudes.
  3295Ôćĺ
  3296ÔćĺAt strong coupling ($\Lambda \sim 300\MeV$), the coefficient
  3297Ôćĺ$c_3$ is $O(1)$ and the potential strength is
  3298Ôćĺ$V_0^{1/4} \sim 65\text{--}85\MeV$, comparable to
  3299Ôćĺ$f_\pi = 92\MeV$---this is not a parametrically suppressed effect.
  3300ÔćĺThe same $\cos(9\delta)$ structure also arises from magnetic
  3301Ôćĺbion contributions on $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, where the
  3302Ôćĺ$\mathbb{Z}_3$-symmetric combination of the SU(3) affine roots
  3303Ôćĺmaps to $\cos(9\delta)$ in the meson phase variable.
  3304Ôćĺ
  3305ÔćĺThe embedding is specific to $N_c = 3$: the condition
  3306Ôćĺ$N_c^2 \mid 6N_c$ requires $N_c \mid 6$, which holds only for
  3307Ôćĺ$N_c = 1, 2, 3, 6$.
  3308Ôćĺ
  3309ÔćĺThe quark Koide phases, by contrast, do \emph{not} sit at
  3310Ôćĺ$\mathbb{Z}_9$ minima: the $(-s,c,b)$ and $(c,b,t)$ phases
  3311Ôćĺare displaced by $39\%$ and $22\%$ of the $\mathbb{Z}_9$
  3312Ôćĺspacing, respectively.  This suggests that the lepton and quark
  3313Ôćĺphases are selected by different dynamical mechanisms---the
  3314Ôćĺinstanton potential for leptons, and possibly the bloom mechanism
  3315Ôćĺor ISS dynamics for quarks.
  3316Ôćĺ
  3317ÔćĺThe Coleman--Weinberg potential of the minimal O'Raifeartaigh model
  3318Ôćĺis flat in $\delta$ at one loop---the pseudo-modulus potential depends
  3319Ôćĺonly on the modulus $|\Phi_0|$, not on the Koide phase.  The
  3320Ôćĺthree-instanton potential above provides the leading
  3321Ôćĺ$\delta$-dependent term that lifts this flat direction.
  3322ÔćĺThe \emph{modulus} direction $|\Phi_0|$ is a separate question:
  3323Ôćĺthe CW potential stabilizes $|\langle\Phi_0\rangle|$ at zero,
  3324Ôćĺbut the Koide seed requires $\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$
  3325Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}).
  3326ÔćĺA negative quartic K\"ahler correction $c\,|X|^4/\Lambda_K^2$
  3327Ôćĺwith $c = -\Lambda_K^2/(12\mu^2)$ pins the pseudo-modulus at
  3328Ôćĺthe K\"ahler pole $v_{\text{pole}} = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ exactly
  3329Ôćĺ(an algebraic result, not numerical tuning;
  3330Ôćĺsee \S\ref{subsec:superpotential}).
  3331ÔćĺThis coefficient is \emph{not} radiatively generated: the
  3332Ôćĺone-loop CW quartic in the O'Raifeartaigh model has $c_4 = -20/3$
  3333Ôćĺat $gf/m^2 = 1$ (exact), which is orders of magnitude too large;
  3334Ôćĺthe ISS one-loop potential produces a positive quartic ($c_{\text{eff}} = +0.014$),
  3335Ôćĺthe wrong sign entirely. The K\"ahler pole mechanism is therefore a
  3336Ôćĺtree-level statement about the effective K\"ahler potential,
  3337Ôćĺnot a consequence of one-loop dynamics.  However, magnetic bions
  3338Ôćĺon $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ generate a quartic K\"ahler correction
  3339Ôćĺof the correct sign.
  3340ÔćĺThe bion-induced coefficient
  3341Ôćĺ$c_{\text{bion}} = -3\pi^2\,e^{-4\pi/(N_c^2\alpha_s)}\,M^2/\alpha_s^2$
  3342Ôćĺ(where $M^2 = [\prod_f(m_f/\Lambda)]^2$ is the zero-mode mass factor)
  3343Ôćĺequals $-1/12$ at $\alpha_s = 0.143$ ($S_{\text{bion}} = 9.76$)
  3344Ôćĺin the ISS model with heavy magnetic quarks ($M^2 = 1$).
  3345ÔćĺWith physical light-quark masses, zero-mode suppression reduces
  3346Ôćĺ$|c_{\text{bion}}|$ by five orders of magnitude; the ISS magnetic
  3347Ôćĺdual---where the pseudo-modulus lives---is the appropriate
  3348Ôćĺsetting for the bion calculation.
  3349ÔćĺThe three-instanton operator $(\det M)^3$ shifts the Seiberg
  3350ÔćĺLagrange multiplier~$X$ (dimension $[\text{mass}]^{-3}$ in the
  3351Ôćĺconfining phase) but this is a different object from the ISS
  3352Ôćĺpseudo-modulus (dimension $[\text{mass}]^{+1}$ in the magnetic
  3353Ôćĺdual).
  3354Ôćĺ
  3355ÔćĺMore broadly, the ISS Coleman--Weinberg potential does \emph{not}
  3356Ôćĺtransmit the Koide condition from the UV quark masses to the IR
  3357Ôćĺmeson spectrum.  In the $N_f = 4$ ISS model, the one-loop CW
  3358Ôćĺmasses for the broken mesons $\chi_a$ are nearly degenerate
  3359Ôćĺ($m_{\text{CW}} \approx 27\MeV$ for all four flavors), because
  3360Ôćĺthe CW mass depends on $m_a$ through the affine map
  3361Ôćĺ$F_a = h\mu^2 - m_a$, and all quark masses satisfy
  3362Ôćĺ$m_a \ll h\mu^2$.  The resulting spectrum gives
  3363Ôćĺ$Q \approx 1/3$ (the degenerate limit), regardless of the UV
  3364Ôćĺvalue.  This confirms that flavor-sensitive mass structure must
  3365Ôćĺarise from non-perturbative effects---the bion K\"ahler
  3366Ôćĺcorrection (\S\ref{subsec:fterm}), which directly involves
  3367Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_k}$, rather than the one-loop effective potential.
  3368Ôćĺ
  3369Ôćĺ\subsection{Connecting the three tuples}
  3370ÔćĺThe unified superpotential (\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}) connects
  3371Ôćĺthe light and heavy sectors through a layered structure:
  3372Ôćĺthe seed Koide fixes $m_c/m_s$ (from $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$),
  3373Ôćĺthe $v_0$-doubling fixes $m_b$ (from the K\"ahler sector),
  3374Ôćĺand the $(c,b,t)$ Koide serves as an overdetermination test.
  3375ÔćĺAfter these constraints, only two to three free parameters remain:
  3376Ôćĺ$m_u$, $m_d$ (connected to the Cabibbo angle), and the overall
  3377Ôćĺscale $m_s$.  The Koide condition does \emph{not} extend to triples
  3378Ôćĺcontaining $m_u$ or $m_d$: $Q(m_u,m_d,m_s) = 0.567$ ($-15\%$)
  3379Ôćĺand $Q(m_u,m_c,m_t) = 0.849$ ($+27\%$), confirming that the
  3380Ôćĺlightest quarks are free parameters of the framework. The $v_0$-doubling is non-holomorphic and lives in the
  3381ÔćĺK\"ahler sector: as shown in \S\ref{subsec:fterm}, the bion
  3382ÔćĺK\"ahler correction $K_{\text{bion}} \sim |\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$
  3383Ôćĺgenerates an effective potential whose minimum enforces
  3384Ôćĺ$\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$.
  3385ÔćĺThe Hessian has rank~1, constraining exactly the $v_0$-doubling
  3386Ôćĺcombination while leaving $m_s$ and $m_c$ to the Koide seed.
  3387ÔćĺThe extension from $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ to
  3388Ôćĺ$\mathbb{R}^4$ relies on \"Unsal's continuity conjecture.
  3389Ôćĺ
  3390Ôćĺ\subsection{Lepton masses are not mesino masses}
  3391ÔćĺThe fermion mass matrix $W_{IJ}$ at the Seiberg vacuum has
  3392Ôćĺlight eigenvalues ($\sim 10^{-3}$--$10^{-2}\MeV$) from the
  3393Ôćĺseesaw between the meson and Lagrange-multiplier sectors.
  3394ÔćĺThese are far too small and too degenerate
  3395Ôćĺ($Q_{\text{light}} = 0.48$, not $2/3$) to reproduce lepton masses.
  3396ÔćĺThe bion-induced K\"ahler correction
  3397Ôćĺ$\delta K_{ij} \sim \zeta^2 \sqrt{m_i m_j}/\Lambda^2 \sim 10^{-7}$
  3398Ôćĺis negligible at the Seiberg vacuum, and a systematic scan over
  3399Ôćĺflavour-dependent K\"ahler parameters finds $Q \in [0.35,\,0.50]$,
  3400Ôćĺnever reaching~$2/3$.
  3401ÔćĺThe obstruction is structural: K\"ahler rescaling
  3402Ôćĺ$K^{-1/2}\,W_{IJ}\,K^{-1/2}$ is a congruence transformation
  3403Ôćĺthat preserves the rank of~$W_{IJ}$ and cannot generate new
  3404Ôćĺmass eigenvalues.
  3405ÔćĺA further dimensionless obstruction: the holomorphic mesino
  3406Ôćĺmass ratio $m_{\psi_{us}}/m_{\psi_{ud}} = M_d/M_s = m_s/m_d = 20.0$
  3407Ôćĺis preserved by any universal K\"ahler rescaling, but the target
  3408Ôćĺratio $m_\mu/m_e = 206.8$ differs by a factor~$10.3$.  Matching

3833Ôćĺ\section{Conclusions}
  3834Ôćĺ\label{sec:conclusions}
  3835Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3836Ôćĺ
  3837ÔćĺSeven principal results:
  3838Ôćĺ
  3839Ôćĺ\paragraph{1.\ The Koide formula is energy equipartition.}
  3840Ôćĺ$Q = 2/3$ is $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$: perturbation energies 
  3841Ôćĺmatch the base scale. The signed-charge framework resolves the 
  3842Ôćĺelectron mass puzzle (near-cancellation, not fine-tuning) and reveals 
  3843Ôćĺthat mesons satisfy Koide with $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ to $0.1\%$.
  3844Ôćĺ
  3845Ôćĺ\paragraph{2.\ Seed triples set the stage for SUSY breaking.}
  3846ÔćĺThe quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ share the
  3847Ôćĺuniversal ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$ and operate at $v_0^2 \sim
  3848Ôćĺ230$--$350\MeV$, close to the lepton triple's $v_0^2 = 314\MeV
  3849Ôćĺ\approx \fpi^2/27$
  3850Ôćĺ(recall that $v_0^2$ carries dimensions of MeV, not MeV$^2$,
  3851Ôćĺbecause $v_0 = z_0$ is a charge with dimensions MeV$^{1/2}$). 
  3852ÔćĺThe massless member is the necessary condition for F-term 
  3853Ôćĺ(O'Raifeartaigh) SUSY breaking. Upon breaking, seeds bloom into 
  3854Ôćĺfull triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi,D_s,B)$. The lepton triple 
  3855Ôćĺis the ``almost-seed'' that has barely bloomed.
  3856Ôćĺ
  3857Ôćĺ\paragraph{3.\ The seed condition is one superpotential constraint,
  3858Ôćĺstabilized by a K\"ahler pole.}
  3859ÔćĺThe O'Raifeartaigh model with superpotential
  3860Ôćĺ$W = f\Phi_0 + m\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\Phi_0\Phi_1^2$ produces a
  3861Ôćĺfermionic spectrum $(0,\, m_-,\, m_+)$ that is an exact Koide
  3862Ôćĺseed triple when $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$. The one-loop CW potential
  3863Ôćĺalone stabilizes the pseudo-modulus at $v = 0$; a negative
  3864Ôćĺquartic K\"ahler correction $c\,|\Phi_0|^4/\Lambda_K^2$ with
  3865Ôćĺ$c = -\Lambda_K^2/(12m^2)$ pins it at the K\"ahler pole
  3866Ôćĺ$v = \sqrt{3}\,m/g$ exactly. The Koide seed emerges at
  3867Ôćĺ$M_0 = 2m/3$.  The coefficient $c = -1/12$ is not radiatively
  3868Ôćĺgenerated but arises from magnetic bions on
  3869Ôćĺ$\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$ at $\alpha_s = 0.143$
  3870Ôćĺin the ISS magnetic dual.
  3871Ôćĺ
  3872Ôćĺ\paragraph{4.\ Two breaking mechanisms plus a heavy-sector origin.}
  3873ÔćĺF-term breaking (via the massless seed member) generates the light 
  3874Ôćĺsector; the Volkov--Akulov relation $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is its 
  3875Ôćĺlow-energy realization, not a separate mechanism. D-term breaking 
  3876Ôćĺ($U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI parameter) provides electromagnetic 
  3877Ôćĺsplittings as a perturbative correction. The heavy triple 
  3878Ôćĺ$(c,b,t)$ stands apart: no seed, no meson shadow, scale far above 
  3879Ôćĺ$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, possibly connected to electroweak breaking.
  3880Ôćĺ
  3881Ôćĺ\paragraph{5.\ The Diophantine counting uniquely selects $N=3$.}
  3882ÔćĺThree equations on the scalar content---$rs = 2N$, $r(r+1)/2 = 2N$,
  3883Ôćĺ$r^2+s^2-1 = 4N$---have the unique positive-integer solution
  3884Ôćĺ$(N,r,s) = (3,3,2)$, which maps to the $SU(5)$ representations
  3885Ôćĺ$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$.
  3886ÔćĺThis combination is anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
  3887Ôćĺ
  3888Ôćĺ\paragraph{6.\ An algebraic electroweak ratio.}
  3889ÔćĺThe eigenvalue equation $x^2 + s(s+1)\,x - s(s+1) = 0$, with
  3890Ôćĺ$s$ the spin Casimir, predicts
  3891Ôćĺ$R = 1 - x_+(1/2)/x_+(1) = 0.22310$, matching the on-shell
  3892Ôćĺ$\sin^2\theta_W = 0.22320$ to $0.4\sigma$ and giving
  3893Ôćĺ$M_W = 80.374\GeV$ ($0.39\sigma$ from PDG). The equation is
  3894Ôćĺpinned by three structural constraints; $R$ follows as a
  3895Ôćĺconsequence, not a free parameter. The dynamical origin of the
  3896Ôćĺequation and the choice of renormalization scheme remain open.
  3897Ôćĺ
  3898Ôćĺ\paragraph{7.\ An explicit Lagrangian with two to three free parameters.}
  3899ÔćĺThe full $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian
  3900Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}) comprises a K\"ahler potential
  3901Ôćĺwith a negative quartic correction $c = -1/12$ (stabilizing
  3902Ôćĺthe pseudo-modulus at $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$) and a bion correction
  3903Ôćĺ$K_{\text{bion}} \sim |\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$,
  3904Ôćĺa superpotential combining the Seiberg constraint
  3905Ôćĺwith Yukawa couplings (using separate $H_u$, $H_d$ doublets)
  3906Ôćĺand an NMSSM coupling $\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$
  3907Ôćĺ($\lambda_S = 0.72$ for $m_h = 125\GeV$, with $S$ a separate
  3908Ôćĺelementary singlet, distinct from the Lagrange multiplier~$X$),
  3909Ôćĺstandard $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ $D$-terms
  3910Ôćĺ(vanishing at $\tan\beta = 1$),
  3911Ôćĺand soft breaking $V_{\text{soft}} = \fpi^2\,\text{Tr}(M^\dagger M)$.
  3912ÔćĺThe supertrace $\text{STr}[M^2] = 18\,\fpi^2$
  3913Ôćĺreceives contributions only from the nine soft meson masses.
  3914ÔćĺThe mixed up/down-type Koide triples require $\tan\beta = 1$
  3915Ôćĺfor the mass-level condition to equal a Yukawa-level
  3916Ôćĺcondition---a prediction independently motivated by $SU(5)_f$.
  3917ÔćĺThe seed Koide fixes the
  3918Ôćĺpseudo-modulus VEV $\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ (predicting $m_c/m_s$); the
  3919Ôćĺbion K\"ahler correction fixes $m_b$ through the
  3920Ôćĺ$v_0$-doubling condition, with Hessian rank~1 constraining
  3921Ôćĺexactly one mass combination.  The self-consistent chain from $m_s$ alone predicts
  3922Ôćĺ$m_c = 1{,}301\MeV$, $m_b = 4{,}177\MeV$, and---if
  3923Ôćĺ$Q(c,b,t) = 2/3$ is imposed---$m_t = 171.3 \pm 1.5\GeV$
  3924Ôćĺ(Table~\ref{tab:predictions}).
  3925ÔćĺThe lepton Koide scale satisfies $M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$
  3926Ôćĺto $0.04\%$, tying lepton masses to the pion decay constant.
  3927ÔćĺThe Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$ has no kinetic term
  3928Ôćĺ($F_X = 0$), and all off-diagonal meson masses are positive;
  3929Ôćĺa block-diagonal obstruction in the K\"ahler sector prevents
  3930Ôćĺcross-flavour condensation at any perturbative order.
  3931ÔćĺThe seesaw structure nevertheless embeds the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin
  3932Ôćĺrelation $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ algebraically,
  3933Ôćĺso the Cabibbo angle is naturally encoded in the mass hierarchy
  3934Ôćĺeven though the mixing itself must originate from a mechanism
  3935Ôćĺbeyond the confined-meson effective theory.
  3936ÔćĺAfter Koide and $v_0$-doubling constraints,
  3937Ôćĺonly $m_u$, $m_d$, and $m_s$ remain free; $m_u$ and
  3938Ôćĺ$m_d$ are connected to the Cabibbo angle through the
  3939ÔćĺGatto--Sartori--Tonin relation.  The $SO(32)$ adjoint provides the UV completion,
  3940Ôćĺwith the $E_8 \times E_8$ alternative blocked by the
  3941Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15}$-vs.-$\mathbf{10}$ obstruction.
  3942Ôćĺ
  3943ÔćĺThe diquark sector, required to complete the SUSY multiplet count,
  3944Ôćĺfills the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ of
  3945Ôćĺ$SU(5)_f$---the representation produced naturally by the SUSY
  3946Ôćĺgenerator acting on quarks and by the $SO(32)$ decomposition. A
  3947Ôćĺkinematic theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) shows that \emph{no}
  3948Ôćĺ$J=0$ color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor, regardless
  3949Ôćĺof orbital angular momentum: the sBootstrap diquark in the
  3950Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite. The Miyazawa supercharge
  3951Ôćĺ$\mathcal{Q}_{[ij]} \in \overline{\mathbf{10}}$
  3952Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:baryon_open}) connects mesons to baryons but
  3953Ôćĺgenerates diquark operators in $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$
  3954Ôćĺ(antisymmetric), not $\mathbf{15}$ (symmetric).  The remaining
  3955Ôćĺcandidate is a fundamental string-theoretic state from the
  3956Ôćĺ$SO(32)$ adjoint.
  3957Ôćĺ
  3958ÔćĺThe organizing principle is that each sector of the spectrum begins
  3959Ôćĺwith a massless member (a seed), which is the necessary condition
  3960Ôćĺfor spontaneous SUSY breaking. The breaking lifts the zero,
  3961Ôćĺgenerates the full triple, and the energy-balance condition
  3962Ôćĺ$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ constrains the initial conditions.
  3963ÔćĺThe bloom from seed to full triple is a phase rotation
  3964Ôćĺ$\delta: 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\text{phys}}$ in the Koide
  3965Ôćĺparametrization, at fixed Koide scale $v_0$; it is not a
  3966Ôćĺcontinuous deformation of the O'Raifeartaigh spectrum (which would
  3967Ôćĺbreak $Q = 2/3$) but a nonperturbative rearrangement.
  3968ÔćĺThe bloom mechanism (preceding section) identifies the
  3969Ôćĺdynamical origin of $\delta$: the adiabatic bion potential is
  3970Ôćĺexactly flat on the Koide manifold, but with frozen topological signs
  3971Ôćĺit provides a restoring force opposing the bloom; the three-instanton
  3972Ôćĺpotential $-\cos(9\delta)$ drives $\delta$ away from the seed, and their
  3973Ôćĺequilibrium lies within $2.8^\circ$ of the physical quark bloom position.
  3974ÔćĺThe unified superpotential has a layered structure:
  3975Ôćĺ(i)~the Seiberg effective superpotential governs the light sector,
  3976Ôćĺwith SUSY breaking forced by the quantum constraint when $m_u = m_d = 0$;
  3977Ôćĺ(ii)~the O'Raifeartaigh condition $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ (equivalently
  3978Ôćĺ$\langle X\rangle = \sqrt{3}\,\mu$ in the magnetic dual) fixes the seed Koide ratio,
  3979Ôćĺpredicting $m_c$ from $m_s$;
  3980Ôćĺ(iii)~the bion K\"ahler correction
  3981Ôćĺ$|\!\sum s_k\sqrt{\hat m_k}|^2$ predicts
  3982Ôćĺ$m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$ via $v_0$-doubling;
  3983Ôćĺ(iv)~the Yukawa sector governs the $(c,b,t)$ triple as an
  3984Ôćĺoverdetermination test.
  3985ÔćĺAfter these constraints, only two to three free parameters remain:
  3986Ôćĺ$m_u$, $m_d$ (connected to the Cabibbo angle through the GST
  3987Ôćĺrelation), and the overall scale $m_s$. All quark Koide triples
  3988Ôćĺobligatorily mix up-type and down-type mass eigenstates.
  3989ÔćĺThe energy-balance condition is a boundary condition on the
  3990Ôćĺsuperpotential, not an RG attractor; the Koide ratios are naturally
  3991Ôćĺprotected against $D$-term perturbations because $D$-terms shift
  3992Ôćĺscalar masses but leave fermion masses untouched.
  3993Ôćĺ
  3994Ôćĺ\begin{thebibliography}{99}
  3995Ôćĺ\bibitem{Rivero2005}
  3996ÔćĺA.~Rivero, ``An interpretation of scalars in SO(32),''
  3997ÔćĺEur.\ Phys.\ J.\ C \textbf{84}, 1058 (2024),
  3998ÔćĺarXiv:2407.05397 [hep-ph]; earlier versions: viXra:0503.001 (2005),
  3999ÔćĺviXra:0809.001 (2008).
  4000Ôćĺ
  4001Ôćĺ\bibitem{Rivero2012}
  4002ÔćĺA.~Rivero, ``A new Koide tuple: strange, charm, bottom,'' 
  4003ÔćĺarXiv:1111.7232 [hep-ph] (2011).
  4004Ôćĺ
  4005Ôćĺ\bibitem{Koide1983}
  4006ÔćĺY.~Koide, ``New viewpoint of quark and lepton mass hierarchy,'' 
  4007ÔćĺPhys.\ Rev.\ D \textbf{28}, 252 (1983).
  4008Ôćĺ
  4009Ôćĺ\bibitem{CattoGursey}
  4010ÔćĺS.~Catto and F.~G\"ursey, ``Algebraic treatment of effective 
  4011Ôćĺsupersymmetry,'' Nuovo Cim.\ A \textbf{86}, 201 (1985).
  4012Ôćĺ
  4013Ôćĺ\bibitem{Miyazawa}
  4014ÔćĺH.~Miyazawa, ``Baryon Number Changing Currents,'' 
  4015ÔćĺProg.\ Theor.\ Phys.\ \textbf{36}, 1266 (1966).
  4016Ôćĺ
  4017Ôćĺ\bibitem{Harari}
  4018ÔćĺH.~Harari, H.~Haut, J.~Weyers, ``Quark masses and Cabibbo

  4020Ôćĺ

  4024Ôćĺ(1995).
  4025Ôćĺ
  4026Ôćĺ\bibitem{ORaifeartaigh}
  4027ÔćĺL.~O'Raifeartaigh, ``Spontaneous symmetry breaking for chiral

  4029Ôćĺ
  4030Ôćĺ\bibitem{FayetIliopoulos}
  4031ÔćĺP.~Fayet and J.~Iliopoulos, ``Spontaneously broken supergauge

  4034Ôćĺ

1348Ôćĺ\section{Two Algebraic Structures}
  1349Ôćĺ\label{sec:two_structures}
  1350Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1351Ôćĺ
  1352ÔćĺThe sBootstrap particle content and mass spectrum are constrained by
  1353Ôćĺtwo \emph{independent} algebraic structures.  Part~A
  1354Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}--\ref{subsec:so32_open}) derives the
  1355Ôćĺrepresentation content from an $SO(32)$ embedding.  Part~B
  1356Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}--\ref{subsec:casimir_open}) derives an
  1357Ôćĺelectroweak mass ratio from a Casimir eigenvalue equation.
  1358ÔćĺNo algebraic connection between the two has been found
  1359Ôćĺ(see \S\ref{subsec:two_gap}).
  1360Ôćĺ
  1361Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1362Ôćĺ\subsection{Part A: The SO(32) embedding chain}
  1363Ôćĺ\label{subsec:so32_chain}
  1364Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1365Ôćĺ
  1366ÔćĺThe $SO(32)$ gauge group of Type~I / heterotic string theory
  1367Ôćĺadmits the maximal-subgroup chain
  1368Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1369Ôćĺ\label{eq:so32_chain}
  1370ÔćĺSO(32) \;\supset\; SU(16) \times U(1)_A
  1371Ôćĺ  \;\supset\; SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B \,.
  1372Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1373ÔćĺThe first step is the standard embedding $SO(2n) \supset SU(n)
  1374Ôćĺ\times U(1)$; the second decomposes $SU(16)$ under the regular
  1375Ôćĺsubalgebra $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_B$.
  1376ÔćĺHere $SU(5)$ is the Georgi--Glashow unification group and $SU(3)$
  1377Ôćĺis a \emph{horizontal} (flavor) symmetry acting on the three
  1378Ôćĺlight generations---it is \textbf{not} $SU(3)_c$.
  1379Ôćĺ
  1380ÔćĺThe fundamental $\mathbf{32}$ of $SO(32)$ decomposes as
  1381Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1382Ôćĺ\label{eq:32decomp}
  1383Ôćĺ\mathbf{32} = \underbrace{(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})}_{R_1}
  1384Ôćĺ  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\bar{\mathbf{3}})}_{R_2}
  1385Ôćĺ  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_3}
  1386Ôćĺ  \;\oplus\; \underbrace{(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})}_{R_4}\,,
  1387Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1388Ôćĺwhere $\dim = 15 + 15 + 1 + 1 = 32$ and the four summands carry
  1389Ôćĺdistinct $U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$ charges.
  1390Ôćĺ
  1391Ôćĺ\subsection{Decomposition of the adjoint}
  1392ÔćĺThe adjoint of $SO(32)$ is the antisymmetric square of the
  1393Ôćĺfundamental: $\mathbf{496} = \wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$.  Distributing
  1394Ôćĺover the four summands in \eqref{eq:32decomp} and using the identity
  1395Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1396Ôćĺ\wedge^2(\mathbf{5},\mathbf{3})
  1397Ôćĺ  = \bigl[\wedge^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes S^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1398Ôćĺ    \;\oplus\;
  1399Ôćĺ    \bigl[S^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})\bigr]
  1400Ôćĺ  = (\mathbf{10},\mathbf{6})
  1401Ôćĺ    \;\oplus\;
  1402Ôćĺ    (\mathbf{15},\bar{\mathbf{3}})\,,
  1403Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1404Ôćĺone obtains the full decomposition listed in
  1405ÔćĺTable~\ref{tab:496}.
  1406Ôćĺ
  1407Ôćĺ\begin{table}[ht]
  1408Ôćĺ\centering
  1409Ôćĺ\caption{Decomposition of the $SO(32)$ adjoint $\mathbf{496}$
  1410Ôćĺunder $SU(5) \times SU(3) \times U(1)_A \times U(1)_B$.}
  1411Ôćĺ\label{tab:496}
  1412Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{lcrrc}
  1413Ôćĺ\toprule
  1414Ôćĺ$SU(5)\!\times\!SU(3)$ rep & Dim & $Q_A$ & $Q_B$ & Origin \\
  1415Ôćĺ\midrule
  1416Ôćĺ\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Adjoint sector
  1417Ôćĺ  ($Q_A = Q_B = 0$, 226 states):}} \\[3pt]
  1418Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{24},\,\mathbf{8})$ & 192 & $0$ & $0$
  1419Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1420Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{24},\,\mathbf{1})$ & 24  & $0$ & $0$
  1421Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1422Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{8})$  & 8   & $0$ & $0$
  1423Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1424Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{1})$  & 1   & $0$ & $0$
  1425Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_2$ \\
  1426Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{1},\,\mathbf{1})$  & 1   & $0$ & $0$
  1427Ôćĺ  & $R_3 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1428Ôćĺ\midrule
  1429Ôćĺ\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Matter sector
  1430Ôćĺ  ($Q_A,\,Q_B \neq 0$, 270 states):}} \\[3pt]
  1431Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{10},\,\mathbf{6})$  & 60 & $+2$ & $+2$
  1432Ôćĺ  & $\wedge^2(R_1)$ \\
  1433Ôćĺ$(\overline{\mathbf{10}},\,\bar{\mathbf{6}})$ & 60 & $-2$ & $-2$
  1434Ôćĺ  & $\wedge^2(R_2)$ \\
  1435Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{15},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})$ & 45 & $+2$ & $+2$
  1436Ôćĺ  & $\wedge^2(R_1)$ \\
  1437Ôćĺ$(\overline{\mathbf{15}},\,\mathbf{3})$ & 45 & $-2$ & $-2$
  1438Ôćĺ  & $\wedge^2(R_2)$ \\
  1439Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{5},\,\mathbf{3})_{+}$  & 15 & $+2$ & $-14$
  1440Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_3$ \\
  1441Ôćĺ$(\mathbf{5},\,\mathbf{3})_{-}$  & 15 & $0$  & $+16$
  1442Ôćĺ  & $R_1 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1443Ôćĺ$(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})_{+}$ & 15 & $-2$ & $+14$
  1444Ôćĺ  & $R_2 \otimes R_3$ \\
  1445Ôćĺ$(\bar{\mathbf{5}},\,\bar{\mathbf{3}})_{-}$ & 15 & $0$  & $-16$
  1446Ôćĺ  & $R_2 \otimes R_4$ \\
  1447Ôćĺ\midrule
  1448Ôćĺ\textbf{Total} & \textbf{496} & & & \\
  1449Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  1450Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  1451Ôćĺ\end{table}
  1452Ôćĺ
  1453Ôćĺ\subsection{Physical content and the projection problem}
  1454Ôćĺ\label{subsec:so32_open}
  1455Ôćĺ
  1456ÔćĺRestricting to the adjoint sector $Q_A = Q_B = 0$ and dropping the
  1457Ôćĺhorizontal $SU(3)$ factor, one reads off the $SU(5)$ content:
  1458Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1459Ôćĺ\mathbf{24} \;\oplus\; \mathbf{1}\,,
  1460Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1461Ôćĺwhich is precisely the meson/slepton content of the sBootstrap.
  1462ÔćĺThe matter sector at $|Q_A| = 2$ contains
  1463Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1464Ôćĺ\mathbf{15} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{15}}
  1465Ôćĺ  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{10} \;\oplus\; \overline{\mathbf{10}}
  1466Ôćĺ  \;\oplus\; \mathbf{5} \;\oplus\; \bar{\mathbf{5}}\,,
  1467Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1468Ôćĺamong which the $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ are
  1469Ôćĺthe symmetric diquarks demanded by the sBootstrap counting
  1470Ôćĺ(\S\ref{subsec:sym15}).
  1471Ôćĺ
  1472ÔćĺThus the sBootstrap representations appear in the $SO(32)$
  1473Ôćĺadjoint: the $\mathbf{24}$ in the neutral sector ($Q_A\!=\!Q_B\!=\!0$)
  1474Ôćĺand the $\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ in the matter
  1475Ôćĺsector ($|Q_A|\!=\!2$). However, the remaining $496 - 54 = 442$
  1476Ôćĺstates (the $\mathbf{10}$, $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$,
  1477Ôćĺ$\mathbf{5}$, $\bar{\mathbf{5}}$, the SU(3)-horizontal copies,
  1478Ôćĺand the neutral singlets) must be projected out or made massive.
  1479ÔćĺThe mechanism for this projection---whether by an orbifold,
  1480Ôćĺa GSO-type condition, or a flux background---is an open problem.
  1481Ôćĺ
  1482ÔćĺThis embedding was first noted in \cite{Rivero2005}.  Its role
  1483Ôćĺhere is to provide a UV origin for the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$
  1484Ôćĺthat the Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) forbids at the
  1485Ôćĺlevel of point-particle composites: in the string picture,
  1486Ôćĺdiquarks are open-string states whose symmetry is determined by
  1487Ôćĺthe gauge group, not by Fermi statistics of constituents.
  1488Ôćĺ
  1489Ôćĺ\paragraph{Why $SO(32)$ and not $E_8 \times E_8$.}
  1490ÔćĺAnomaly cancellation in ten dimensions selects two gauge groups:
  1491Ôćĺ$SO(32)$ and $E_8 \times E_8$. The latter cannot accommodate the
  1492ÔćĺsBootstrap. Every decomposition of $E_8$ under $SU(5)$ produces
  1493Ôćĺthe antisymmetric $\mathbf{10}$, never the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$:
  1494Ôćĺthe maximal-subgroup chain $E_8 \supset SU(5) \times SU(5)$ gives
  1495Ôćĺ$\mathbf{248} = (\mathbf{24},\mathbf{1}) + (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{24})
  1496Ôćĺ+ (\mathbf{10},\bar{\mathbf{5}}) + (\overline{\mathbf{10}},\mathbf{5})
  1497Ôćĺ+ (\mathbf{5},\mathbf{10}) + (\bar{\mathbf{5}},\overline{\mathbf{10}})$,
  1498Ôćĺwith no $\mathbf{15}$ appearing anywhere.  The obstruction is
  1499Ôćĺirreducible: it reflects the fact that $E_8$'s simply-laced root
  1500Ôćĺlattice admits only antisymmetric tensor representations of
  1501Ôćĺ$SU(5)$.  By contrast, $SO(32)$'s adjoint $\mathbf{496} =
  1502Ôćĺ\wedge^2(\mathbf{32})$ naturally contains the symmetric
  1503Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15}$ through the identity
  1504Ôćĺ$\wedge^2(\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{3}) \supset
  1505ÔćĺS^2(\mathbf{5}) \otimes \wedge^2(\mathbf{3})
  1506Ôćĺ= (\mathbf{15}, \bar{\mathbf{3}})$.  The Type~I realization, where
  1507Ôćĺquark Chan-Paton factors directly generate $SO(32)$ from
  1508Ôćĺ$5\!\times\!3 + 1 = 16$ endpoint labels, is the most natural home.
  1509Ôćĺ
  1510ÔćĺThe $E_8 \times E_8$ heterotic string is connected to Type~I $SO(32)$
  1511Ôćĺby a chain of dualities (S-duality to heterotic $SO(32)$, then
  1512ÔćĺT-duality on a non-simply-connected torus).  These dualities may
  1513Ôćĺallow the sBootstrap content to appear in the $E_8 \times E_8$ frame
  1514Ôćĺthrough a non-standard embedding, but the direct decomposition
  1515Ôćĺroute is blocked by the $\mathbf{15}$ vs.\ $\mathbf{10}$
  1516Ôćĺobstruction.
  1517Ôćĺ
  1518Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1519Ôćĺ\subsection{Part B: The Casimir eigenvalue equation}
  1520Ôćĺ\label{subsec:casimir_eq}
  1521Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1522Ôćĺ
  1523ÔćĺConsider the eigenvalue equation
  1524Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1525Ôćĺ\label{eq:casimir_quartic}
  1526Ôćĺx^2 \;+\; C_2\,x \;-\; C_2 = 0\,,
  1527Ôćĺ\qquad C_2 = s(s+1)\,,
  1528Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1529Ôćĺwhere $C_2$ is the quadratic Casimir of $SU(2)$ at spin $s$ and
  1530Ôćĺ$x = M^2/m^2$ for a mass scale $m$ to be determined.  Equivalently,
  1531Ôćĺ$x^2/(1-x) = C_2$: the eigenvalue is a self-consistent solution
  1532Ôćĺwhere the ``squared coupling'' $x^2$ equals $C_2$ times the
  1533Ôćĺcomplement $(1-x)$.  The solutions are
  1534Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1535Ôćĺ\label{eq:casimir_roots}
  1536Ôćĺx_{\pm}(s) = \frac{-C_2 \pm \sqrt{C_2^2 + 4C_2}}{2}\,.
  1537Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1538Ôćĺ
  1539Ôćĺ\paragraph{Structural constraints (codimension analysis).}
  1540ÔćĺThe most general two-parameter family of such equations is
  1541Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1542Ôćĺx^2 + (f_0 + f_1 C_2)\,x + (g_0 + g_1 C_2) = 0\,,
  1543Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1544Ôćĺwith four real coefficients $(f_0, f_1, g_0, g_1)$.  Three
  1545Ôćĺstructural conditions pin the polynomial to \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}:
  1546Ôćĺ\begin{enumerate}
  1547Ôćĺ\item $f_0 = 0$: no spin-independent mass term;
  1548Ôćĺ\item $g_0 = 0$: a massless eigenstate exists at $s = 0$;
  1549Ôćĺ\item $g_1 = -f_1$: coefficient antisymmetry
  1550Ôćĺ  (the constant and linear terms in $C_2$ are related).
  1551Ôćĺ\end{enumerate}
  1552ÔćĺTogether with the normalization $f_1 = 1$, these exhaust the
  1553Ôćĺfreedom.  A systematic scan of 582 polynomial alternatives
  1554Ôćĺ(quadratics, cubics, quartics with Casimir-type functions and
  1555Ôćĺrational coefficients) confirms uniqueness: no other
  1556Ôćĺequation of this class reproduces $\sin^2\!\theta_W$ to
  1557Ôćĺsub-percent accuracy at $(s_1, s_2) = (1/2, 1)$.
  1558ÔćĺThe ratio derived below is therefore a
  1559Ôćĺ\emph{consequence} of these constraints, not a free parameter.
  1560Ôćĺ
  1561Ôćĺ\subsection{The electroweak ratio}
  1562ÔćĺDefine the ratio
  1563Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1564ÔćĺR \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}\,.
  1565Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1566ÔćĺEvaluating \eqref{eq:casimir_roots} at $s = 1/2$ ($C_2 = 3/4$)
  1567Ôćĺand $s = 1$ ($C_2 = 2$):
  1568Ôćĺ\begin{align}
  1569Ôćĺx_+(1/2) &= \tfrac{1}{8}\bigl(-3 + \sqrt{57}\bigr)
  1570Ôćĺ  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1/2)\,m^2 = M_W^2\,,
  1571Ôćĺ\\[4pt]
  1572Ôćĺx_+(1) &= -1 + \sqrt{3}
  1573Ôćĺ  \,,\quad\text{so } x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2\,.
  1574Ôćĺ\end{align}
  1575ÔćĺThe identification with $W$ and $Z$ is motivated by their
  1576Ôćĺ$SU(2)$ quantum numbers ($s = 1/2$ maps to the doublet structure of $W$,
  1577Ôćĺ$s = 1$ to the triplet).  Then
  1578Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1579ÔćĺR = 1 - \frac{x_+(1/2)}{x_+(1)}
  1580Ôćĺ  = \frac{(\sqrt{19} - 3)(\sqrt{19} - \sqrt{3}\,)}{16}
  1581Ôćĺ  \;\approx\; 0.2231013\ldots
  1582Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1583ÔćĺThe factored form follows from the discriminants of
  1584Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic}: $57 = 3 \times 19$ at $s = 1/2$ and
  1585Ôćĺ$12 = 4 \times 3$ at $s = 1$.  Rationalizing the denominator
  1586Ôćĺ$\sqrt{3} - 1$ yields an expression in $\sqrt{19}$ and $\sqrt{3}$
  1587Ôćĺalone, which coincides term-by-term with the form found
  1588Ôćĺempirically by de~Vries.
  1589Ôćĺ
  1590ÔćĺThis should be compared with the on-shell electroweak mixing
  1591Ôćĺparameter:
  1592Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1593Ôćĺ\label{eq:sin2_onshell}
  1594Ôćĺ\sin^2\!\theta_W^{\text{os}}
  1595Ôćĺ  \;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{M_W^2}{M_Z^2}
  1596Ôćĺ  = 0.22320 \pm 0.00026\,,
  1597Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1598Ôćĺusing $M_Z = 91.1876 \pm 0.0021\GeV$ and
  1599Ôćĺ$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ (PDG~2024).
  1600Ôćĺ
  1601ÔćĺSince $R = 1 - x_+(1/2)/x_+(1)$ and
  1602Ôćĺ$\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2$, the two expressions are
  1603Ôćĺ\emph{algebraically identical} once one identifies
  1604Ôćĺ$x_+(s) = M^2(s)/m^2$.  The prediction $R \approx
  1605Ôćĺ\sin^2\!\theta_W$ and the $W$-mass prediction derived below are
  1606Ôćĺtherefore \textbf{one and the same observation}, not two
  1607Ôćĺindependent tests.
  1608Ôćĺ
  1609Ôćĺ\paragraph{$W$-mass prediction.}
  1610ÔćĺFrom $M_W = M_Z\sqrt{1 - R}$:
  1611Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1612ÔćĺM_W^{\text{pred}}
  1613Ôćĺ  = 91.1876 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.22310}
  1614Ôćĺ  = 80.374 \pm 0.002\GeV\,,
  1615Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1616Ôćĺwhich lies $0.39\sigma$ from the PDG average
  1617Ôćĺ$M_W = 80.369 \pm 0.013\GeV$ and strongly disfavors the
  1618Ôćĺanomalous CDF-II measurement $M_W^{\text{CDF}} = 80.4335 \pm
  1619Ôćĺ0.0094\GeV$ (6.2$\sigma$ tension with $R$).
  1620Ôćĺ
  1621Ôćĺ\paragraph{Scale parameter.}
  1622ÔćĺSetting $x_+(1)\,m^2 = M_Z^2$ gives
  1623Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1624Ôćĺm = \frac{M_Z}{\sqrt{\sqrt{3} - 1}}
  1625Ôćĺ  = 106.577\GeV
  1626Ôćĺ  \approx m_\mu \times 10^3\,,
  1627Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1628Ôćĺa coincidence whose significance is unclear.
  1629Ôćĺ
  1630Ôćĺ\subsection{Open issues for the Casimir equation}
  1631Ôćĺ\label{subsec:casimir_open}
  1632Ôćĺ
  1633Ôćĺ\paragraph{Renormalization scheme dependence.}
  1634ÔćĺThe comparison \eqref{eq:sin2_onshell} uses the on-shell
  1635Ôćĺdefinition $\sin^2\!\theta_W = 1 - M_W^2/M_Z^2 = 0.22320$,
  1636Ôćĺ\textbf{not} the $\overline{\text{MS}}$ value
  1637Ôćĺ$\sin^2\!\theta_W(\overline{\text{MS}}) = 0.23122 \pm 0.00004$.
  1638ÔćĺThe two differ by $\Delta \approx 0.008$, far larger than
  1639Ôćĺexperimental errors.  The algebraic argument
  1640Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} contains no dynamical mechanism to
  1641Ôćĺprefer one scheme over the other.  Until such a mechanism is
  1642Ôćĺidentified, the on-shell agreement should be regarded as
  1643Ôćĺsuggestive but scheme-dependent.
  1644Ôćĺ
  1645Ôćĺ\paragraph{The fourth eigenvalue.}
  1646ÔćĺAt $s = 1/2$, the second root of \eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} gives
  1647Ôćĺ\begin{equation}
  1648Ôćĺ|M(1/2, -)|
  1649Ôćĺ  = m\,\sqrt{|x_-(1/2)|}
  1650Ôćĺ  = 122.4\GeV\,,
  1651Ôćĺ\end{equation}
  1652Ôćĺwhich is 2.3\% below $M_H = 125.25 \pm 0.17\GeV$.  However,
  1653Ôćĺthis eigenstate carries spin $1/2$, while the Higgs boson is
  1654Ôćĺspin $0$.  The near-degeneracy is noted as a numerical curiosity;
  1655Ôćĺwe do not claim a connection.
  1656Ôćĺ
  1657Ôćĺ\paragraph{Origin.}
  1658ÔćĺThis observation was first made by H.~de~Vries in a Physics
  1659ÔćĺForums discussion (November 2004) and subsequently studied in
  1660Ôćĺ\cite{Rivero2005, Rivero2005b}.
  1661Ôćĺ
  1662Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1663Ôćĺ\subsection{Independence of the two structures}
  1664Ôćĺ\label{subsec:two_gap}
  1665Ôćĺ%----------------------------------------------------------------------
  1666Ôćĺ
  1667ÔćĺParts~A and~B describe independent algebraic structures that share
  1668Ôćĺthe same physical particles:
  1669Ôćĺ\begin{itemize}
  1670Ôćĺ\item The $SO(32)$ embedding (\S\ref{subsec:so32_chain}) governs
  1671Ôćĺ  the \emph{representation content}---which $SU(5)$ multiplets
  1672Ôćĺ  appear.
  1673Ôćĺ\item The Casimir equation (\S\ref{subsec:casimir_eq}) governs
  1674Ôćĺ  the \emph{mass/coupling pattern}---what numerical ratios emerge
  1675Ôćĺ  among the gauge-boson masses.
  1676Ôćĺ\item The two structures live over different number fields:
  1677Ôćĺ  the adjoint decomposition involves only rational
  1678Ôćĺ  dimensions, while the eigenvalue ratio $R$ lies in
  1679Ôćĺ  $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3},\sqrt{19}\,)$---an extension not
  1680Ôćĺ  generated by any invariant of $SO(32)$.
  1681Ôćĺ\end{itemize}
  1682ÔćĺNo algebraic homomorphism linking the embedding chain
  1683Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:so32_chain} to the eigenvalue equation
  1684Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:casimir_quartic} has been found.  The gap between
  1685Ôćĺ\emph{content} (which states exist) and \emph{dynamics}
  1686Ôćĺ(what masses they acquire) remains the central open problem of
  1687Ôćĺthe sBootstrap program.
  1688Ôćĺ
  1689Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1690Ôćĺ\section{SUSY Breaking: From Seeds to Spectrum}
  1691Ôćĺ\label{sec:breaking}
  1692Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  1693Ôćĺ
  1694ÔćĺThe SUSY-breaking story begins with the foundational observation 
  1695Ôćĺ\eqref{eq:pimu}: $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2$. This is the 
  1696ÔćĺVolkov--Akulov mass formula for a pseudo-Goldstino---the muon---whose 
  1697Ôćĺsuperpartner---the pion---is a pseudo-Goldstone boson. The SUSY-breaking
