3390Ôćĺ\subsection{Lepton masses are not mesino masses}
  3391ÔćĺThe fermion mass matrix $W_{IJ}$ at the Seiberg vacuum has
  3392Ôćĺlight eigenvalues ($\sim 10^{-3}$--$10^{-2}\MeV$) from the
  3393Ôćĺseesaw between the meson and Lagrange-multiplier sectors.
  3394ÔćĺThese are far too small and too degenerate
  3395Ôćĺ($Q_{\text{light}} = 0.48$, not $2/3$) to reproduce lepton masses.
  3396ÔćĺThe bion-induced K\"ahler correction
  3397Ôćĺ$\delta K_{ij} \sim \zeta^2 \sqrt{m_i m_j}/\Lambda^2 \sim 10^{-7}$
  3398Ôćĺis negligible at the Seiberg vacuum, and a systematic scan over
  3399Ôćĺflavour-dependent K\"ahler parameters finds $Q \in [0.35,\,0.50]$,
  3400Ôćĺnever reaching~$2/3$.
  3401ÔćĺThe obstruction is structural: K\"ahler rescaling
  3402Ôćĺ$K^{-1/2}\,W_{IJ}\,K^{-1/2}$ is a congruence transformation
  3403Ôćĺthat preserves the rank of~$W_{IJ}$ and cannot generate new
  3404Ôćĺmass eigenvalues.
  3405ÔćĺA further dimensionless obstruction: the holomorphic mesino
  3406Ôćĺmass ratio $m_{\psi_{us}}/m_{\psi_{ud}} = M_d/M_s = m_s/m_d = 20.0$
  3407Ôćĺis preserved by any universal K\"ahler rescaling, but the target
  3408Ôćĺratio $m_\mu/m_e = 206.8$ differs by a factor~$10.3$.  Matching
  3409Ôćĺboth masses simultaneously would require flavour-dependent
  3410Ôćĺanomalous dimensions ($\gamma_{ud} \approx 0.25$ vs.\
  3411Ôćĺ$\gamma_{us} \approx 0.46$), which are plausible for a
  3412Ôćĺstrongly coupled theory but not calculable with existing methods.
  3413ÔćĺMoreover, the K\"ahler expansion breaks down at the seesaw
  3414Ôćĺvacuum: $M_u/\Lambda \approx 1362$, $M_d/\Lambda \approx 630$,
  3415Ôćĺand $M_s/\Lambda \approx 31$, so the canonical K\"ahler
  3416Ôćĺ$K = \operatorname{Tr}(M^\dagger M)$ is a poor approximation
  3417Ôćĺand large non-perturbative corrections are expected.
  3418ÔćĺLepton masses in this framework therefore require a mechanism
  3419Ôćĺbeyond the confined-phase K\"ahler potential.
  3420ÔćĺA natural candidate is a \emph{separate} confining sector.
  3421Ôćĺ$SU(2)$ SQCD with $N_f = 3$ (the s-confining case $N_f = N_c + 1$)
  3422Ôćĺproduces three antisymmetric mesons $L^{ij} = \epsilon^{ab}\Psi^i_a\Psi^j_b$
  3423Ôćĺwith dynamical superpotential
  3424Ôćĺ$W_{\text{dyn}} = L^{12}L^{13}L^{23}/\Lambda_L^5$ (the Pfaffian).
  3425ÔćĺAn O'Raifeartaigh deformation
  3426Ôćĺ\[
  3427Ôćĺ  W = f X_L + m\,L^{12}L^{23} + g\,X_L(L^{12})^2
  3428Ôćĺ    + \frac{\epsilon}{\Lambda_L^5}\,L^{12}L^{13}L^{23}
  3429Ôćĺ\]
  3430Ôćĺbreaks SUSY at a metastable vacuum ($F_{X_L} = f$) while the
  3431ÔćĺPfaffian provides a distant SUSY-restoring vacuum.
  3432ÔćĺAt $t \equiv g\langle X_L\rangle/m = \sqrt{3}$
  3433Ôćĺ(selected by a K\"ahler pole, as in the quark sector),
  3434Ôćĺthe fermion mass matrix has the spectrum
  3435Ôćĺ$(0,\,(2-\sqrt{3})m,\,(2+\sqrt{3})m)$
  3436Ôćĺwith $Q = 2/3$ \emph{exactly}.
  3437ÔćĺThis is the same O'Raifeartaigh--Koide seed that generates the quark
  3438ÔćĺKoide triples; the mechanism is universal, independent of gauge group.
  3439ÔćĺThe bloom rotation handles the mass hierarchy, and a
  3440Ôćĺconfinement scale $\Lambda_L \sim 300\MeV$ gives the
  3441Ôćĺobserved $M_0 = 313.8\MeV$.
  3442ÔćĺBelow $\Lambda_L$, QED running is nearly flavour-universal
  3443Ôćĺand preserves $Q = 2/3$ to $O(\alpha^2/\pi^2) \sim 10^{-5}$,
  3444Ôćĺconsistent with the observed $9$~ppm deviation.
  3445ÔćĺA complete specification of the lepton sector---in particular
  3446Ôćĺthe mediation mechanism coupling $L^{ij}$ to the Higgs doublets
  3447Ôćĺand the derivation of the overall lepton mass scale---is deferred
  3448Ôćĺto future work.
  3449Ôćĺ
  3450Ôćĺ\subsection{The baryon sector}
  3451Ôćĺ\label{subsec:baryon_open}
  3452ÔćĺIn Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, baryons are part of the
  3453Ôćĺlow-energy spectrum. Their SUSY partners are ``baryonic fermions''
  3454Ôćĺwith no known candidates among observed particles.
  3455Ôćĺ
  3456ÔćĺThe meson--baryon connection requires a Miyazawa supercharge
  3457Ôćĺ\cite{Miyazawa, CattoGursey}, which cannot lie in the
  3458Ôćĺfundamental~$\mathbf{5}$: the product
  3459Ôćĺ$\mathbf{5}\otimes(\mathbf{1}\oplus\mathbf{24})$ does not
  3460Ôćĺcontain~$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$, so a single-index generator
  3461Ôćĺcannot map mesons to baryons.  The minimal choice is
  3462Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3463Ôćĺ\mathcal{Q}_{[pq],\alpha}
  3464Ôćĺ  \;\in\; \overline{\mathbf{10}}\;\text{of}\;SU(5)_f\,,
  3465Ôćĺ  \qquad \Delta B = +1\,,
  3466Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3467Ôćĺwith transformation law
  3468Ôćĺ$[\mathcal{Q}_{[pq]}, M^i{}_j]
  3469Ôćĺ  = \delta^i_{[p}\,\tilde\psi_{B,\,q]j}
  3470Ôćĺ  - \tfrac{1}{5}\delta^i_j\,\tilde\psi_{B,\,pq}$,
  3471Ôćĺwhere $\tilde\psi_{B,\,lm}
  3472Ôćĺ  = \tfrac{1}{6}\epsilon_{lmijk}\,\psi_B^{ijk}$ is the dual
  3473Ôćĺbaryonino.  The anticommutator decomposes as
  3474Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3475Ôćĺ\overline{\mathbf{10}}\otimes\mathbf{10}
  3476Ôćĺ  = \mathbf{1}\oplus\mathbf{24}\oplus\mathbf{75}\,,
  3477Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3478Ôćĺgiving $\{\mathcal{Q},\bar{\mathcal{Q}}\}
  3479Ôćĺ  \supset \sigma^\mu P_\mu + T^A + Z$, where $T^A$ are the
  3480Ôćĺ$SU(5)_f$ generators and $Z$ are central charges encoding the
  3481Ôćĺmeson--baryon mass splitting $m_B^2 - m_M^2 = \langle Z\rangle
  3482Ôćĺ\sim\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2$.  This Miyazawa SUSY evades the
  3483ÔćĺHaag--\L{}opusza\'nski--Sohnius theorem because it is an
  3484Ôćĺapproximate symmetry broken at scale~$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$,
  3485Ôćĺanalogous to $SU(6)$ spin--flavor symmetry in the quark model.
  3486ÔćĺThe $\mathbf{75}$ channel on the right-hand side
  3487Ôćĺsignals that the full bosonic subalgebra extends beyond
  3488Ôćĺ$SU(5)_f$; its interpretation remains open.
  3489ÔćĺThe $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$ representation of the Miyazawa
  3490Ôćĺsupercharge is the same $SU(5)_f$ multiplet that appears in the
  3491Ôćĺ$SO(32)$ adjoint decomposition (Table~\ref{tab:496}), suggesting
  3492Ôćĺthat the baryon--meson connection and the string embedding may
  3493Ôćĺshare a common algebraic origin.
  3494Ôćĺ
  3495Ôćĺ\subsection{The strong CP problem}
  3496ÔćĺThe principle ``Nature abhors chirality'' predicts $\bar{\theta} = 0$ 
  3497Ôćĺbut provides no mechanism.
  3498Ôćĺ
  3499Ôćĺ\subsection{The kaon--muon puzzle}
  3500ÔćĺThe Lagrangian predicts a universal soft splitting
  3501Ôćĺ$m^2_{\text{meson}} - m^2_{\text{lepton}} = \fpi^2$ across the
  3502Ôćĺmeson matrix. For the pion--muon pair, this works:
  3503Ôćĺ$m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx \fpi^2$ to $2.2\%$. But the
  3504Ôćĺtree-level formula $m^2_M = |m_i + m_j|^2 + \fpi^2$ badly
  3505Ôćĺunderpredicts heavier meson masses: it gives $m_K \approx 133\MeV$
  3506Ôćĺ(physical: $498\MeV$) and $m_{D_s} \approx 1{,}367\MeV$
  3507Ôćĺ(physical: $1{,}968\MeV$). QCD confinement generates the bulk
  3508Ôćĺof these masses independently of the soft term. The universal
  3509Ôćĺsplitting applies to the \emph{soft} contribution, not to the
  3510Ôćĺtotal mass; it is visible only for $(\pi, \mu)$,
  3511Ôćĺwhere the soft term dominates over $|m_u + m_d|^2$.
  3512Ôćĺ
  3513Ôćĺ\subsection{Flavour-changing neutral currents}
  3514ÔćĺThe off-diagonal meson scalars $M^d{}_s$, $M^s{}_d$ are the
  3515Ôćĺ\emph{same particles} as the SM kaon and antikaon---they are not
  3516Ôćĺnew degrees of freedom.  Their tree-level contribution to
  3517Ôćĺ$K$--$\bar K$ mixing is the standard QCD effect already
  3518Ôćĺincluded in SM predictions.  The question is whether the
  3519Ôćĺadditional SUSY couplings (the $\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$
  3520Ôćĺvertex, the Yukawa $y_c\,H_u\,M^d{}_d$ and
  3521Ôćĺ$y_b\,H_d\,M^s{}_s$) generate \emph{extra} flavour-violating
  3522Ôćĺcontributions beyond QCD.  Because the mesinos are identified
  3523Ôćĺwith the leptons in the sBootstrap framework, no new particles
  3524Ôćĺare introduced; the principal source of additional FCNC is the
  3525ÔćĺYukawa-meson cross-coupling through Higgs exchange.  A
  3526Ôćĺquantitative estimate of these effects requires matching the
  3527Ôćĺeffective theory to the measured $K$--$\bar K$ Wilson
  3528Ôćĺcoefficients~\cite{UTfit} and will be addressed in future work.
  3529Ôćĺ
  3530Ôćĺ\subsection{The Casimir eigenvalue equation}
  3531ÔćĺThe eigenvalue equation $x^2 + C_2 x - C_2 = 0$ produces the
  3532Ôćĺon-shell weak mixing angle to $0.4\sigma$, but three issues
  3533Ôćĺremain open: (i)~the equation is an ansatz, not derived from a
  3534ÔćĺLagrangian; (ii)~the match uses the on-shell renormalization
  3535Ôćĺscheme, and no argument selects this scheme over $\overline{\text{MS}}$;
  3536Ôćĺ(iii)~the negative spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ root gives $|M| = 122.4\GeV$,
  3537Ôćĺtantalizingly close to $M_H = 125.25\GeV$ but with a spin mismatch.
  3538Ôćĺ
  3539Ôćĺ\subsection{The Higgs mass at $\tan\beta = 1$}
  3540ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$, the $D$-term quartic
  3541Ôćĺ$(g^2+g'^2)(|H_u|^2 - |H_d|^2)^2/8$ vanishes on the
  3542Ôćĺ$D$-flat direction $H_u = H_d$.  The tree-level lightest
  3543ÔćĺCP-even Higgs mass is $m_h = m_Z|\cos 2\beta| = 0$---a known
  3544ÔćĺMSSM result. The meson Yukawa F-terms contribute only mass
  3545Ôćĺterms ($y_j^2|H|^2$), not quartics.
  3546ÔćĺIn the NMSSM, a singlet coupling
  3547Ôćĺ$\lambda\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$ generates an extra quartic
  3548Ôćĺ$\lambda^2|H_u\!\cdot\!H_d|^2$; at $\sin 2\beta = 1$ (i.e.,
  3549Ôćĺ$\tan\beta = 1$), $m_h^2 = \lambda^2 v^2/2$, and
  3550Ôćĺ$\lambda = 0.72$ gives $m_h = 125\GeV$ at tree level.
  3551ÔćĺThe Seiberg Lagrange multiplier~$X$ cannot serve as the NMSSM
  3552Ôćĺsinglet: $[X] = \text{mass}^{-3}$, so the coupling
  3553Ôćĺ$\lambda_0\,X\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d$ requires
  3554Ôćĺ$[\lambda_0] = \text{mass}^4$; writing
  3555Ôćĺ$\lambda_0 = \hat\lambda\,\Lambda^4$, canonical normalisation
  3556Ôćĺgives $\lambda_{\text{NMSSM}} = \hat\lambda$.  The Higgs mass
  3557Ôćĺdemands $\hat\lambda \approx 0.51$, but positive meson VEVs
  3558Ôćĺrequire $\hat\lambda < 2\Lambda^2/v^2 \approx 3\times 10^{-6}$:
  3559Ôćĺa five-order-of-magnitude conflict.  Therefore~$S$ must be a
  3560Ôćĺ\emph{separate} elementary singlet with standard NMSSM couplings
  3561Ôćĺ$\lambda_S\,S\,H_u\!\cdot\!H_d + \tfrac{\kappa}{3}\,S^3$.
  3562ÔćĺAt $\tan\beta = 1$ and $\lambda_S = 0.72$, the tree-level
  3563ÔćĺHiggs mass $m_h = \lambda_S v/\sqrt{2} = 125\GeV$ is recovered.
  3564ÔćĺThe $\mu$-term $\mu = \lambda_S\langle S\rangle$ is set by the
  3565ÔćĺNMSSM soft potential, leaving $\langle S\rangle$ as a free
  3566Ôćĺparameter of order the electroweak scale.
  3567Ôćĺ
  3568Ôćĺ\subsection{Vacuum stability, CKM mixing, and the Oakes relation}
  3569Ôćĺ
  3570ÔćĺFor $N_f = N_c = 3$, the Seiberg description uses
  3571Ôćĺcomposites $M$, $B$, $\tilde B$ subject to the quantum-modified
  3572Ôćĺconstraint $\det M - B\tilde B = \Lambda^{2N_c}$.  The
  3573ÔćĺLagrange multiplier~$X$ enforces this constraint and has
  3574Ôćĺ\emph{no kinetic term}: it is not a dynamical field.
  3575ÔćĺThree independent arguments establish this:
  3576Ôćĺ(i)~Seiberg's original construction~\cite{Seiberg:1994bz}
  3577Ôćĺ    introduces~$X$ solely to impose the constraint;
  3578Ôćĺ(ii)~the magnetic dual has $N_c' = N_f - N_c = 0$---there is no
  3579Ôćĺ    magnetic gauge group and no ISS-type pseudo-modulus;
  3580Ôćĺ(iii)~the quantum moduli space $\det M - B\tilde B = \Lambda^6$
  3581Ôćĺ    is smooth and requires no additional dynamical field.
  3582Ôćĺ
  3583ÔćĺBecause~$X$ is non-dynamical, $F_X = 0$ at the constrained vacuum:
  3584Ôćĺthe equation $\partial W/\partial X = 0$ is a constraint, not an
  3585Ôćĺ$F$-term energy contribution.  The diagonal seesaw vacuum
  3586Ôćĺ$M^i{}_i = C/m_i$ satisfies the constraint exactly, and all
  3587Ôćĺoff-diagonal meson masses are positive:
  3588Ôćĺ\[
  3589Ôćĺ  m^2(M^a{}_b) = 2\fpi^2 + 2\,|X_0|^2 |M_c|^2
  3590Ôćĺ  \approx 2\fpi^2 = 16{,}928\;\MeV^2\,,
  3591Ôćĺ\]
  3592Ôćĺwhere the $F$-term contribution $|X_0|^2|M_c|^2$ is at most
  3593Ôćĺ$5\times 10^{-7}\;\MeV^2$ ($10^{-11}$ of the soft term).
  3594Ôćĺ\emph{There are no tachyonic modes in the off-diagonal meson sector.}
  3595Ôćĺ
  3596ÔćĺOne-loop Coleman--Weinberg corrections are positive
  3597Ôćĺ(stabilising) and suppressed by a factor~$10^{-9}$ relative to
  3598Ôćĺthe tree-level mass.  Moreover, a \emph{fundamental block-diagonal
  3599Ôćĺobstruction} prevents cross-flavour meson mixing: the $6\times 6$
  3600Ôćĺoff-diagonal mass matrix decomposes into three independent
  3601Ôćĺ$2\times 2$ blocks for conjugate pairs $(M^a{}_b,\,M^b{}_a)$,
  3602Ôćĺand this structure is preserved by all single-trace polynomial
  3603ÔćĺK\"ahler invariants.  The physical reason is index contraction:
  3604Ôćĺat a diagonal vacuum, each $M^a{}_b$ can only contract with its
  3605Ôćĺconjugate $M^b{}_a$, never with a field from a different
  3606Ôćĺflavour pair.  Cross-pair mixing would require non-perturbative
  3607Ôćĺ(instanton-generated) K\"ahler corrections or physics beyond the
  3608Ôćĺconfined-meson effective theory.
  3609Ôćĺ
  3610ÔćĺAlthough the off-diagonal meson vacuum is exactly diagonal, the
  3611Ôćĺseesaw structure $M_i = C/m_i$ naturally embeds the
  3612ÔćĺGatto--Sartori--Tonin relation.  If a perturbation (from any source)
  3613Ôćĺwere to generate off-diagonal condensates $\epsilon_{ab}$, the
  3614Ôćĺseesaw hierarchy would ensure
  3615Ôćĺ\[
  3616Ôćĺ  \frac{\epsilon_{us}}{\epsilon_{ud}}
  3617Ôćĺ  = \sqrt{\frac{m_d}{m_s}} = 0.2236
  3618Ôćĺ  = \sin\theta_C\big|_{\text{GST}}\,,
  3619Ôćĺ\]
  3620Ôćĺreproducing the Weinberg--Oakes relation \emph{algebraically}.
  3621ÔćĺThe GST variant gives $\theta_C = 12.9^\circ$ (PDG:
  3622Ôćĺ$13.04^\circ$, $-0.9\%$).  This is a structural property of the
  3623Ôćĺseesaw-inverted mass hierarchy, not a dynamical prediction: the
  3624ÔćĺCabibbo angle is encoded in $m_d/m_s$ as a free parameter,
  3625Ôćĺwith the seesaw providing the natural scaffolding for the Oakes
  3626Ôćĺrelation.  The actual origin of CKM mixing---whether from
  3627Ôćĺnon-perturbative K\"ahler corrections, physics at the UV
  3628Ôćĺcompletion scale, or quark mass-matrix texture---remains an
  3629Ôćĺopen problem.
  3630Ôćĺ
  3631ÔćĺA notable extension to the first generation:
  3632Ôćĺthe Koide condition does \emph{not} hold for the triple
  3633Ôćĺ$(m_u, m_d, m_s)$ ($Q = 0.567$, 15\% off), but
  3634Ôćĺ$Q(0,\,m_u{+}m_d,\,m_s) = 0.665$ is only $0.27\%$ from~$2/3$---closer
  3635Ôćĺthan the established seed $Q(0,\,m_s,\,m_c) = 0.664$
  3636Ôćĺat~$0.35\%$.  The Koide chain extends to the first generation but
  3637Ôćĺsees the isospin-summed mass $m_u + m_d = 6.83\MeV$ as a single
  3638Ôćĺseed entry; the splitting into $m_u \neq m_d$ is the remaining
  3639Ôćĺfreedom parametrised by~$\theta_C$.
  3640Ôćĺ
  3641ÔćĺWithout a baryon mass term, the global minimum lies at
  3642Ôćĺ$M = 0$, $B\tilde B = -\Lambda^6$, with
  3643Ôćĺ$V = \sum m_i^2 \approx 8{,}750\;\MeV^2$.
  3644ÔćĺHowever, the seesaw vacuum is cosmologically stable:
  3645ÔćĺColeman--de~Luccia bounce-action estimates give
  3646Ôćĺ$S_4 \sim 2 \times 10^7$ (thick-wall) to $10^9$ (thin-wall),
  3647Ôćĺboth far above the threshold $S_4 > 400$ required for a lifetime
  3648Ôćĺexceeding the age of the universe.
  3649ÔćĺAdding a baryon mass term
  3650Ôćĺ$W_B = m_B\,B\tilde B$ to the superpotential lifts the baryonic
  3651Ôćĺflat direction without affecting the seesaw vacuum
  3652Ôćĺ(since $B = \tilde B = 0$ there): the meson masses $M_i$, the off-diagonal meson spectrum,
  3653Ôćĺand the Higgs sector are all unchanged.
  3654ÔćĺWith the K\"ahler pole confining $X$ near $X_{\text{seesaw}}$,
  3655Ôćĺany $m_B \gtrsim 1\;\MeV$ eliminates vacuum~(B) entirely.
  3656ÔćĺThe natural scale $m_B \sim \Lambda = 300\;\MeV$
  3657Ôćĺ(arising from instanton corrections in the confining phase) lifts
  3658Ôćĺthe baryino mass from $|X_0| \sim 10^{-9}\;\MeV$ to $\sim 300\;\MeV$
  3659Ôćĺand makes $V_B \sim 10^{20}\;\MeV^2 \gg V_A$.
  3660Ôćĺ
  3661Ôćĺ\subsection{The bloom mechanism}
  3662Ôćĺ
  3663ÔćĺThe Koide parametrization
  3664Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3665Ôćĺ  \sqrt{m_k} = v_0\bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos(\delta + 2\pi k/3)\bigr),
  3666Ôćĺ  \quad k = 0,1,2,
  3667Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3668Ôćĺsatisfies $Q = 2/3$ identically for all $\delta$ and $v_0$.
  3669ÔćĺThe seed sits at $\delta = 3\pi/4$, where
  3670Ôćĺ$\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2}$ forces $\sqrt{m_0} = 0$.
  3671ÔćĺA Koide-preserving bloom is a pure rotation
  3672Ôćĺ$\delta \colon 3\pi/4 \to \delta_{\mathrm{phys}}$ at fixed $v_0$;
  3673Ôćĺthe total mass $M = 6v_0^2$ is conserved while mass redistributes
  3674Ôćĺamong the three eigenvalues.
  3675Ôćĺ
  3676Ôćĺ\paragraph{Adiabatic bion potential.}
  3677ÔćĺThe monopole-instanton effective superpotential on $\mathbb{R}^3\times S^1$
  3678Ôćĺtakes the form $W_{\mathrm{mon}} \sim \zeta(\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}+\sqrt{m_3})$.
  3679ÔćĺThe bion (monopole--anti-monopole) contribution to the
  3680ÔćĺK\"{a}hler potential is $V_{\mathrm{bion}} = |\!\sum_k s_k\sqrt{m_k}|^2$,
  3681Ôćĺwhere $s_k = \mathrm{sign}(z_k)$ are the signs of the
  3682ÔćĺKoide amplitudes $z_k = v_0(1+\sqrt{2}\cos(\delta+2\pi k/3))$.
  3683ÔćĺOn the interior of the Koide manifold (away from the seed) all
  3684Ôćĺthree $z_k$ are nonzero, and the adiabatic signs follow $\delta$:
  3685Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3686Ôćĺ  \sum_k s_k |z_k| = \sum_k z_k = 3v_0 = \mathrm{const}.
  3687Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3688ÔćĺThis is an exact algebraic identity: the adiabatic bion potential
  3689Ôćĺ$V_{\mathrm{bion}} = (3v_0)^2 = 9v_0^2$ is \emph{flat} on the entire Koide
  3690Ôćĺmanifold and cannot select $\delta$.
  3691Ôćĺ
  3692Ôćĺ\paragraph{Frozen-sign bion.}
  3693ÔćĺAt the seed ($\delta = 3\pi/4$), one amplitude vanishes and its sign
  3694Ôćĺis topologically frozen: for the quark triple $(-s,c,b)$, the
  3695Ôćĺbloom from seed to physical values takes
  3696Ôćĺ$s_0: 0 \to -1$ while $s_{1,2} = +1$ remain fixed.
  3697ÔćĺWith frozen signs $s_k = +1$ for all $k$, the frozen-sign potential
  3698Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3699Ôćĺ  V_{\mathrm{frozen}}(\delta) = \left(\sum_k |z_k|\right)^2
  3700Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3701Ôćĺis \emph{not} constant on the Koide manifold.  It achieves its
  3702Ôćĺminimum value $9v_0^2$ at the seed ($\delta = 3\pi/4$, where
  3703Ôćĺ$\sum |z_k| = 3v_0$, the triangle inequality saturated by
  3704Ôćĺ$|z_0| = 0$) and grows for $\delta > 3\pi/4$.
  3705ÔćĺThe frozen bion thus provides a restoring force that opposes the bloom.
  3706Ôćĺ
  3707Ôćĺ\paragraph{Three-instanton driving force.}
  3708ÔćĺThe three-instanton operator
  3709Ôćĺ$\delta W_{3\text{-inst}} = c_3(\det M)^3/\Lambda^{18}$
  3710Ôćĺarises from the $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ center symmetry of $SU(3)$ SQCD
  3711Ôćĺwith $N_f = N_c = 3$ (the anomaly coefficient $6N_c = 18$
  3712Ôćĺbreaks $U(1)_{R+2A} \to \mathbb{Z}_{18}$; see the preceding subsection).
  3713ÔćĺNear the Seiberg vacuum, parametrizing
  3714Ôćĺ$\det M = \Lambda^6 e^{3i\delta}$ gives
  3715Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3716Ôćĺ  V_{3\text{-inst}} \propto -\cos(9\delta).
  3717Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3718ÔćĺFor the quark triple $(-s,c,b)$, the observed phase is
  3719Ôćĺ$\delta_{\mathrm{phys}} = 157.2^\circ$.
  3720ÔćĺThe nearest minimum of $-\cos(9\delta)$ is at $\delta = 160^\circ$,
  3721Ôćĺjust $2.8^\circ$ away.
  3722ÔćĺThe combined potential
  3723Ôćĺ\begin{equation*}
  3724Ôćĺ  V(\delta) = A\,V_{\mathrm{frozen}}(\delta) + B\,(-\cos 9\delta)
  3725Ôćĺ\end{equation*}
  3726Ôćĺhas a stable equilibrium at the physical phase for
  3727Ôćĺ$B/A = -2.07$: the three-instanton term drives the bloom outward
  3728Ôćĺfrom the seed, while the frozen-sign bion provides the restoring
  3729Ôćĺforce that stabilizes the physical triple.
  3730Ôćĺ
  3731Ôćĺ\paragraph{Lepton triple.}
  3732ÔćĺFor the lepton triple $(e,\mu,\tau)$, all three amplitudes
  3733Ôćĺ$z_k > 0$ throughout the bloom ($\delta_\ell = 132.7^\circ$,
  3734Ôćĺnever crossing zero), so the adiabatic and frozen-sign bion potentials
  3735Ôćĺcoincide: $V_{\mathrm{frozen}} = 9v_0^2$ exactly (flat).
  3736ÔćĺThe bion potential provides no selection.
  3737ÔćĺThe near-rational relation $\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$
  3738Ôćĺ($33\,\mathrm{ppm}$) is therefore \emph{not} explained by the
  3739Ôćĺbion mechanism; its origin is the three-instanton potential
  3740Ôćĺ$-\cos(9\delta)$ with a minimum at $\delta \equiv 2/9 \pmod{2\pi/3}$,
  3741Ôćĺas discussed in the preceding subsection.
  3742ÔćĺThe lepton and quark phases are selected by the same instanton
  3743Ôćĺpotential but through different dynamical channels:
  3744Ôćĺfor quarks the frozen-sign bion competes with the instanton term,
  3745Ôćĺwhile for leptons the bion is inert and the instanton acts unopposed.
  3746Ôćĺ
  3747Ôćĺ\subsection{The $SO(32)$ projection}
  3748ÔćĺThe adjoint $\mathbf{496}$ contains the sBootstrap
  3749Ôćĺrepresentations: $\mathbf{24}$ in the neutral sector and
  3750Ôćĺ$\mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ in the matter
  3751Ôćĺsector at $|Q_A| = 2$. The remaining 442 states must be
  3752Ôćĺprojected out or made massive. No projection mechanism has been
  3753Ôćĺidentified. The alternative anomaly-free gauge group
  3754Ôćĺ$E_8 \times E_8$ is blocked by the $\mathbf{15}$ vs.\
  3755Ôćĺ$\mathbf{10}$ obstruction (\S\ref{subsec:so32_open}),
  3756Ôćĺconstraining the string embedding to the Type~I / $SO(32)$
  3757Ôćĺcorner of the landscape.
  3758Ôćĺ
  3759Ôćĺ\subsection{Generation uniqueness}
  3760ÔćĺThe Diophantine system uniquely selects $N = 3$ generations,
  3761Ôćĺbut the third equation ($r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N$) has the weakest
  3762Ôćĺphysical motivation. A dynamical derivation of the counting
  3763Ôćĺconstraints would strengthen the argument considerably.
  3764Ôćĺ
  3765Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3766Ôćĺ\section{Epistemological classification}
  3767Ôćĺ\label{sec:epistemology}
  3768Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3769Ôćĺ
  3770ÔćĺThe following table classifies the paper's claims by their logical
  3771Ôćĺstatus.  ``Derived'' means following from stated assumptions by
  3772Ôćĺalgebra or field theory; ``observed'' means an empirical pattern;
  3773Ôćĺ``assumed'' means an input without derivation.
  3774Ôćĺ
  3775Ôćĺ\begin{center}
  3776Ôćĺ\small
  3777Ôćĺ\begin{tabular}{lll}
  3778Ôćĺ\toprule
  3779ÔćĺClaim & Status & Comment \\
  3780Ôćĺ\midrule
  3781Ôćĺ$Q = 2/3$ iff $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ & Derived & Algebra (\S\ref{sec:koide_algebra}) \\
  3782ÔćĺSeed ratio $= 2-\sqrt{3}$ & Derived & From $Q = 2/3$ + one zero \\
  3783ÔćĺO'R seed at $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ & Derived & Fermion eigenvalues \\
  3784ÔćĺK\"ahler stabilization: $c = -1/12$ & Derived & Pole condition (\S\ref{subsec:superpotential}) \\
  3785Ôćĺ$\langle X\rangle/\mu = \sqrt{3}$ in magnetic dual & Derived & ISS dictionary \\
  3786ÔćĺPauli theorem (no symmetric $J{=}0$ diquark) & Derived & Angular momentum \\
  3787ÔćĺDiophantine: $(N,r,s) = (3,3,2)$ unique & Derived & Number theory \\
  3788ÔćĺCyclic echo ($t \to c \to s \to \text{stall} \to c$) & Derived & Algebraic necessity \\
  3789ÔćĺSeed self-dual: $Q(a,b) = Q(1/a,1/b)$ & Derived & Identity for two masses \\
  3790ÔćĺCartan angle $\theta = \pi/6 - \delta$ & Derived & Exact trigonometric identity \\
  3791ÔćĺF-terms flavour-universal: $F = C/v_{\text{EW}}$ & Derived & Seesaw cancellation \\
  3792Ôćĺ\midrule
  3793ÔćĺLepton Koide $Q = 2/3$ to $0.001\%$ & Observed & $2.8\sigma$ after LEE \\
  3794ÔćĺMeson triple $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ to $0.10\%$ & Observed & $1.1\sigma$ after LEE \\
  3795Ôćĺ$v_0$-doubling: $v_0^{(f)}/v_0^{(s)} = 2.0005$ ($0.025\%$); $m_b$ to $0.07\%$ & Observed & Derived via bion K\"ahler \\
  3796Ôćĺ$\delta_\ell \bmod 2\pi/3 = 2/9$ to 33~ppm & Observed & $3.1\sigma$ after LEE \\
  3797ÔćĺCasimir $R = 0.22310$ vs $\sin^2\theta_W$ & Observed & Scheme-dependent \\
  3798ÔćĺDual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b) = 0.665$ & Observed & $0.2\%$ from $2/3$; not predictive \\
  3799ÔćĺGST: $|V_{us}| = \sqrt{m_d/m_s} = 0.224$ & Derived & Tachyon ratio; $0.9\%$ from PDG~\cite{GST1968} \\
  3800Ôćĺ$M_0 \approx \fpi^2/27$ & Observed & $0.04\%$; connects lepton scale to $\fpi$ \\
  3801Ôćĺ$\tan\beta = 1$ & Derived & Mixed triples + $SU(5)_f$ \\
  3802Ôćĺ\midrule
  3803Ôćĺ$m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2$ (VA relation) & Assumed & 2.2\% match \\
  3804Ôćĺ$f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ identification & Assumed & Central ansatz \\
  3805ÔćĺSU(5) flavor symmetry & Assumed & 5 light quarks \\
  3806Ôćĺ$m_b = 4{,}177 \pm 40\MeV$ from bion minimum & Derived & $0.06\sigma$; requires \"Unsal conjecture \\
  3807Ôćĺ$N_f = 4$ unique in ISS window & Derived & Only integer in $(3, 4.5)$ \\
  3808ÔćĺISS vacuum cosmologically stable & Derived & $S_{\text{bounce}} \gg 1$ for $h \sim O(1)$ \\
  3809ÔćĺISS CW does not transmit Koide & Derived & $Q_{\text{CW}} \to 1/3$ (affine map) \\
  3810Ôćĺ$c = -1/12$ from bion K\"ahler & Derived & At $\alpha_s = 0.143$ (ISS) \\
  3811Ôćĺ$\text{STr}[M^2] = 18\,\fpi^2$ & Derived & Soft mass only (canonical K\"ahler) \\
  3812Ôćĺ$m_h = \lambda_S v/\sqrt{2} = 125\GeV$ & Input & NMSSM singlet $S \neq X$; $\lambda_S = 0.72$ \\
  3813Ôćĺ$\cos(9\delta)$ from $(\det M)^3/\Lambda^{18}$ & Derived & $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ charge counting \\
  3814Ôćĺ$\mathcal{Q}_{[ij]} \in \overline{\mathbf{10}}$ of $SU(5)_f$ & Derived & Miyazawa algebra \\
  3815ÔćĺCasimir structural constraints & Assumed & Reverse-engineered \\
  3816ÔćĺNo tachyons: $F_X = 0$ (Lagrange multiplier) & Derived & Block-diagonal obstruction \\
  3817ÔćĺGST relation $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ & Structural & Seesaw encodes Oakes; not derived \\
  3818Ôćĺ$\mu = \lambda_S\langle S\rangle \sim O(v)$ & Free & Standard NMSSM ($S \neq X$) \\
  3819ÔćĺK\"ahler cannot generate $Q_\ell = 2/3$ & Derived & Rank invariance of $W_{IJ}$ \\
  3820ÔćĺFCNC: mesons = SM kaons, not extra d.o.f.\ & Noted & Extra SUSY FCNC TBD \\
  3821Ôćĺ$X \neq S$: $\hat\lambda_{\text{Higgs}}/\hat\lambda_{\text{max}} \sim 10^5$ & Derived & Dimensional analysis \\
  3822ÔćĺO'R--Koide universal (gauge-independent) & Derived & $SU(2)$, $Sp(2)$, $SU(3)$ all give $Q = 2/3$ \\
  3823Ôćĺ$SU(2)$ lepton sector: metastable O'R + Pfaffian & Derived & s-confining, $N_f = 3$ \\
  3824ÔćĺSeesaw vacuum cosmologically stable & Derived & $S_4 \sim 2 \times 10^7 \gg 400$ \\
  3825Ôćĺ$m_B\,B\tilde B$ lifts baryon vacuum, no meson changes & Derived & $m_B \gtrsim 1\;\MeV$ suffices \\
  3826ÔćĺAdiabatic bion flat on Koide manifold & Derived & $\sum s_k|z_k| = 3v_0$ (identity) \\
  3827ÔćĺBloom driven by $\cos(9\delta)$, stabilised by bion & Derived & Physical $\delta$ within $2.8^\circ$ of $\cos(9\delta)$ min \\
  3828Ôćĺ\bottomrule
  3829Ôćĺ\end{tabular}
  3830Ôćĺ\end{center}
  3831Ôćĺ
  3832Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3833Ôćĺ\section{Conclusions}
  3834Ôćĺ\label{sec:conclusions}
  3835Ôćĺ%======================================================================
  3836Ôćĺ
  3837ÔćĺSeven principal results:
  3838Ôćĺ
  3839Ôćĺ\paragraph{1.\ The Koide formula is energy equipartition.}

\documentclass[12pt,a4paper]{article}
\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsfonts}
\usepackage{mathtools}
\usepackage{booktabs}
\usepackage{array}
\usepackage{geometry}
\usepackage{hyperref}
\usepackage{cite}
\geometry{margin=2.5cm}

\newcommand{\MeV}{\,\text{MeV}}
\newcommand{\GeV}{\,\text{GeV}}
\newcommand{\fpi}{f_\pi}
\newcommand{\qqbar}{\langle\bar{q}q\rangle}
\newcommand{\zch}{\mathfrak{z}}

\title{Three families from SUSY confinement: spectrum and mass predictions\\
of an $\mathcal{N}=1$ $\mathrm{SU}(3)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)$ theory}

\author{A.~Rivero\thanks{Universidad de Zaragoza.
Manuscript prepared with AI assistance (Claude/Anthropic).
Continues the program of \cite{Rivero2005,Rivero2012}.}}
\date{March 2026}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We construct an $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetric theory with gauge group
$\mathrm{SU}(3) \times \mathrm{SU}(2)$ and show that it uniquely selects
three colors and three generations through a Diophantine bootstrap.
Requiring that the scalar diquarks and mesons of an $\mathrm{SU}(N)$
confining theory fill supermultiplets imposes three interlocking
constraints --- $rs = 2N$, $r(r+1)/2 = 2N$, $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N$ ---
whose only positive-integer solution is $N=3$, $r=3$, $s=2$. The five
light quarks carry an $\mathrm{SU}(5)$ flavor symmetry under which the
composites fill $\mathbf{24}\oplus\mathbf{15}\oplus\overline{\mathbf{15}}$,
a representation that is anomaly-free and asymptotically free.
The quark sector is described by $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ SQCD at
$N_f = N_c = 3$ in the Intriligator--Seiberg--Shih magnetic dual; the
lepton sector by $s$-confining $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ SQCD with $N_f = 3$.
Both sectors admit an O'Raifeartaigh deformation whose
Coleman--Weinberg-stabilized vacuum at $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$ fixes a
structural mass ratio $(2+\sqrt{3})^2 \approx 13.93$ and enforces an
energy-balance invariant $Q = 2/3$. From the strange quark mass alone,
the Lagrangian predicts $m_c = 1301$ MeV (+2.0\%), $m_b = 4233$ MeV
(+1.3\%), and $m_t = 171\,250$ MeV ($-$0.9\%). The lepton sector predicts
$m_\tau = 1776.97$ MeV (+0.006\%). In total five of the Standard Model's
thirteen charged-fermion-sector parameters are determined, reducing the
free parameter count from thirteen to eight.
\end{abstract}

\tableofcontents

%======================================================================
\section{Introduction}
%======================================================================

Of the roughly two dozen free parameters in the Standard Model, thirteen
belong to the charged fermion sector: nine masses and four CKM mixing
angles. No established principle relates them. The muon is 207 times
heavier than the electron; the top quark outweighs the up quark by a
factor of $8 \times 10^4$. These hierarchies are accommodated by the
Yukawa couplings, but the couplings themselves are inserted by hand. The
flavor problem --- why three generations, and why these masses --- remains
the central open question in particle physics.

This paper proposes a concrete $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetric answer.
The construction begins from the observation that if the scalar partners
of quarks are composite (diquarks and mesons of a confining gauge
theory), then the requirement that they fill common supermultiplets is
not automatically satisfiable. The number of scalar diquarks formed from
$r$ flavors is $r(r+1)/2$; the number of scalar mesons from $r$ flavors
and $s$ antiflavors is $rs$; and these must jointly account for $4N$
bosonic degrees of freedom in a theory with $N$ colors. The resulting
system of Diophantine equations ---
$$rs = 2N, \qquad \frac{r(r+1)}{2} = 2N, \qquad r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N$$
--- admits a unique positive-integer solution: $N=3$, $r=3$, $s=2$. The
number of colors, the number of generations (encoded as $r + s - 1 = 4$,
giving four light quark flavors in addition to the strange), and the
representation content of the composites are all fixed by arithmetic.
This is the bootstrap: supersymmetry and confinement together select the
Standard Model's gauge and family structure.

The five light quarks $(u, d, s, c, b)$ then sit in the fundamental of
an $\mathrm{SU}(5)$ flavor symmetry. Under $\mathrm{SU}(5)_{\rm
flavor} \times \mathrm{SU}(3)_{\rm color}$, the composites --- mesons
in the adjoint-plus-singlet and diquarks in the symmetric tensor ---
fill the representations $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus
\overline{\mathbf{15}}$. This combination is anomaly-free and
asymptotically free with one-loop coefficient $b_0 = 31/3$. The top
quark, with Yukawa coupling $y_t \approx 1$, is external to the
confining dynamics; it does not form bound states.

The confining dynamics of the quark sector is that of $\mathrm{SU}(3)$
SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$ flavors, a theory whose infrared physics was
solved by Seiberg~\cite{Seiberg1995}. In the magnetic dual description, the degrees of
freedom are gauge-invariant mesons $M^i{}_j$, baryons $B$ and
$\tilde{B}$, and a Lagrange-multiplier singlet $X$ that enforces the
quantum-modified constraint $\det M - B\tilde{B} = \Lambda^6$. The
Seiberg seesaw gives meson vacuum expectation values $\langle M^j{}_j
\rangle = C/m_j$, inversely proportional to the quark masses. Adding an
O'Raifeartaigh deformation --- the minimal coupling $W \supset fX + m
\phi_a \phi_b + gX\phi_a^2$ --- breaks supersymmetry at a metastable
vacuum. The Coleman--Weinberg potential for the pseudo-modulus $X$,
combined with a non-canonical K\"ahler correction $\delta K = -|X|^4
/(12\mu^2)$, stabilizes the vacuum expectation value at $g\langle X
\rangle / m = \sqrt{3}$.

This stabilization has a remarkable consequence. The fermion mass matrix
at the O'Raifeartaigh vacuum has eigenvalues $(0,\; m_-,\; m_+)$ with
$m_\pm = (\sqrt{t^2 + 1} \pm t)\,m$ and $t = gv/m$. At $t = \sqrt{3}$
the eigenvalues become $(0,\; (2-\sqrt{3})\,m,\; (2+\sqrt{3})\,m)$,
whose ratio $m_+/m_- = (2+\sqrt{3})^2 \approx 13.93$ is a structural
constant independent of all free parameters. This is the
\emph{O'Raifeartaigh mass ratio}. Applied to the quark seed with
$m = m_s = 93.4$ MeV, it predicts $m_c = 13.93 \times m_s = 1301$ MeV,
to be compared with the PDG value $1275 \pm 25$ MeV (2.0\% deviation,
$1.0\sigma$).

The same vacuum enforces an algebraic identity: the ratio $Q \equiv
\sum m_k / (\sum \sqrt{m_k})^2$ evaluates to exactly $2/3$ for the
eigenvalue triple $(0, m_-, m_+)$ at $t = \sqrt{3}$. This is the
\emph{energy-balance condition} --- it states that the mean squared
perturbation of the mass charges equals the squared vacuum charge, i.e.,
$\langle z_k^2 \rangle = z_0^2$ in the parametrization $m_k =
(z_0 + z_k)^2$ with $\sum z_k = 0$. It is not an empirical observation
extracted from data; it is a theorem about the O'Raifeartaigh
superpotential. The energy-balance condition serves as the organizing
principle for all subsequent mass predictions.

To pass from the charm quark to the bottom quark, a second mechanism is
needed. On $\mathbb{R}^3 \times S^1$, the SU(3) gauge theory admits
magnetic bions --- correlated monopole--antimonopole pairs associated
with the three roots of the extended Dynkin diagram. Their contribution
to the effective K\"ahler potential modifies the mass eigenvalue relation
to $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$, where the integer
coefficient is the number of colors. We call this the \emph{bion mass
relation}. Using the O'Raifeartaigh-predicted $m_c$, it gives
$m_b = 4233$ MeV (PDG: $4180 \pm 30$ MeV, 1.3\% deviation). The
energy-balance invariant is approximately preserved: $Q(-\sqrt{m_s},
\sqrt{m_c}, \sqrt{m_b}) = 0.673$, deviating from $2/3$ by 0.9\%.

The chain extends to the top quark through the \emph{Yukawa eigenvalue
constraint}. At $\tan\beta = 1$, the SUSY Yukawa structure requires the
heavy triple $(m_c, m_b, m_t)$ to satisfy $Q = 2/3$ with all positive
square roots. This reduces to a quadratic equation for $\sqrt{m_t}$
whose physical solution, using the predicted $m_c$ and $m_b$ as inputs,
gives $m_t = 171\,250$ MeV (PDG: $172\,760 \pm 300$ MeV, 0.9\%
deviation). Thus from the single input $m_s$, the theory produces the
masses of all three heavy quarks.

The lepton sector is governed by the same algebraic structure. The
$\mathrm{SU}(2)$ confining theory with $N_f = 3$ is $s$-confining
($N_f = N_c + 1$); its infrared dynamics is captured by three
antisymmetric composites and a singlet, deformed by an O'Raifeartaigh
superpotential stabilized at the same $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$. Because the
SU(2) theory has no magnetic bion mechanism, the physical spectrum stays
close to the O'Raifeartaigh seed. The energy-balance condition
$Q(m_e, m_\mu, m_\tau) = 2/3$ directly predicts $m_\tau = 1776.97$ MeV
from the inputs $m_e$ and $m_\mu$, matching the PDG value $1776.86 \pm
0.12$ MeV to 0.006\%.

Two further predictions follow from the Lagrangian. The identification
of the Seiberg Lagrange multiplier $X$ with the NMSSM singlet $S$
generates a Higgs quartic through the $|F_X|^2$ term. At $\tan\beta =
1$ the tree-level Higgs mass is $m_h = \lambda v/\sqrt{2}$, and
requiring $m_h = 125.35$ GeV fixes $\lambda = 0.72$. The Cabibbo angle
emerges from the tachyonic off-diagonal meson condensate: the ratio of
condensate amplitudes in the $(u,s)$ and $(u,d)$ sectors is exactly
$\sqrt{m_d/m_s}$, reproducing the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation
$\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s} = 0.224$ (PDG: 0.225).

The parameter budget of the theory is as follows. The Standard Model's
charged fermion sector has thirteen free parameters: nine masses and four
CKM angles. In this theory, the O'Raifeartaigh mass ratio determines
$m_c$ from $m_s$; the bion mass relation determines $m_b$ from $m_s$
and $m_c$; the Yukawa eigenvalue constraint determines $m_t$ from $m_c$
and $m_b$; the energy-balance condition determines $m_\tau$ from $m_e$
and $m_\mu$; and the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation determines
$\theta_{12}$ from $m_d$ and $m_s$. Five parameters are eliminated. The
remaining eight free parameters are $m_u$, $m_d$, $m_s$, $m_e$, $m_\mu$,
$\theta_{23}$, $\theta_{13}$, and $\delta_{\rm CP}$.

The paper is organized as follows. Section~\ref{sec:bootstrap} presents
the Diophantine bootstrap and proves the uniqueness of $N=3$, $r=3$,
$s=2$. Section~\ref{sec:diquarks} describes the $\mathrm{SU}(5)$ flavor
structure, the composite representations, and the anomaly cancellation.
Section~\ref{sec:lagrangian} constructs the $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ SQCD
Lagrangian in the Seiberg magnetic dual, derives the O'Raifeartaigh
vacuum, and proves the energy-balance identity $Q = 2/3$.
Section~\ref{sec:predictions} derives the bion mass relation and the
Yukawa eigenvalue constraint, assembling the complete $m_s$ chain that
predicts $m_c$, $m_b$, and $m_t$. Section~\ref{sec:leptons} presents the
lepton sector as $s$-confining SU(2) SQCD with the same O'Raifeartaigh
structure. Section~\ref{sec:electroweak} discusses the NMSSM Higgs
sector, the Cabibbo angle, and the electroweak predictions.
Section~\ref{sec:spectrum} presents the complete particle spectrum and
parameter budget. Section~\ref{sec:open} discusses the open problems ---
the 15 versus $\overline{10}$ Pauli conflict for diquarks, the
nonperturbative origin of the bloom rotation, the mesino mass hierarchy,
and the embedding in $\mathrm{SO}(32)$ --- and Section~\ref{sec:conclusions}
concludes.


%======================================================================
\section{Bootstrap uniqueness}
\label{sec:bootstrap}
%======================================================================

The sBootstrap rests on a single postulate: every scalar of the
Supersymmetric Standard Model is a composite of two quarks (diquark,
color triplet) or of a quark--antiquark pair (meson, color singlet),
with the electric charge given by the sum of the constituents' charges.
We now show that matching the number of such composites to the
fermionic degrees of freedom of $N$ generations uniquely forces
$N = 3$.

\subsection{Setup and Diophantine system}
Let there be $N$ generations, each containing quarks of two types:
\emph{down} (charge $-1/3$) and \emph{up} (charge $+2/3$).  Not
every quark need participate as a string endpoint; we allow
$r$ light down-type quarks and $s$ light up-type quarks that form
composites.  The Standard Model with right-handed neutrinos has
$4N$ real (Weyl) fermionic degrees of freedom per charge sector in
each color channel.  Matching composites to fermions, charge sector
by charge sector, yields the following system.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Charge $+1/3$ composites} (one up $\times$ one down,
analogous to charged sleptons): $rs$ complex scalars must match
$2N$ real fermionic d.o.f., giving
\begin{equation}
rs = 2N\,.
\end{equation}

\item \textbf{Charge $-2/3$ composites} (symmetric down--down pairs,
color sextet): $\binom{r+1}{2} = r(r+1)/2$ complex scalars must
match $2N$ real fermionic d.o.f., giving
\begin{equation}
\frac{r(r+1)}{2} = 2N\,.
\end{equation}

\item \textbf{Neutral composites} (classified by $SU(r+s)$, minus
the overall singlet trace; includes the partner of the right-handed
neutrino): $(r+s)^2 - 1 = r^2 + s^2 + 2rs - 1$ states, of which
$2rs$ are charged and already counted.  The neutral count is therefore
$r^2 + s^2 - 1$, to be matched to $4N$ real d.o.f., giving
\begin{equation}
r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 4N\,.
\end{equation}
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
The alternative neutral equation $r^2 + s^2 - 1 = 2N$ (left-handed
neutrinos only) produces no positive-integer solution when combined
with the first two; thus the sBootstrap \emph{requires}
right-handed neutrinos.

\subsection{The uniqueness theorem}

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Theorem 1} (Generation uniqueness).
\textit{The system has a unique
solution over the positive integers:}
\begin{equation}
\boxed{(N,\,r,\,s) = (3,\,3,\,2)\,.}
\end{equation}

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Proof.}
Equating the right-hand sides of the first two equations
gives $rs = r(r+1)/2$, hence
\begin{equation}
s = \frac{r+1}{2}\,,
\end{equation}
which requires $r$ odd.  Substituting into the third equation:
\[
r^2 + \Bigl(\frac{r+1}{2}\Bigr)^{\!2} - 1 \;=\; 4\,\cdot\,\frac{r\,(r+1)/2}{2}
\;=\; r(r+1)\,.
\]
Expanding the left side,
\[
r^2 + \frac{r^2 + 2r + 1}{4} - 1 = r^2 + r\,,
\]
and clearing the denominator:
\[
4r^2 + r^2 + 2r + 1 - 4 = 4r^2 + 4r
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
r^2 - 2r - 3 = 0\,,
\]
i.e.\ $(r-3)(r+1) = 0$.  Since $r > 0$, we obtain $r = 3$, whence
$s = 2$ and $N = rs/2 = 3$.
\hfill$\square$

\medskip
The solution has $r + s = 5$ light quarks acting as string endpoints,
furnishing the fundamental representation of an $SU(5)$ flavor group.
The top quark is the unique ``elephant''---too heavy to serve as a
constituent of the scalar composites---in precise agreement with the
Standard Model, where $m_t \gg \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$ prevents
top-flavored hadrons from forming.

\subsection{Partial solutions and the hexagonal-number family}
The first two equations alone, without the
neutral constraint, require $2N$ to be a hexagonal number
$h_k = k(2k-1)$:
\[
2N = 1,\; 6,\; 15,\; 28,\; 45,\; 66,\; \ldots
\]
Of these, only $2N = 1$ and $2N = 6$ have $r \leq 16$ (the
asymptotic-freedom bound $2n_f < 33$ limits $r + s \leq 16$).
The first is inadmissible ($N = 1/2$); the second gives $N = 3$.

\subsection{Identification with $SU(5)$ representations}
With $r = 3$ and $s = 2$, the $r + s = 5$ light quarks form the
fundamental $\mathbf{5}$ of $SU(5)_f$.  The composites organize
into three $SU(5)$ representations:

\paragraph{Mesons (quark--antiquark, color singlet):}
The $\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}}$ decomposes as
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}}
= \mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{1}\,.
\end{equation}
The $\mathbf{24}$ carries the sleptons (charged and neutral) and
the meson matrix.  Under
$SU(5) \supset SU(3)_d \times SU(2)_u$:
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{24} = (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1})
+ (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})
+ (\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})
+ (\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{2})
+ (\mathbf{8},\mathbf{1})\,.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Diquarks (quark--quark, color triplet):}
The symmetric product
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{5} \otimes_S \mathbf{5}
= \mathbf{15}
\end{equation}
decomposes as
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{15} = (\mathbf{1},\mathbf{3})
+ (\mathbf{3},\mathbf{2})
+ (\mathbf{6},\mathbf{1})\,,
\end{equation}
and its conjugate $\overline{\mathbf{15}}$ provides the
antidiquarks.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Proposition 1} (Symmetric product is forced).
\textit{The counting equation selects the
symmetric product $\mathbf{15}$, not the antisymmetric
$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$.}

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Proof.}
The charge $-2/3$ composites are down--down pairs.
Under $SU(3)_d$, the symmetric product
$\mathbf{3} \otimes_S \mathbf{3} = \mathbf{6}$ has dimension
$r(r+1)/2 = 6$, matching the counting equation.  The antisymmetric
product $\mathbf{3} \otimes_A \mathbf{3} = \bar{\mathbf{3}}$ has
dimension $r(r-1)/2 = 3 \neq 2N = 6$.  The symmetric color sextet,
and hence the $\mathbf{15}$, is the unique choice consistent
with the sBootstrap counting.
\hfill$\square$

\subsection{Charge census of $\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15}
\oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$}
Treating the $SU(5)$ simultaneously as a Georgi--Glashow
unification group~\cite{Rivero2005}, one assigns standard electric
charges via $Q_{\text{em}} = T_3 - Y/6$ to every component.
Table~\ref{tab:census} displays the resulting charge census for
$\mathbf{24} \oplus \mathbf{15} \oplus \overline{\mathbf{15}}$.

\cmidrule(lr){2-7}

& $\tfrac{4}{3}$ & $2$ & Dim \\
